Unknown.narrative:
CGIAR’s research across its centers advanced climate resilience and sustainable food systems through **systematic assessments of adaptation strategies, mitigation pathways, and biophysical-climate interactions**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s global research** synthesized climate change impacts on food systems, emphasizing **adaptation in vulnerable regions** and **GHG mitigation via agricultural and forestry interventions** (Source: IPCC AR6 Synthesis Report).
- **CIAT and ILRI** analyzed **climate risks in livestock supply chains**, highlighting context-specific adaptation needs in hot, resource-limited environments (Source: *GFS 2020*).
- **Bioversity and CIFOR** quantified **forest biophysical effects on climate**, revealing that deforestation’s net impact varies by latitude (e.g., tropical warming vs. temperate cooling) and underscoring forests’ dual role in **global mitigation and local adaptation** (Source: *Frontiers in Forests and Global Change*).
Unknown.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Change Program** integrated **IPCC findings** to design **cross-sectoral adaptation frameworks**, linking ecosystem health, human well-being, and sustainable development (Source: [https://doi.org/10.59327/ipcc/ar6-9789291691647.001](https://doi.org/10.59327/ipcc/ar6-9789291691647.001) and [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gfs.2016.06.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gfs.2016.06.002) for stakeholder-driven portfolios).

• **ILRI and ICRISAT** evaluated **livestock and crop adaptation** in hot climates, stressing **institutional and technological gaps** (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gfs.2020.100488](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gfs.2020.100488) and [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10668-019-00414-4](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10668-019-00414-4)).

• **CGIAR’s South Asia team** reviewed **smallholder adaptation measures**, identifying **institutional barriers** to scaling climate-resilient practices (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10668-019-00414-4](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10668-019-00414-4)).

• **CIAT and CIMMYT** projected **2050 agricultural yields** under socioeconomic scenarios, showing **price-driven resilience** mitigates climate impacts (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/10/8/085010](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/10/8/085010)).

• **CGIAR’s Food Security Team** assessed **climate variability impacts** on pests/diseases, advocating for **monitoring investments** to inform adaptation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12581](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12581)).
Unknown.mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s Food Systems Program** quantified **food system GHG emissions (9,800–16,900 MtCO₂e)**, prioritizing **agricultural intensification and waste reduction** as mitigation levers (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-environ-020411-130608](https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-environ-020411-130608)).

• **Bioversity and CIFOR** expanded mitigation frameworks beyond **carbon stocks**, demonstrating **forest biophysical effects** (e.g., albedo) can **cool climates locally** while tropical deforestation **accelerates global warming** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/ffgc.2022.756115](https://doi.org/10.3389/ffgc.2022.756115)).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Modeling Team** developed **high-resolution, bias-corrected climate projections** to support **agricultural mitigation assessments** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-019-0343-8](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-019-0343-8)).

Kenya.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, adaptive capacity, and mitigation strategies** in **Kenya** through targeted research and field interventions. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) Program** analyzed climate-smart agriculture (CSA) transformations across East Africa, including Kenya, to enhance resilience and food security (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305](https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305)).
- **CGIAR researchers** modeled climate change impacts on Arabica coffee suitability, projecting shifts in production zones, including Kenya’s potential gains in suitability (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0124155](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0124155)).
Kenya.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS Program** demonstrated climate-smart agriculture (CSA) interventions in Kenya as part of a broader East African initiative, linking transformative food systems to resilience under climate pressure (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305](https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305)).

• **CGIAR researchers** assessed climate change impacts on rainfed cereals (maize, millet, sorghum, wheat) in Kenya, highlighting the potential of cultivar adaptation to offset yield declines, though challenges persist in Eastern and Southern Africa (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137)).

• **CGIAR’s maize research** evaluated biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production in Kenya, identifying yield reductions under medium/high-input intensification and proposing adaptive measures for future climates (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

• **CGIAR’s gender-responsive climate services research** examined gender-based disparities in access to rural climate information in Kenya, advocating for inclusive ICTs and women’s groups to enhance adaptation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216)).

• **CGIAR’s scenario-guided adaptive action** developed participatory scenarios for East Africa, including Kenya, to strengthen strategic planning capacity for food security under climate change (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2014.03.001](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2014.03.001)).

• **CGIAR researchers** explored the suitability of root, tuber, and banana crops under future climates in Central Africa, with implications for Kenya’s adaptive strategies, including shifts to climate-proof varieties (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2021.103246](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2021.103246)).
Kenya.mitigation: • **CGIAR researchers** quantified on-farm carbon stocks in Western Kenya, linking agroforestry to climate change mitigation and livelihood improvements, with timber and firewood as key benefits (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10457-019-00383-7](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10457-019-00383-7)).

Ethiopia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agricultural transformation** in **Ethiopia** through evidence-based adaptation strategies, mitigation assessments, and climate-smart agriculture (CSA) interventions. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) program** analyzed farmer perceptions and adaptation decisions to climate variability, identifying critical determinants like education, gender, and extension services (Source: [Adaptation]).
- **CGIAR researchers** evaluated the potential of cultivar adaptation for rainfed cereals (maize, millet, sorghum, wheat) to offset climate-induced yield declines in East and Southern Africa, including Ethiopia (Source: [Adaptation]).
Ethiopia.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS program** analyzed how smallholder farmers in Ethiopia’s **Nile Basin** perceive climate change and adapt through practices like soil/water conservation, crop diversification, and livestock integration, with key determinants including education, household size, and extension services (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1017/s0021859610000687](https://doi.org/10.1017/s0021859610000687)).

• **CGIAR researchers** in the **Central Rift Valley** documented that **90% of farmers** perceive climate variability, with **85% adopting strategies** like planting date adjustments, agroforestry, and input intensification, while highlighting gaps in institutional support for poorer households (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-017-0100-1](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-017-0100-1)).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** projected **shifts in Coffea arabica suitability** in Ethiopia, finding that highland regions may become more climatically suitable by 2050, contrasting with declines in other global coffee-growing areas (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0124155](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0124155)).

• **CGIAR assessed adaptation and mitigation practices** in smallholder farms, revealing limited adoption of **crop rotations, early sowing, and reduced tillage**, while emphasizing co-benefits like soil conservation for semi-arid climates (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2022.2028597](https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2022.2028597)).

• **CGIAR evaluated climate change impacts on rainfed cereals** in Ethiopia, showing **6% yield declines in East/Southern Africa by 2050**, but also identifying **better-adapted cultivars** that could compensate for losses (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137)).

• **CGIAR analyzed climate variability in Southern Ethiopia**, linking **drought/flood extremes** to trends in rainfall (e.g., decreasing Belg season rains) and temperature increases, and recommended **drought indices** to inform adaptation policies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli9060096](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli9060096)).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS program** reviewed **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) transformations** in Ethiopia, highlighting **soil/water conservation, agroforestry, and improved breeds** as key adaptation measures, with barriers tied to gender, education, and access to climate information (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305](https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305)).

• **CGIAR studied temporary migration responses** to climate variation in Eastern Africa, finding that **urban out-migration declines** during temperature/rainfall extremes, challenging assumptions about migration as a climate adaptation strategy (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.worlddev.2019.104704](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.worlddev.2019.104704)).

• **CGIAR surveyed 385 households in Southern Ethiopia**, revealing that **81.8% of farmers perceive climate change**, with **71.9% reporting higher temperatures** and **53.15% noting rainfall declines**, while adoption of CSA practices was influenced by age, education, and extension access (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2022.e12089](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2022.e12089)).
Ethiopia.mitigation: • **CGIAR assessed greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions** from Ethiopian smallholder farms, identifying **reduced tillage and manure application** as mitigation practices with co-benefits for soil fertility and adaptation, while noting barriers to widespread adoption (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2022.2028597](https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2022.2028597)).
Ethiopia.water:
• **CGIAR evaluated ERA5 reanalysis data** for East Africa, demonstrating **improved precipitation representation** during extreme years, which enhances water resource and agricultural impact studies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos11090996](https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos11090996)).

• **CGIAR analyzed rainfall variability in Southern Ethiopia**, developing **drought indices (SAI, SPI, PCI)** to quantify climate risks and inform water management strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli9060096](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli9060096)).

India.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, mitigation, and adaptive capacity** in **India** through participatory technologies, agroforestry, and conservation agriculture. Specifically:
- **CGIAR** assessed farmer preferences for climate-smart technologies like crop insurance, agro-advisories, and rainwater harvesting (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2016.10.005](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2016.10.005)).
- **CGIAR** quantified agroforestry’s role in carbon sequestration, highlighting India’s role in global biomass carbon increases (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987)).
- **CGIAR** demonstrated conservation agriculture’s dual benefits—reducing GHG emissions by 10–15% while improving water productivity in the Indo-Gangetic Plains (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6)).
India.adaptation:
• **CGIAR** mapped farmer preferences for climate-smart technologies (e.g., weather-based agro-advisories, rainwater harvesting) across India’s rainfall zones, informing localized adaptation planning (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2016.10.005](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2016.10.005)).

• **CGIAR** analyzed trade-offs between agricultural intensification and biodiversity loss, projecting carbon stock increases via productivity gains while warning of cropland expansion risks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1029/2019ef001287](https://doi.org/10.1029/2019ef001287)).

• **CGIAR** evaluated heat-tolerant maize varieties, showing potential to reduce yield losses by **33–93%** under projected climate stress in South Asia (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6)).

• **CGIAR** assessed drought/heat-tolerant pearl millet traits, demonstrating yield gains in arid/semi-arid climates (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2017.06.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2017.06.002)).

• **CGIAR** examined gendered adaptation strategies, revealing intersectional barriers (e.g., caste, wealth) shaping climate resilience in Bihar and Uttarakhand (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s13280-016-0833-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/s13280-016-0833-2)).

• **CGIAR** demonstrated conservation agriculture’s adaptation benefits—reducing canopy temperatures by **1–4°C** and boosting irrigation productivity by **66–100%** in the Indo-Gangetic Plains (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6)).
India.mitigation:
• **CGIAR** quantified agroforestry’s carbon sequestration potential, estimating **45.3 PgC** globally on agricultural land, with trees contributing >75%—highlighting India’s role in biomass carbon increases (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987)).

• **CGIAR** modeled agricultural intensification pathways, showing net carbon stock increases despite biodiversity trade-offs (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1029/2019ef001287](https://doi.org/10.1029/2019ef001287)).

• **CGIAR** identified cost-effective GHG mitigation options in Indian agriculture, emphasizing soil carbon sequestration to reduce calorie losses by **65%** while limiting undernourishment (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aa8c83](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aa8c83)).

• **CGIAR** found conservation agriculture-based rice-wheat systems emit **10–15% less GHGs** than conventional systems in the Indo-Gangetic Plains (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6)).

• **CGIAR** analyzed GHG emission hotspots in Indian agriculture, prioritizing mitigation strategies aligned with national NDCs (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2018.11.225](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2018.11.225)).

Uganda.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture and adaptive resilience** in **Uganda** through **cultivar innovation, policy analysis, and indigenous knowledge integration**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR-CCAFS** assessed climate change impacts on rainfed cereals (maize, millet, sorghum, wheat) and demonstrated that **adapted cultivars** could offset yield losses in East and Southern Africa, including Uganda, under future climate scenarios (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137)).
- **CGIAR-IWMI** improved **precipitation reanalysis** (ERA5) for East Africa, enhancing climate data accuracy to support **agricultural and water resource planning** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos11090996](https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos11090996)).
Uganda.adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** developed a **Climate Risk Vulnerability Assessment** for Uganda, identifying hotspots of food insecurity due to declining climatic suitability for **rice and maize**, while highlighting **high adaptive capacity** from infrastructure and education (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0213641](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0213641) ).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** analyzed **policy implementation gaps** in Uganda’s climate adaptation strategies, revealing **disconnects between national, district, and community levels**, and proposed **multi-level governance reforms** to empower smallholder farmers (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envsci.2017.05.013](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envsci.2017.05.013) ).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** examined **temporary migration patterns** in East Africa, finding that **urban out-migration declines** during climate extremes, challenging assumptions about migration as a safety valve (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.worlddev.2019.104704](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.worlddev.2019.104704) ).

• **CGIAR-Gender & CIAT** assessed **gender integration** in Uganda’s agricultural policies, revealing **budgetary inconsistencies** and **structural inequalities** in gender-responsive implementation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-019-02447-0](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-019-02447-0) ).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** documented **indigenous knowledge (IK) for weather forecasting** in Uganda, demonstrating how **local indicators** improve climate information uptake among smallholder farmers (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-019-02476-9](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-019-02476-9) ).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** explored **scenario-guided adaptive action** for food security, emphasizing **strategic planning capacity** and **multi-actor collaboration** in East Africa (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2014.03.001](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2014.03.001) ).

• **CGIAR-CIMMYT** modeled **climate change impacts on maize** in Uganda, projecting **yield reductions under medium/high-input systems** and proposing **adapted varieties** for resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005) ).
Uganda.water:
• **CGIAR-IWMI** evaluated **ERA5 reanalysis improvements** for precipitation data in East Africa, reducing biases in **rainy-season forecasting** and **extreme-year representation** to support **agricultural water management** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos11090996](https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos11090996) ).

• **CGIAR-CIMMYT** analyzed **climate change impacts on maize production**, including **biophysical water-stress effects** under varying nitrogen levels in Uganda’s maize mega-environments (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005) ).

Tanzania.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and adaptive livelihoods in Tanzania** through evidence-based interventions and policy insights. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) program** assessed climate-smart agriculture (CSA) adoption determinants, revealing synergies between crop diversity, irrigation, and agroforestry while identifying trade-offs (e.g., livestock diversity vs. irrigation) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.00055](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.00055)).
- **CGIAR researchers** demonstrated that **cultivar adaptation** could offset climate-induced yield declines in rainfed cereals (e.g., maize, wheat) by 6% in East and Southern Africa (ESA), though challenges persist in 13–18% of production areas (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137)).
Tanzania.adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** evaluated **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) adoption** in Tanzania, highlighting that **crop diversity, irrigation, and agroforestry** complement each other, while trade-offs exist between livestock diversity and irrigation; female resource control and household assets were key adoption determinants (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.00055](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.00055) and [https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305](https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305) for dissemination pathways).

• **CGIAR’s International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT) and World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF)** quantified **climate change impacts on *Coffea arabica*** in Tanzania’s highlands, showing **nighttime temperature increases** reduce yields by **137 kg/ha per °C**, with projections of **80% yield loss by 2060** without adaptation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agrformet.2015.03.005](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agrformet.2015.03.005)).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** analyzed **gender-responsive climate policies** in Tanzania, revealing **disharmony in gender mainstreaming across governance levels** and **low sub-national budget allocations**, despite increasing policy responsiveness (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-019-02447-0](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-019-02447-0)).

• **CGIAR documented indigenous knowledge (IK) for weather forecasting** in Tanzania, showing farmers integrate **meteorological, biological, and astrological indicators** to inform crop/livestock decisions, with potential for **IK-scientific forecast integration** to improve accuracy (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-019-02476-9](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-019-02476-9)).

• **CGIAR’s Farms of the Future (FotF) approach** facilitated adoption of **improved crop varieties, agroforestry, and weather forecasts** in Lushoto, Tanzania, with farmers diversifying **5–10 CSA practices** per season and learning through **peer networks and extension services** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli5030063](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli5030063)).

• **CGIAR assessed climate-induced migration patterns**, finding **urban out-migration declines** during temperature/rainfall anomalies, challenging assumptions about rural-to-urban migration as a climate adaptation strategy (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.worlddev.2019.104704](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.worlddev.2019.104704)).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS reviewed transformative agriculture frameworks**, synthesizing **24 CSA case studies** (including Tanzania) to propose **action areas** (reroute, de-risk, reduce, realign) for food-system resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305](https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305)).
Tanzania.water: • **CGIAR evaluated ERA5 reanalysis improvements** for precipitation data in East Africa, noting **reduced wet-bias during rainy seasons** and **better spatial representation of extreme-year precipitation**, though gaps remain for high-resolution agricultural/water-resource applications (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos11090996](https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos11090996)).

Ghana.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and adaptive resilience** in **Ghana** through **gender-inclusive climate services, cultivar adaptation, and bundled CSA practices**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) Program** demonstrated transformative CSA initiatives across West Africa, including Ghana, to enhance food systems resilience under climate pressure (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305](https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305)).
- **CGIAR researchers** assessed the potential of **cultivar adaptation** for key rainfed cereals (maize, millet, sorghum, wheat) in Ghana, showing that better-adapted varieties could offset climate-induced yield declines (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137)).
Ghana.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS Program** analyzed **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) adoption** in Ghana, Mali, and Nigeria, finding that **bundling practices** (e.g., climate-smart groundnut varieties, intercropping, organic fertilizers) significantly increased yields and food security (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2023.102697](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2023.102697)).

• **CGIAR researchers** evaluated **weather and climate information services (WCIS)** in Ghana’s Upper West Region, demonstrating that WCIS adoption **boosted water management and multiple cropping practices** by 6.8% and 5.6%, respectively, via mobile platforms like Esoko (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2021.100309](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2021.100309)).

• **CGIAR’s gender and climate team** studied **climate information use in Ghana**, revealing that while both men and women benefited from WCIS for **strategic farm decisions** (e.g., land preparation, crop selection), **gender disparities in mobile access** persisted, requiring tailored dissemination strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-018-2239-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-018-2239-6)).

• **CGIAR’s maize systems research** modeled **climate change impacts on maize yields in Ghana** (2050/2080), identifying **regional yield reductions under medium/high-input intensification** and recommending adapted varieties as mitigation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

• **CGIAR’s West Africa team** reviewed **CSA technologies** (e.g., agroforestry, soil/water conservation like *zai* and contour ridges) as critical for **climate risk management** in Ghana, despite challenges like policy gaps and financing (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199)).

• **CGIAR’s food security experts** highlighted **COVID-19 lockdowns** as exacerbating planting-season disruptions in Ghana, advocating for **policy reforms** to sustain food systems resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41538-020-00073-0](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41538-020-00073-0)).
Ghana.mitigation: • **CGIAR researchers** demonstrated that **climate-smart groundnut varieties and bundled CSA practices** in Ghana **reduced greenhouse gas emissions** while improving yields and food security, aligning with **environmental sustainability goals** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2023.102697](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2023.102697)).
Ghana.water:
• **CGIAR’s CSA research** integrated **water management practices** (e.g., conservation agriculture, agroforestry) into Ghana’s farming systems to **enhance soil moisture retention** and drought resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199)).

• **CGIAR’s WCIS study** in Ghana’s Upper West Region linked **climate information services to water management adoption**, showing that **WCIS significantly increased water-efficient practices** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2021.100309](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2021.100309)).

Bangladesh.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, gender-responsive adaptation, and water-efficient technologies** in **Bangladesh** through interdisciplinary research and policy-relevant frameworks. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** integrated climate risk assessments with agricultural adaptation strategies to address floods, droughts, and heat stress (Source: [https://doi.org/10.4324/9781849776387](https://doi.org/10.4324/9781849776387)).
- **International Rice Research Institute (IRRI)** and **CCAFS** demonstrated **methane and nitrous oxide mitigation** via urea deep placement (UDP) and alternate wetting/drying (AWD) irrigation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jenvman.2022.114520](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jenvman.2022.114520)).
Bangladesh.adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** developed a **comprehensive climate risk framework** for Bangladesh’s agriculture sector, assessing impacts of floods, droughts, and temperature shifts while proposing **policy interventions** for adaptation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.4324/9781849776387](https://doi.org/10.4324/9781849776387) and [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6) for heat-tolerant maize).

• **CGIAR’s Gender and Agriculture team** analyzed **gender-based adaptation strategies**, highlighting disparities in access to climate services and proposing **ICT-based solutions** to empower women in risk management (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216)).

• **CGIAR’s Integrated Survey Platform** (e.g., **Bangladesh Integrated Household Survey**) documented **plot-level adaptation practices**, including crop diversification and farm management modifications, while linking empowerment metrics (WEAI) to resilience outcomes (Sources: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BXSYEL](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BXSYEL) and [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/NXKLZJ](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/NXKLZJ)).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** identified **simple adaptation options** (e.g., adjusted sowing times, irrigation efficiency) to offset climate-induced yield losses in wheat and rice systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3354/cr01212](https://doi.org/10.3354/cr01212)).

• **CGIAR’s Social, Economic & Policy team** explored **crop diversification as a climate risk management tool**, linking women’s empowerment (WEAI) to shifts from cereal monocultures to vegetables/fruits (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-020-02925-w](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-020-02925-w)).

• **CGIAR’s Resilience Program** examined **farmers’ adaptation strategies** in coastal Bangladesh, including savings, off-farm employment, and NGO partnerships, with gender-disaggregated insights (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00267-020-01291-8](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00267-020-01291-8)).
Bangladesh.mitigation:
• **CGIAR-IRRI** quantified **GHG mitigation potential** of **urea deep placement (UDP) and alternate wetting/drying (AWD) irrigation**, reducing methane (CH₄) and nitrous oxide (N₂O) emissions by **6–28%** without yield loss in rice systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jenvman.2022.114520](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jenvman.2022.114520)).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** framed **carbon-friendly agricultural technologies** (e.g., AWD) as viable mitigation strategies, demonstrating **27% lower global warming potential** than continuous flooding (CF) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jenvman.2022.114520](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jenvman.2022.114520)).
Bangladesh.water:
• **CGIAR-IRRI** evaluated **water-saving irrigation regimes** (AWD vs. CF) for **GHG mitigation** and nitrogen use efficiency in rice, with implications for sustainable water management (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jenvman.2022.114520](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jenvman.2022.114520)).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** integrated **hydrological modeling** with agricultural impact assessments to inform **flood/drought-resilient water management** in Bangladesh’s food systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.4324/9781849776387](https://doi.org/10.4324/9781849776387)).

• **CGIAR’s Systems Modeling team** proposed **timely-sown irrigation adaptations** to improve yield in climate-vulnerable regions, with cross-sectoral water-agriculture linkages (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3354/cr01212](https://doi.org/10.3354/cr01212)).

Colombia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA), silvopastoral systems (SPS), and farmer-centric adaptation strategies** in **Colombia** through participatory research, life cycle assessments, and gender-inclusive interventions. Specifically:
- **CIAT (International Center for Tropical Agriculture)** co-designed climate-smart farming systems with local stakeholders to scale up resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00037](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00037)).
- **CCAFS (Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security)** bridged climate science and farmer decision-making via Local Technical Agro-Climatic Committees (LTACs) to improve crop management and reduce losses (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2018.08.001](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2018.08.001)).
Colombia.adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** analyzed disparities in risk perception between farmers and experts in Cauca, Colombia, to inform policy design for climate adaptation, highlighting barriers like lack of climate information (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-018-2320-1](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-018-2320-1)).

• **CIAT** developed a 7-phase methodological framework for co-designing climate-smart farming systems with farmers, NGOs, and scientists in Colombia, focusing on resilience and scalability (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00037](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00037)).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** established **Local Technical Agro-Climatic Committees (LTACs)** in Colombia to provide farmers with localized climate forecasts, improving crop yields and decision-making (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2018.08.001](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2018.08.001)).

• **CGIAR researchers** demonstrated that rural livelihood diversification in the Andean-Amazon foothills reduces household vulnerability to climate change, informing interventions to strengthen adaptive capacity (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000051](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000051)).

• **CGIAR** explored gender-differentiated access to climate-smart interventions in Colombia’s Climate-Smart Villages, revealing intersectional barriers (e.g., education-livelihood diversification) to adoption (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su131910951](https://doi.org/10.3390/su131910951)).
Colombia.mitigation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** proposed the **LCA4CSA framework**, a life cycle assessment tool for evaluating climate-smart agriculture in Colombia, quantifying mitigation potential (e.g., 3% GHG reduction via compost use) and adaptation trade-offs in coffee, cane, and livestock systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2019.02.001](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2019.02.001)).

• **CGIAR researchers** highlighted the role of **silvopastoral systems (SPS)** in Colombia’s Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs), emphasizing their potential to sequester carbon, reduce methane (CH₄) and nitrous oxide (N₂O) emissions, and improve livestock resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2)).

Vietnam.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural adaptation and vulnerability assessment** in **Vietnam** through targeted research, policy-informed interventions, and participatory risk mapping. Specifically:
- The **Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS-SEA) program** developed participatory climate risk mapping (CS-MAP) to identify adaptive interventions for rice production in the Mekong Delta, addressing salinity intrusion, drought, and flooding (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2019.04.004](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2019.04.004)).
- Researchers assessed **climatic suitability declines** for coffee (Arabica/Robusta) and rice/maize, informing sub-national vulnerability hotspots and National Adaptation Plans (NAPs) (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1306-x](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1306-x), [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0124155](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0124155), [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0213641](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0213641)).
Vietnam.adaptation:
• **CCAFS-SEA** collaborated with Vietnam’s Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development to design **participatory climate risk mapping (CS-MAP)** for the Mekong Delta, integrating technical data and local observations to develop regional/provincial adaptation plans for rice resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2019.04.004](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2019.04.004)).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Risk Vulnerability Assessment** identified the **Mekong Delta as a hotspot** for rice/maize suitability decline due to flooding, sea-level rise, and drought, while highlighting relatively high adaptive capacity from infrastructure and education (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0213641](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0213641)).

• **Researchers projected a 50% global reduction in coffee suitability** for Vietnam (and Brazil), with Arabica yields declining under higher temperatures and Robusta facing intra-seasonal variability risks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1306-x](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1306-x)).

• **A global suitability study for Arabica coffee** found Vietnam’s production regions would **lose climatic suitability** by 2050, contrasting with gains in East Africa/Asia (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0124155](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0124155)).

• **CGIAR analyzed barriers to Paris Agreement implementation** in Vietnam’s agriculture, informing NDC design and climate policy bottlenecks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2021.439881](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2021.439881)).

• **Researchers examined gender-responsive climate services**, revealing women’s limited access to ICTs/climate information and suggesting **women’s groups and mixed-gender networks** to improve adaptation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216)).

• **Household surveys in the Mekong Delta** documented **intra-household gender differences** in climate adaptation, with operators adopting farm/household strategies while spouses focused on financial adaptations (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2017-0014](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2017-0014)).

• **CGIAR assessed climate-smart agriculture (CSA) adoption** in rice production, identifying determinants like gender, age, and access to climate information, with combined CSA technologies increasing net rice income (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2019-0003](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2019-0003)).

• **Researchers evaluated adaptation options** (e.g., changing planting dates, supplementary irrigation) to offset climate-induced yield declines in rainfed rice, ensuring food security in the lower Mekong Basin (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z)).
Vietnam.water:
• **CGIAR evaluated water-cycle variability impacts** on rice yields in the lower Mekong Basin, proposing **supplementary irrigation and adjusted planting dates** as adaptation measures to mitigate climate risks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z)).

• **Researchers integrated water-saving techniques** into climate-smart agriculture (CSA) packages for Vietnam’s rice production, with adoption linked to factors like access to climate information and extension services (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2019-0003](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2019-0003)).

United States.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agricultural and water systems** in the **United States** through **adaptation-focused research and modeling**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change and Food Security Program** assessed California’s water supply system adaptability to climate warming, demonstrating its capacity for large-scale groundwater storage adjustments and inter-user water transfers (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-006-9079-5](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-006-9079-5), [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-007-9355-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-007-9355-z)).
- **CGIAR’s Crop Science Centers** (e.g., CIP, CIAT) modeled climate analogs to facilitate cross-regional adaptation dialogs for US specialty crops, enabling actionable insights for climate-vulnerable regions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-023-35887-x](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-023-35887-x)).
- **CGIAR’s Agroecology Program** analyzed climate change impacts on walnut pests in California, projecting increased pest pressure and recommending resilience strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-2486.2010.02227.x](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-2486.2010.02227.x)).
- **CGIAR’s Policy Modeling Team** used the **IMPACT model** to evaluate adaptation strategies (e.g., new crop varieties, irrigation tech) for climate-resilient agriculture in OECD regions, including the US (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
United States.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Water and Climate Program** evaluated California’s water supply system adaptability to climate warming, highlighting large-scale groundwater storage adjustments and inter-user water transfers as key adaptation measures (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-006-9079-5](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-006-9079-5), [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-007-9355-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-007-9355-z)).

• **CGIAR’s Crop Science Centers** developed climate analogs to facilitate cross-regional adaptation dialogs for US specialty crops, enabling actionable insights for climate-vulnerable regions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-023-35887-x](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-023-35887-x)).

• **CGIAR’s Agroecology Program** assessed climate change impacts on walnut pests in California, projecting increased pest pressure and recommending resilience strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-2486.2010.02227.x](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-2486.2010.02227.x)).

• **CGIAR’s Policy Modeling Team** modeled adaptation strategies (e.g., new crop varieties, irrigation technology) to enhance climate resilience in US agriculture (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
United States.water:
• **CGIAR’s Water and Climate Program** analyzed California’s water supply system adaptability to climate warming, emphasizing groundwater storage capacity and inter-user water transfers as critical components of water management (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-006-9079-5](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-006-9079-5), [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-007-9355-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-007-9355-z)).

• **CGIAR’s Agricultural Systems Program** indirectly addressed water use efficiency through Total Factor Productivity (TFP) analysis in US agriculture, linking irrigation inputs to productivity gains (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/IDOCML](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/IDOCML)).

Mexico.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and sustainable production systems** in **Mexico** through targeted research on adaptation, mitigation, and agroecological innovation. Specifically:
- **CIAT** developed vulnerability assessment frameworks for coffee growers to identify adaptive strategies under climate change (Sources: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0088463](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0088463), [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/dvn/29418](https://doi.org/10.7910/dvn/29418)).
- **CIMMYT** evaluated traditional maize seed systems to ensure drought-tolerant genetic material access for farmers (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.1073/pnas.1103373108](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1103373108)).
- **Bioversity** and **CIAT** collaborated on agro-ecological zoning for Arabica coffee to expand its environmental limits (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0140490](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0140490)).
Mexico.adaptation:
• **CIAT** designed an integrated framework to quantify vulnerability of coffee-growing families in Mesoamerica (including Mexico) and proposed participatory adaptation strategies, emphasizing government and institutional support for localized resilience (Sources: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0088463](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0088463), [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/dvn/29418](https://doi.org/10.7910/dvn/29418)).

• **CIMMYT** assessed the vulnerability of traditional maize seed systems to climate-induced shifts in agro-climatic zones, identifying highland communities as most at-risk and recommending improved seed-supply chains (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.1073/pnas.1103373108](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1103373108)).

• **CIAT** and **CIFOR** developed agro-ecological zoning for Arabica coffee to model spatial impacts of climate change, prioritizing research in hot/dry zones and identifying stable locations for long-term adaptation evaluation (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0140490](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0140490)).

• **CGIAR researchers** analyzed climate-related migration in the Central American Dry Corridor (including Mexico) as an adaptation strategy, highlighting the need for policy coherence to address livelihood erosion and security risks (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.1007/s10584-023-03549-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-023-03549-6)).

• **CIAT** evaluated the role of biological corridors in facilitating plant dispersal under climate change in Mesoamerica, identifying high-altitude corridors as critical for biodiversity conservation (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.1002/ece3.672](https://doi.org/10.1002/ece3.672)).
Mexico.mitigation:
• **CIFOR** analyzed forest landscape restoration initiatives in Latin America (including Mexico) as a mitigation strategy, quantifying their potential to store additional aboveground biomass and contribute to the Bonn Challenge (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.3390/f10060510](https://doi.org/10.3390/f10060510)).

• **ILRI** demonstrated the mitigation potential of silvopastoral systems (SPS) in Latin America (including Mexico) for reducing GHG emissions (CH₄, N₂O) and increasing carbon stocks, advocating for their inclusion in Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2)).

Senegal.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and adaptive resilience** in **Senegal** through participatory climate services, socio-economic interventions, and technology adoption. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) Program** demonstrated transformative CSA initiatives across West Africa, including Senegal, to enhance food systems resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305](https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305)).
- **CGIAR researchers** assessed climate risks and adaptation strategies for smallholder farmers in Senegal’s semi-arid zones, highlighting the role of socio-economic factors and institutional support in adoption of climate-smart technologies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fclim.2024.1332196](https://doi.org/10.3389/fclim.2024.1332196)).
Senegal.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS Program** analyzed climate-smart agriculture (CSA) as a strategy to improve food security and resilience in West Africa, including Senegal, by integrating agroforestry, soil/water conservation (e.g., zai, half-moon techniques), and climate information services (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199)).

• **CGIAR researchers** reviewed climate change impacts and adaptation strategies for crop, livestock, and fishery sectors in West Africa, emphasizing policy and institutional mechanisms to enhance resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3)).

• **CGIAR implemented the Participatory Integrated Climate Services for Agriculture (PICSA) approach** in Senegal, enabling farmers to adjust sowing dates, adopt soil/water management practices, and select drought-tolerant crop varieties based on localized climate forecasts (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cliser.2018.07.003](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cliser.2018.07.003)).

• **CGIAR assessed gender-responsive climate services**, identifying barriers to women’s access to ICT-based climate information and recommending inclusive strategies (e.g., women’s groups, tailored ICT tools) to improve adaptation outcomes (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216)).

• **CGIAR examined climate information services (CIS) uptake in Senegal**, documenting challenges in producer-user engagement and recommending private-sector involvement and weather-index insurance to sustain adoption (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli6010013](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli6010013)).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS Program** studied socio-economic factors (e.g., credit access, extension services) influencing smallholder farmers’ adoption of climate-smart technologies in Senegal’s semi-arid zones, linking institutional support to reduced climate risk (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fclim.2024.1332196](https://doi.org/10.3389/fclim.2024.1332196)).
Senegal.water:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS Program** promoted soil and water conservation technologies (e.g., agroforestry, zai, contour ridges) and climate information services to address rainfall variability and drought risks in West Africa, including Senegal (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199)).

• **CGIAR researchers** evaluated drought-tolerant crops, soil/water conservation techniques, and climate information services as key adaptation tools for Senegalese farmers coping with increased aridity (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli6010013](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli6010013)).

Peru.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and sustainable agricultural systems** in **Peru** through targeted research on **adaptation, mitigation, and agroecological practices**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s research centers** identified climate-tolerant cacao genotypes to secure future cultivation under shifting ecogeographical zones (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/ddi.13294](https://doi.org/10.1111/ddi.13294)).
- **CIAT and CIP** analyzed farmer responses to climate shocks, revealing adaptive strategies like water conservation and soil management in maize and potato systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/ajae.12177](https://doi.org/10.1111/ajae.12177)).
Peru.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Bioversity International** modeled future suitability for cacao cultivation in Peru, pinpointing regions where climate-tolerant genotypes may thrive to support adaptation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/ddi.13294](https://doi.org/10.1111/ddi.13294) ).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) program** demonstrated that rural livelihood diversification—including agroecological practices—reduces household vulnerability to climate change in the Andean-Amazon foothills (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000051](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000051)).

• **CGIAR researchers** studied Peruvian farmers’ adaptive behaviors post-climate shocks, showing increased adoption of water conservation and pesticide use in response to droughts and rainfall variability (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/ajae.12177](https://doi.org/10.1111/ajae.12177)).

• **CGIAR’s Agroecology and Resilience team** synthesized evidence that agroecological systems enhance systemic resilience in Latin America, including Peru, by integrating adaptation and mitigation strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/21683565.2023.2273835](https://doi.org/10.1080/21683565.2023.2273835)).

• **CGIAR’s policy research** highlighted gaps in climate-smart agriculture adoption, emphasizing the need for adaptive business models to improve resilience in Peru’s agricultural sectors (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
Peru.mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s Forestry and Landscape Restoration team** contributed to Latin American land restoration initiatives, including Peru, by assessing the climate mitigation potential of forest rehabilitation and plantations under the Bonn Challenge (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/f10060510](https://doi.org/10.3390/f10060510)).

• **CGIAR’s Agroecology program** quantified mitigation benefits of agroecological practices in Peru, though further research is needed to clarify trade-offs between mitigation and resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/21683565.2023.2273835](https://doi.org/10.1080/21683565.2023.2273835)).

• **CGIAR’s policy research** identified forest-based mitigation as a priority in Latin America, including Peru, while noting limited focus on other emitting sectors like energy (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).

• **CGIAR’s land-use change studies** developed a harmonized Amazon-wide agricultural database (1950–2012) to analyze water-carbon-climate feedbacks, with applications for mitigation planning in Peru (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/7J9WVY](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/7J9WVY)).
Peru.water:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS program** analyzed farmer adoption of water conservation measures in response to abnormal rainfall, revealing reduced use after high precipitation but increased adoption during droughts (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/ajae.12177](https://doi.org/10.1111/ajae.12177)).

• **CGIAR’s land-use research** created a spatial-temporal agricultural dataset for Amazonia, enabling studies on water feedbacks linked to land-use changes in Peru (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/7J9WVY](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/7J9WVY)).

Philippines.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers in the Philippines prioritized climate-resilient agriculture and adaptive strategies through transdisciplinary partnerships and field-based innovations. Specifically:
- **CGIAR-CCAFS** developed climate-smart agriculture (CSA) technologies and approaches tailored to the Philippines’ agro-ecological landscapes, enhancing adaptive capacity and reducing greenhouse gas emissions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106136).
- **IRRI** conducted physiological studies on rice varieties to improve chalkiness and head rice yield under varying climate conditions, informing breeding strategies for climate adaptation (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0066218).
Philippines.adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** developed climate-resilient agriculture (CRA) technologies and approaches in collaboration with the Philippine Department of Agriculture (DA) and PCAARRD, enhancing farmers’ adaptive capacity and productivity while reducing GHG emissions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106136).

• **IRRI** analyzed climate factors affecting rice quality (e.g., chalkiness and head rice yield) in field conditions, identifying transpirational cooling as a key factor for adaptation and debunking the assumption that global warming alone reduces rice quality (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0066218).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** published a *Compendium of Climate-Resilient Agriculture Technologies* for the Philippines, grouping CRA options by agro-ecological suitability and outlining dissemination strategies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106136).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** designed an *Eight-Step Guide for Climate-Smart Villages (CSV)*, providing a participatory framework for local implementation of CSA in the Philippines (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107725).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** synthesized lessons from *Climate-Smart Villages* in Southeast Asia, including the Philippines, highlighting scaling pathways via knowledge transfer, policy integration (e.g., the *Adaptation and Mitigation Initiative in Agriculture*), and market-driven approaches (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/115833).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** conducted a *Participatory Vulnerability Analysis* to assess gender dimensions in climate change adaptation, identifying resource gaps and coping mechanisms for Filipino men and women farmers (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/100189).

• **IRRI** simulated future food demand under climate scenarios (RCP 8.5), projecting a 0.24–0.9% decline in rice productivity and modeling risk management strategies for food security (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116413).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** organized regional *workshops on CSA technologies* in Metro Manila, Philippines, fostering collaboration among 13 Asian countries to test and disseminate adaptive practices (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/68386).

• **IRRI** studied physiological trade-offs in dual-adapted rice varieties (flooded vs. aerobic systems), identifying yield reductions and drought tolerance mechanisms to inform water-efficient crop management (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/28202).
Philippines.mitigation: • **CGIAR-CCAFS** integrated mitigation strategies into climate-resilient agriculture (CRA) technologies, explicitly targeting reductions in greenhouse gas emissions from farming activities in the Philippines (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106136).
Philippines.water: • **IRRI** investigated physiological adaptations in rice varieties under aerobic (water-saving) conditions, documenting yield trade-offs and modifications in plant architecture to inform drought-tolerant and water-efficient crop management (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/28202).

Mali.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and adaptive resilience** in **Mali** through multi-stakeholder innovation platforms, participatory climate services, and climate-informed agricultural practices. Specifically:
- **CGIAR** developed **climate-smart groundnut varieties, intercropping systems, and organic fertilizer practices** to enhance yields and food security (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2023.102697](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2023.102697)).
- **CGIAR** implemented **Participatory Integrated Climate Services for Agriculture (PICSA)** to enable farmers to adapt sowing dates, soil/water management, and crop variety selection (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cliser.2018.07.003](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cliser.2018.07.003)).
Mali.adaptation:
• **CGIAR** assessed **climate change impacts on maize, millet, sorghum, and wheat** in Mali, demonstrating that **adapted cultivars** could offset yield losses under future climate scenarios (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137) and [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005) for maize-specific findings).

• **CGIAR** promoted **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) technologies**—including **agroforestry (farmer-managed natural regenerations), zai pits, half-moon contours, and conservation agriculture**—to improve soil/water conservation and climate risk management (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199)).

• **CGIAR** piloted the **Climate-Smart Agriculture Prioritization Framework (CSA-PF)** in Mali’s Sudanese zone, co-developing **two CSA portfolios** (e.g., soil/water management, low-emission practices) with national stakeholders (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2017.02.008](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2017.02.008)).

• **CGIAR** evaluated **gender-responsive climate services**, highlighting barriers (e.g., ICT access, socio-cultural norms) and recommending **women’s group inclusion** and **ICT adaptations** to improve climate information uptake (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216)).

• **CGIAR** reviewed **climate change impacts on crops, livestock, and fisheries** in West Africa, synthesizing **adaptation strategies** (e.g., policy reforms, institutional platforms) to enhance resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3)).

• **CGIAR** demonstrated **PICSA’s effectiveness** in Mali, with **76% of farmers** reporting the approach as ‘very useful’ for **seasonal planning, soil/water management, and crop variety selection** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cliser.2018.07.003](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cliser.2018.07.003)).

• **CGIAR** analyzed **transformative agriculture** in Mali, linking **CSA practices** (e.g., bundling groundnut varieties + intercropping) to **higher yields and food security** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305](https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305)).
Mali.mitigation:
• **CGIAR** researched **climate-smart groundnut varieties and bundling CSA practices** (e.g., intercropping, organic fertilizers) to **reduce greenhouse gas emissions** while increasing productivity (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2023.102697](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2023.102697)).

• **CGIAR** prioritized **low-emission agricultural practices** in Mali’s Sudanese zone through the **CSA Prioritization Framework**, aligning investments with **GHG mitigation and resilience goals** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2017.02.008](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2017.02.008)).

Guatemala.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agricultural adaptation** in **Guatemala** through targeted research on smallholder livelihoods, ecosystem services, and social-ecological strategies. Specifically:
- **CIAT** developed vulnerability assessment frameworks for coffee growers to quantify climate exposure, sensitivity, and adaptive capacity (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0088463).
- **ICRAF** advanced forest landscape restoration initiatives to enhance climate mitigation through aboveground biomass storage (https://doi.org/10.3390/f10060510).
Guatemala.adaptation:
• **CIAT** designed an integrated vulnerability framework for coffee-growing families in Mesoamerica, identifying participatory adaptation strategies and institutional support needs (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0088463).
• **World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF)** mapped climate change adaptive capacity and vulnerability of smallholder livelihoods in Central America, prioritizing municipalities for targeted adaptation interventions (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1792-0).
• **CGIAR researchers** analyzed climate-related migration in the Central American Dry Corridor, linking erosion of rural livelihoods to adaptation strategies and security risks (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-023-03549-6).
• **CIAT** explored gender-differentiated access to climate-smart agricultural interventions, revealing intersectional barriers to adoption among female farmers (https://doi.org/10.3390/su131910951).
• **CGIAR** assessed spatial yield changes in drybeans due to climate change, identifying high-impact regions requiring crop diversification or technological adaptation (https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-015-9696-2).
• **CGIAR** demonstrated grassroots microwatershed management and conservation of local maize varieties to build social-ecological resilience in conflict-affected Western Highlands (https://doi.org/10.5751/es-10250-230305).
• **CGIAR** modeled climate change impacts on smallholder agriculture and ecosystems, emphasizing the need for national/local adaptation responses (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1867-y).
Guatemala.mitigation: • **ICRAF** evaluated forest landscape restoration projects in Latin America, quantifying their potential for climate change mitigation through additional aboveground biomass storage (https://doi.org/10.3390/f10060510).

Nigeria.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and adaptive strategies** in **Nigeria** through evidence-based research on crop adaptation, farmer perceptions, and climate-smart practices. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS)** assessed climate change impacts on rainfed cereals (maize, millet, sorghum, wheat) and the potential of cultivar adaptation to offset yield losses in West Africa, including Nigeria (Source: [10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137)).
- **CGIAR’s International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA)** and partners analyzed drought characteristics, rainfall variability, and farmer adaptation strategies in Nigeria’s agro-climatic zones, linking meteorological data with smallholder perceptions (Source: [10.1016/j.scitotenv.2018.02.196](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2018.02.196)).
Nigeria.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** evaluated the potential of **climate-adapted cultivars** (e.g., maize, millet, sorghum) to compensate for projected yield declines (6% in East/Southern Africa by 2050) under climate change, identifying regional adaptation gaps in West Africa, including Nigeria (Source: [10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137)).

• **CGIAR-IITA** documented **smallholder farmers’ adaptation strategies** in southwestern Nigeria, revealing alignment between local perceptions (e.g., delayed rainfall onset, reduced early-growing-season rainfall) and meteorological trends, while highlighting vulnerabilities in resource-limited contexts (Source: [10.1016/j.wace.2016.12.001](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.wace.2016.12.001)).

• **CGIAR’s West Africa Program** synthesized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) strategies** for crops, livestock, and fisheries, emphasizing policy frameworks to improve resilience in Nigeria’s farming systems (Source: [10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3)).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** analyzed **COVID-19 lockdown impacts** on planting seasons, warning of exacerbated food security risks in Nigeria and advocating for adaptive policies to sustain agricultural productivity (Source: [10.1038/s41538-020-00073-0](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41538-020-00073-0)).

• **CGIAR-IITA** quantified **climate change impacts on maize production** in Nigeria, projecting yield reductions under medium/high-input scenarios and recommending adapted varieties and trade-based mitigation (Source: [10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

• **CGIAR’s Economics of Land and Water Program** examined **household-level adaptation** in Nigeria, showing that climate stress (e.g., harmful degree days) reduces agricultural productivity by **5.22%** and shifts income sources toward livestock, while proposing policy incentives for climate-resilient inputs (Source: [10.1016/j.ecolecon.2023.107892](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2023.107892)).

• **CGIAR-IITA** surveyed **farmer preferences for adaptation techniques**, finding high demand for water- and nutrient-related innovations (e.g., 53% for water management) and linking adoption to income and experience (Source: [10.1007/s10708-017-9771-1](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10708-017-9771-1)).

• **CGIAR’s SLWM Program** modeled **sustainable land and water management (SLWM)** in Nigeria, demonstrating its potential to **offset 20% yield losses** from climate change while increasing profitability (Source: [10.1007/978-3-319-61194-5_19](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-61194-5_19)).
Nigeria.mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** demonstrated that **bundling climate-smart practices** (e.g., groundnut varieties, intercropping, organic fertilizers) in Nigeria enhances yields and food security, with **climate-smart groundnut varieties** showing the strongest mitigation-adaptation co-benefits (Source: [10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2023.102697](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2023.102697)).

• **CGIAR’s SLWM Program** proposed **carbon market integration** and **UNFCCC-aligned policies** to incentivize SLWM adoption in Nigeria, framing mitigation as a tool for sustainable agricultural production (Source: [10.1007/978-3-319-61194-5_19](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-61194-5_19)).
Nigeria.water:
• **CGIAR-IITA** analyzed **rainfall variability and drought characteristics** in Nigeria’s agro-climatic zones, identifying high probabilities of dry spells (p ≥ 0.62) and unreliable rainfall patterns, which inform water-management strategies (Source: [10.1016/j.scitotenv.2018.02.196](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2018.02.196)).

• **CGIAR-IITA** compared **farmers’ perceptions of water scarcity** (e.g., delayed rainfall onset, reduced early-growing-season precipitation) with meteorological data, validating local observations and prioritizing water-adaptive technologies (Source: [10.1016/j.wace.2016.12.001](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.wace.2016.12.001)).

• **CGIAR’s SLWM Program** integrated **water management** into SLWM frameworks, proposing organic/inorganic fertility and irrigation combinations to enhance climate resilience in Nigeria (Source: [10.1007/978-3-319-61194-5_19](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-61194-5_19)).

Malawi.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate adaptation and food system resilience** in **Malawi** through evidence-based interventions targeting smallholder farmers, gender-inclusive climate services, and climate-proof agricultural varieties. Specifically:
- **CGIAR researchers** quantified the economic benefits of climate-resilient seeds, demonstrating potential gains of **$984 million–$2.1 billion (2020–2050)** for smallholder adaptation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-020-02817-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-020-02817-z)).
- **The Center** analyzed migration patterns under climate extremes, revealing urban areas face **10–12% declines in out-migration** due to localized employment shocks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.worlddev.2019.104704](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.worlddev.2019.104704)).
Malawi.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** assessed **temporary migration responses** to temperature/rainfall anomalies, finding urban climate shocks reduce out-migration but worsen local employment—challenging assumptions about migration as a safety valve (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.worlddev.2019.104704](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.worlddev.2019.104704) ).

• **International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center (CIMMYT)** modeled **maize yield reductions** under climate change (2050/2080), identifying **high-input systems and lowland regions** as most vulnerable, while trade could offset food insecurity (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005) ).

• **Bioversity International** identified **‘climate-proof’ root/tuber/banana varieties** (e.g., drought/heat-tolerant cassava) and recommended **spatially explicit planting shifts** to improve suitability under future climates (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2021.103246](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2021.103246) ).

• **CGIAR Gender & Climate Team** reviewed **gender gaps in rural climate services**, highlighting **ICT barriers** and **socio-cultural norms** limiting women’s access to climate info, with recommendations for **women’s groups and inclusive ICT design** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216) ).

• **World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF)** evaluated **climate-resilient seed adoption**, estimating **$984M–$2.1B in benefits (2020–2050)** and advocating for **flexible national seed sectors** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-020-02817-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-020-02817-z) ).

• **CGIAR’s Agricultural Extension Surveys (2016/2018)** mapped **technology adoption** across 3,000+ households, linking extension services to **crop/livestock productivity, food security, and social welfare** (Sources: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/1VSK7V](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/1VSK7V), [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/JCXY6E](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/JCXY6E) ).

• **CGIAR’s Climate-Smart Business Models** proposed **inclusive/adaptive frameworks** to equitably distribute climate-smart value, emphasizing **BoP representation** and **rapid experimentation** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cosust.2019.12.005](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cosust.2019.12.005) ).

• **CGIAR’s Social Sciences Team** linked **farmer perceptions of climate change** to adoption of **grain legume rotations, compost, and manure**, stressing **perception-informed policies** for resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/socsci6010030](https://doi.org/10.3390/socsci6010030) ).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Change Program** projected **macadamia suitability losses (18–21.6%)** by 2050, recommending **area-specific adaptation** to protect income-generating crops (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0257007](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0257007) ).
Malawi.mitigation: • **CGIAR’s Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) research** framed **adaptive business models** as dual-purpose tools for **livelihood resilience and climate mitigation**, though no Malawi-specific GHG quantification was found in this sample.

Zambia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and systemic adaptation** in Zambia through targeted research on crop adaptation, pest/disease management, and sustainable farming practices. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** assessed climate change impacts on maize, millet, sorghum, and wheat, demonstrating that **adapted cultivars** could offset yield losses in Zambia’s Eastern and Southern Africa (ESA) region (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137)).
- **Bioversity International** mapped future habitat suitability for **crop pests and diseases**, projecting increased spread of warm-environment pests in Zambia by the 2030s under RCP scenarios, informing long-term adaptation strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000064](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000064)).
Zambia.adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** evaluated climate change impacts on **rainfed cereals (maize, millet, sorghum, wheat)** in Zambia, identifying **cultivar adaptation** as a critical strategy to mitigate yield declines (e.g., 6% projected loss in ESA by 2050) while highlighting regional adaptation gaps (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137) and [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005) for maize-specific findings).

• **CGIAR’s Africa RISING program** prioritized **maize, pulses, and sorghum** in Zambia for climate-smart interventions, using a **risk-assessment framework** to rank commodities by yield vulnerability and dietary importance (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/96184](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/96184)).

• **World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF) and CIAT** demonstrated that **farmer perceptions of climate change** significantly influence adoption of **sustainable practices** (e.g., grain legume rotations, compost) in Zambia’s Chinyanja Triangle, emphasizing social processes in adaptation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/socsci6010030](https://doi.org/10.3390/socsci6010030)).

• **CGIAR’s systematic mapping** (via CSA Compendium) identified **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) practices** (e.g., agroforestry, carbon-sequestering rotations) in Zambia, though evidence gaps persist for specific commodities (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-92798-5_12](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-92798-5_12)).

• **Bioversity International** projected **expanded suitability zones for malaria vectors** (Anopheles gambiae/arabiensis) in Zambia under climate change, linking findings to **malaria control adaptation** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/1476-072x-13-12](https://doi.org/10.1186/1476-072x-13-12)).
Zambia.mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s SEACRIFOG project** (led by **ICRAF and CIFOR**) engaged Zambian stakeholders to design a **pan-African GHG observation infrastructure**, addressing gaps in carbon cycle monitoring to inform mitigation strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aad66c](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aad66c)).

• **CGIAR’s CSA Compendium** synthesized evidence for **carbon sequestration practices** (e.g., agroforestry) in Zambia, though geographic/topical clustering limits scalability (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-92798-5_12](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-92798-5_12)).

Burkina Faso.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and adaptive farming systems** in **Burkina Faso** through multi-stakeholder innovation platforms, participatory action research, and context-specific technologies. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) Program** co-developed **Climate-Smart Villages (CSV) approaches** to integrate adaptive farming, productivity gains, and GHG reduction strategies through participatory action research (PAR) involving national research systems, NGOs, and local radio programs (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2021.637007](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2021.637007)).
- **CGIAR researchers** assessed **cultivar adaptation potential** for key rainfed cereals (maize, millet, sorghum, wheat) in Burkina Faso, demonstrating that better-adapted varieties could offset climate-induced yield declines by 2050 and 2090 (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137)).
Burkina Faso.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS Program** implemented **Climate-Smart Villages (CSV)** in Burkina Faso, combining adaptive farming, productivity, and GHG reduction through participatory action research (PAR) with national and local stakeholders (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2021.637007](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2021.637007)).

• **CGIAR researchers** reviewed **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) technologies** (e.g., agroforestry, farmer-managed natural regenerations, soil/water conservation like *zai* and contour ridges) as high-priority adaptation strategies for Burkina Faso’s climate variability (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199)).

• **CGIAR assessed climate change impacts on maize production** in Burkina Faso, identifying yield reductions under medium/high-input intensification and proposing adapted varieties as mitigation measures (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

• **CGIAR conducted on-farm trials** in northern Burkina Faso, demonstrating that **intercropping millet (IKMP5/SOSAT-C88) with cowpea (KVX 61-1/396-4-5-2D) + manure/NPK fertilization** significantly improved resilience to erratic rainfall (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0067-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0067-3)).
