• **CGIAR analyzed farmer-driven adaptation** in Burkina Faso, linking **drought-tolerant varieties, mineral fertilization, and market-driven changes** to perceived climate variability (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10113-016-1029-9](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10113-016-1029-9)).

• **CGIAR documented indigenous adaptation strategies** in northern Burkina Faso, identifying **86 ligneous species** used for food, fodder, and medicine, with 11 prioritized species facing high vulnerability (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-017-0090-z](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-017-0090-z)).

• **CGIAR reviewed gender-responsive climate services**, highlighting barriers (e.g., ICT access, socio-cultural norms) limiting women’s use of climate information in Burkina Faso (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216)).

• **CGIAR synthesized climate change impacts and adaptation policies** for West Africa, including Burkina Faso, focusing on **crop, livestock, and fishery sector resilience** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3)).
Burkina Faso.mitigation: • **CGIAR’s CCAFS Program** integrated **GHG reduction strategies** into Climate-Smart Villages (CSV) approaches in Burkina Faso, aligning adaptive farming with mitigation goals (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2021.637007](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2021.637007)).

Nepal.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural adaptation** and **livelihood resilience** in **Nepal** through participatory research, gender-inclusive climate risk assessments, and ecosystem-based adaptation strategies. Specifically:
- CGIAR documented **local adaptation strategies** in the Upper Koshi and Panchase regions, including water harvesting, cropping pattern shifts, and forest management (Sources: [Adaptation, Water], [Adaptation]).
- The Center assessed **heat-tolerant maize varieties** to mitigate yield losses under projected climate stress (Source: [Adaptation]).
- CGIAR’s **TAFSSA initiative** conducted district-level agrifood systems assessments in Surkhet and Banke, linking climate risk responses with gender dynamics and natural resource management (Sources: [Adaptation, Water]).
Nepal.adaptation:
• CGIAR developed **heat-tolerant maize varieties** to reduce yield losses (33–93%) under projected heat stress (2030–2050) in South Asia, including Nepal (https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6).

• The Center identified **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) technologies** (e.g., direct seeded rice, laser land leveling, green manuring) to reduce women farmers’ drudgery and improve productivity in high-climate-risk districts like Rupandehi and Chitwan (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-018-2350-8).

• CGIAR documented **local adaptation strategies** in the Upper Koshi, including water harvesting, altered cropping patterns, and forest management to cope with reduced precipitation and pest outbreaks (https://doi.org/10.1080/21513732.2015.1027793).

• Researchers analyzed **household and community-level adaptation** in Panchase, highlighting livelihood diversification, multipurpose plant species, and collaborative forest/water management plans (https://doi.org/10.3390/environments5030042).

• CGIAR’s **TAFSSA initiative** conducted gender-disaggregated assessments in Surkhet and Banke, linking climate risk responses to agricultural activities, water access, and resilience (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Z0RF6L, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/THVTYH).

• The Center evaluated **hybrid adaptive knowledge systems** in the Asian Highlands, emphasizing state-local partnerships for climate change coping (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1090-7).
Nepal.water:
• CGIAR analyzed **hydrological regime shifts** in the Koshi Basin, projecting increased monsoon/post-monsoon precipitation, high peak flows, and decreased baseflows, informing targeted adaptation strategies (https://doi.org/10.1080/02626667.2014.952639).

• The Center’s **TAFSSA assessments** integrated water resource management with climate risk responses, including gendered access to water in Surkhet and Banke (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Z0RF6L, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/THVTYH).

• Researchers in the Upper Koshi documented **local water harvesting innovations** as adaptive responses to irregular rainfall patterns (https://doi.org/10.1080/21513732.2015.1027793).

Rwanda.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture and adaptive food systems** in **Rwanda** through **resilience-building, participatory climate services, and crop-specific adaptation strategies**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s CCAFS program** analyzed climate-smart agriculture (CSA) frameworks to accelerate food-system transformation in Rwanda, integrating lessons from ground-level projects (e.g., CSA initiatives) to inform action areas like de-risking and realigning agricultural practices (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305](https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305)).
- **CGIAR researchers** developed **scenario-guided adaptive planning** to enhance strategic capacity for climate-resilient food security, engaging multi-actor contexts in East Africa, including Rwanda (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2014.03.001](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2014.03.001)).
Rwanda.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS program** demonstrated how climate-smart agriculture (CSA) can transform food systems in Rwanda by addressing climate vulnerability through participatory, context-specific interventions (e.g., rerouting, de-risking, and realigning agricultural systems) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305](https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305) and [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00021](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00021) for climate services co-production).

• **CGIAR’s climate services research** identified scalable innovations to meet farmers’ needs in Rwanda, including participatory communication processes and localized climate information for decision-making (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00021](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00021)).

• **CGIAR’s scenario-based adaptive action** framework was applied in East Africa to develop long-term strategic planning capacity, ensuring resilience in Rwanda’s food systems through stakeholder-led processes (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2014.03.001](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2014.03.001)).

• **CGIAR’s CIP/IITA collaboration** assessed climate-proof varieties for root, tuber, and banana crops in the Great Lakes Region (including Rwanda), identifying shifts in planting dates and variety types to improve suitability under future climates (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2021.103246](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2021.103246)).

• **CGIAR’s gender-responsive climate services research** highlighted disparities in access to rural climate information for women in Rwanda, recommending ICT-based solutions and inclusion of women’s groups in advisory systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216)).

• **CGIAR’s potato late blight research** (via CIP) found region-specific risks in Rwanda’s Lake Kivu Highlands, emphasizing the need for adaptive planting dates to mitigate disease severity under climate change (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12587](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12587)).

• **CGIAR’s regional climate modeling** (via CIAT/IFPRI) analyzed land cover/land use change (LCLUC) impacts on crop yields in East Africa, including Rwanda, to inform localized food security strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-011-0116-7](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-011-0116-7)).

• **CGIAR’s agricultural productivity research** (via ICRISAT/CGIAR) provided replication data on fertilizer subsidies in Rwanda, linking policy reforms to crop yields and sustainable agriculture outcomes (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SMGIBM](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SMGIBM)).
Rwanda.mitigation: • **CGIAR’s regional climate modeling** examined how elevated greenhouse gases (GHG) and land cover/land use change (LCLUC) influence crop yields in East Africa, including Rwanda, to inform mitigation-informed food security strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-011-0116-7](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-011-0116-7)).
Rwanda.water:
• **CGIAR’s rainfall variability study** (via ICARDA/IITA) analyzed trends in Rwanda’s rainfall (1983–2020), identifying statistically significant increases (11.97 mm/year) to support climate-resilient water resource management and drought/flood risk mitigation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/rs14010096](https://doi.org/10.3390/rs14010096)).

• **CGIAR’s maize systems research** (via CIMMYT) modeled climate change impacts on water-dependent maize production in Rwanda, assessing yield reductions under varying nitrogen levels and mega-environments (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

Honduras.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, adaptive capacity assessments, and water resource mapping** in **Honduras** through **co-design frameworks, yield modeling, and high-resolution climate/soil data**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** developed **climate-smart farming systems** via co-design with local stakeholders to enhance resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00037](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00037)).
- **CIP/CIAT** mapped **climate change adaptive capacity** and vulnerability at the municipal level to inform policy (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1792-0](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1792-0)).
- **CIAT** generated **30-second gridded climate surfaces** for precipitation and temperature to support adaptation planning (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/QET5UQ](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/QET5UQ)).
Honduras.adaptation:
• **CIAT** co-designed **climate-smart farming systems** with farmers, scientists, and NGOs in Honduras (2015–2017) using a 7-phase methodological framework to scale climate-resilient agricultural practices (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00037](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00037)).

• **CIAT/CIP** conducted **municipality-level vulnerability assessments** in Central America (including Honduras) to rank adaptive capacity and identify high-priority regions for intervention, linking crop suitability shifts to livelihood resources (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1792-0](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1792-0)).

• **CIAT** modeled **spatial yield changes for drybeans** under climate change, identifying **high-impact (10–38% yield loss) and adaptation corridors** in Honduras (e.g., Lake Nicaragua to central regions) and recommending crop diversification strategies (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-015-9696-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-015-9696-2), [https://doi.org/10.7910/dvn/heqqih](https://doi.org/10.7910/dvn/heqqih)).

• **CIAT** analyzed **gender-differentiated access** to climate-smart interventions in Honduras, revealing intersectional barriers (e.g., education-livelihood diversification) that hinder equitable adoption of climate information and practices (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su131910951](https://doi.org/10.3390/su131910951)).

• **CIAT** projected **productivity losses** (e.g., 12% for maize, 10% for beans) in Honduran agriculture by 2050, modeling farmer responses (e.g., increased productivity investments) and policy options to mitigate shocks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.133215](https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.133215)).
Honduras.water:
• **CIAT** generated **30-second (1 km²) gridded climate surfaces** for Honduras (1981–2010), including monthly/seasonal precipitation and temperature data, to inform adaptation and policy analysis (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/QET5UQ](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/QET5UQ)).

• **CIAT** produced **digital soil property maps** (30m resolution) for western and central Honduras, mapping **field capacity, wilting point, water holding capacity, and curve numbers** to support small-scale irrigation planning via the **AGRI tool** (Sources: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/QVXA7U](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/QVXA7U), [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/MBHUV4](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/MBHUV4)).

China.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural solutions in China** through interdisciplinary research on mitigation, adaptation, and water management. Specifically:
- **Mitigation:** CGIAR quantified agroforestry’s carbon sequestration potential and assessed GHG mitigation trade-offs in high-population regions like China (Sources: [1](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987), [2](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aa8c83), [7](https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2018.1430018)).
- **Adaptation:** CGIAR modeled climate-resilient crop varieties, analyzed northern paddy rice expansion, and evaluated hybrid knowledge systems for drought-prone regions (Sources: [4](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gfs.2016.08.003), [6](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/abfac0), [8](https://doi.org/10.1108/caer-10-2016-0173), [10](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/10/8/084003)).
- **Water:** CGIAR studied land-cover/climate interactions in SW China and proposed water-saving irrigation to offset drought impacts (Sources: [3](https://doi.org/10.1002/hyp.7233), [6](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/abfac0), [8](https://doi.org/10.1108/caer-10-2016-0173), [10](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/10/8/084003)).
China.adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** modeled drought/heat-tolerant crop varieties, demonstrating yield gains comparable to climate change impacts (Source: [4](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gfs.2016.08.003)).
• **ICRISAT/World Agroforestry Centre** analyzed northern paddy rice expansion in China, linking temperature increases to latitudinal shifts in cultivation (Source: [6](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/abfac0)).
• **CGIAR-Climate** simulated climate change impacts on grain production in China, showing water-saving irrigation and new varieties could offset ~19.6% yield losses (Source: [8](https://doi.org/10.1108/caer-10-2016-0173)).
• **CIAT** highlighted hybrid local-scientific knowledge systems for adaptive capacity in Asian Highlands, including China (Source: [9](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1090-7)).
• **CGIAR-Water** identified water stress as the primary driver of crop failures in NE China, emphasizing irrigation/breeding as critical adaptation tools (Source: [10](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/10/8/084003)).
China.mitigation:
• **World Agroforestry Centre** quantified trees on agricultural land in China, estimating >75% of biomass carbon stored in agroforestry systems (Source: [1](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987)).
• **CGIAR-Climate** assessed GHG mitigation trade-offs in China, showing soil carbon sequestration could reduce food calorie losses by 65% (Source: [2](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aa8c83)).
• **CGIAR** proposed country-specific mitigation targets for China, allocating reductions based on historical emissions and capability (Source: [7](https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2018.1430018)).
China.water:
• **CGIAR-Water** analyzed land-cover/climate interactions in SW China’s Kejie Watershed, showing reforestation offset climate-driven hydrological changes (Source: [3](https://doi.org/10.1002/hyp.7233)).
• **CGIAR-Climate** demonstrated water-saving irrigation could mitigate seasonal drought impacts on China’s grain production (Source: [8](https://doi.org/10.1108/caer-10-2016-0173)).
• **CGIAR-Water** identified irrigation and water-stress-tolerant breeding as key adaptation methods for NE China’s crop failures (Source: [10](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/10/8/084003)).

Myanmar.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA), resilience-building, and carbon sequestration** in Myanmar through transdisciplinary research and policy strategies. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) program** advanced climate-smart rice systems (e.g., Direct Seeded Rice Consortium) to enhance resilience to extreme weather (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035)).
- **The Center** developed Myanmar’s **Climate-Smart Agriculture Strategy** to integrate adaptation, mitigation, and sustainable food systems (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69091](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69091)).
- **Bioversity International** assessed agroforestry’s role in carbon sequestration, highlighting Myanmar’s declining biomass carbon trends (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987)).
Myanmar.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** promoted **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) interventions** in four climate-smart villages, linking diversification (e.g., fruit trees, livestock) to long-term food security and dietary resilience, though short-term food insecurity improvements were limited (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli9110166](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli9110166)).

• **CGIAR and partners** designed the **Direct Seeded Rice Consortium (DSRC)** to address reduced crop yields from variable rainfall and extreme weather, fostering transdisciplinary networks for climate-resilient rice systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035)).

• **CGIAR’s local food systems research** in Htee Pu Village (Central Dry Zone) identified climate adaptation gaps, emphasizing infrastructure (e.g., roads) and multi-stakeholder engagement to mitigate climate impacts on food production (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.indic.2022.100170](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.indic.2022.100170)).

• **The Center** formalized Myanmar’s **national CSA strategy** to align agricultural development with climate adaptation, mitigation, and international climate negotiations (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69091](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69091)).
Myanmar.mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s Bioversity International** quantified **biomass carbon stocks on agricultural land**, revealing Myanmar’s **decline in tree cover (and carbon sequestration) between 2000–2010**, despite global agroforestry gains (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987)).

• **CGIAR’s CSA strategy** included **mitigation co-benefits** by promoting sustainable agriculture practices to reduce GHG emissions while enhancing food security (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69091](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69091)).
Myanmar.water: • **CGIAR’s Myanmar Agricultural Performance Survey (MAPS)** collected **nationally representative weather data** on monsoon patterns, dry-season constraints, and crop marketing to inform climate-informed agricultural planning (Sources: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SPMYR4](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SPMYR4), [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/IWQUU4](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/IWQUU4), [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/IUCGVE](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/IUCGVE)).

Indonesia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, biodiversity conservation, and carbon sequestration** in **Indonesia** through **agroforestry, policy integration, and species-specific adaptation strategies**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Forestry and Climate Change Research** quantified agroforestry’s role in **carbon sequestration**, revealing Indonesia’s agricultural lands stored **>75% of biomass carbon** (2010), with trees contributing to a **4.6% increase (2000–2010)** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987)).
- **CGIAR’s Climate and Land Use Policy Teams** analyzed **barriers to cross-level governance** in mitigation/adaptation, identifying **power imbalances** hindering policy coherence in Indonesia’s land use sector (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2018.10.003](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2018.10.003)).
Indonesia.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Change and Food Security Research** mapped **agro-ecological zones for Arabica coffee**, identifying **up-slope migration (500m)** and **hot/dry zones** requiring adaptation to expand environmental limits (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0140490](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0140490)).

• **CGIAR’s Biodiversity and Ecosystem Services Team** projected **80% habitat loss** for **20 medicinal plant species** in Indonesia by 2080 (RCP8.5), prioritizing **conservation refuges** in Papua, Java, and Sulawesi (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gecco.2021.e01752](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gecco.2021.e01752)).

• **CGIAR’s Coffee Research (CIP/CIAT)** modeled **climate-induced deforestation risks** for Arabica coffee, warning of **suitable land reduction by 2050** while recommending **expansion into deforested areas** to mitigate livelihood impacts (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10113-014-0713-x](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10113-014-0713-x)).

• **CGIAR’s Orangutan Conservation Initiative** identified **refuge habitats (2000–42,000 km²)** for Bornean orangutans, integrating **climate and deforestation models** to guide translocation efforts (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12814](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12814)).

• **CGIAR’s Rice Program** linked **climate-smart agriculture (e.g., Direct Seeded Rice Consortium)** to **resilience against extreme weather**, addressing reduced yields in rice-based systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035)).

• **CGIAR’s Potato Late Blight Research** analyzed **planting date shifts** to reduce climate-induced disease risk, noting **variable regional impacts** (e.g., increased risk in Lake Kivu Highlands vs. decreases in Southeast Asian Highlands) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12587](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12587)).
Indonesia.mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s Agroforestry and Carbon Research** demonstrated that **trees on agricultural land** contributed **>75% of biomass carbon stocks** globally, with Indonesia among the top countries for **carbon sequestration increases (2000–2010)** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987)).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Policy Integration Team** critiqued **bureaucratic barriers** to **mitigation-adaptation-development coherence**, emphasizing the need for **sectoral mandates** to strengthen land-use climate action (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envsci.2016.11.004](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envsci.2016.11.004)).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Modeling** projected **greenhouse gas scenarios (RCP4.5/RCP8.5)** to assess **medicinal plant distribution loss**, highlighting **synergies between mitigation (conservation) and adaptation** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gecco.2021.e01752](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gecco.2021.e01752)).

Niger.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and adaptive resilience** in **Niger** through multi-stakeholder innovation platforms, climate-resilient technologies, and participatory action research. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) program** advanced CSA strategies, including agroforestry (farmer-managed natural regenerations), soil/water conservation (zai pits, contour ridges), and climate information services to enhance adaptation and risk management (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199)).
- **CGIAR researchers** evaluated household-level adaptation strategies (crop diversification, drought-resilient varieties, irrigation practices) and demonstrated their positive impacts on income (+721 FCFA) and food security (+7–9% likelihood) in Niger’s Sahelian regions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su14052847](https://doi.org/10.3390/su14052847)).
Niger.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS and World Agroforestry (ICRAF)** implemented **Climate-Smart Villages (CSV)** in Niger, co-designing participatory action research (PAR) to integrate climate information, agroforestry, and soil/water conservation (e.g., zai pits) at plot, community, and landscape scales (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2021.637007](https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2021.637007)).

• **CGIAR assessed climate change impacts on rainfed cereals** (maize, millet, sorghum, wheat) in Niger, identifying **cultivar adaptation potential** to offset yield declines (6% projected drop by 2050) and highlighting remaining challenges for 33% of potential production (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137)).

• **CGIAR’s International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA) and partners** demonstrated that **zaï pits**—adopted by empowered, drought-perceiving households—**increased cereal yields** in Niger, with counterfactual analysis showing larger benefits for non-adopters (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-017-2096-8](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-017-2096-8)).

• **CGIAR reviewed climate-smart agriculture (CSA) adoption** in West Africa, linking **bundled practices** (e.g., climate-smart groundnut varieties + intercropping) to **higher yields and food security**, with groundnut varieties showing the strongest association (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2023.102697](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2023.102697)).

• **CGIAR analyzed drivers of farming practice changes** in Niger, showing that **drought-tolerant varieties, mineral fertilization, and market opportunities** were key to adapting to erratic rainfall, with technical/financial support accelerating adoption (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10113-016-1029-9](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10113-016-1029-9)).

• **CGIAR’s International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center (CIMMYT)** modeled **maize yield reductions under climate change** in Niger, emphasizing the need for **adapted improved varieties** and input-level-specific adaptation measures (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
Niger.mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS and ICRAF** integrated **GHG mitigation** into CSA strategies, demonstrating that **bundling practices** (e.g., agroforestry, organic fertilizers) reduces emissions while improving resilience and food security in Niger (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2023.102697](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2023.102697)).

• **CGIAR’s modeling framework** linked **land use change (LUC) and GHG concentrations** to regional climate impacts in West Africa, showing that **agricultural expansion** (driven by socioeconomic factors in Niger’s east and climate-induced yield drops in the west) generates **comparable climate signals to GHG-induced changes** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000712](https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000712)).
Niger.water:
• **CGIAR promoted water conservation technologies** as part of CSA, including **zai pits, half-moon contours, and agroforestry** to improve soil moisture retention and reduce runoff in Niger’s rainfed systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199)).

• **CGIAR evaluated irrigation and water conservation practices** (e.g., improved agronomic techniques) as critical adaptation strategies, with **93% of surveyed households** in Niger reporting changes in rainfall patterns necessitating water-efficient interventions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su14052847](https://doi.org/10.3390/su14052847)).

Pakistan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and water management** in **Pakistan** through **science-based interventions** and **adaptation strategies**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** and **International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center (CIMMYT)** developed **heat-tolerant maize varieties** to mitigate yield losses under projected temperature increases (https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6).
- **CGIAR’s research hubs** assessed **climate-smart agricultural practices** (e.g., Direct Seeded Rice Consortium) to enhance resilience in rice-based systems (https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035).
Pakistan.adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS and CIMMYT** developed **heat-tolerant maize varieties** to reduce yield losses (3.3–12.2% under future climate) and shield smallholder farmers from heat stress, with potential yield gains of **36–93%** under rainfed/irrigated conditions (https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6).

• **CGIAR’s InfoCrop-WHEAT modeling** demonstrated that **adjusting sowing times** and optimizing inputs could offset **6–25% wheat yield reductions** projected by 2050–2080, emphasizing **location-specific adaptations** (https://doi.org/10.3354/cr01212).

• **CGIAR’s transdisciplinary networks** (e.g., Direct Seeded Rice Consortium) facilitated **climate-smart agriculture** to improve resilience against extreme weather events in rice systems (https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035).

• **CGIAR’s vulnerability assessments** highlighted **hybrid adaptive knowledge** (local + scientific) as critical for **Pakistan’s arid/semi-arid regions**, where recurring floods (2010, 2022) and droughts threaten **4.4M acres of crops** and **food security** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/138510).

• **CGIAR’s IFPRI-led studies** warned that **climate-induced productivity challenges** will require **risk management adaptations**, though most farmers face **major hurdles** in implementation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/33400).

• **CGIAR’s regional literature reviews** underscored the need for **state support in integrating local knowledge** with policy for **Asian Highlands adaptation**, including Pakistan (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1090-7).
Pakistan.mitigation: • **CGIAR’s climate-smart agriculture strategy** for Pakistan framed **GHG reduction** as a priority, aligning with **climate-vulnerable nations’ needs** despite Pakistan’s low emissions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/138510).
Pakistan.water:
• **CGIAR’s SWAT modeling** (via **International Water Management Institute, IWMI**) assessed **climate change impacts on consumptive water use (CWU)** in Pakistan’s **Lower Chenab Canal (Indus Basin)**, projecting **7–11% increases in annual water consumption** by 2020 under RCP 4.5/8.5 scenarios (https://doi.org/10.2166/nh.2016.102).

• **CGIAR-IWMI** evaluated **water productivity of maize** in Punjab using the **SWAP model**, finding **15–25% declines in potential/actual water productivity (WPET/WPAI)** by mid-century due to temperature rises (2.7–3.2°C), guiding **irrigation management decisions** (https://doi.org/10.3390/su12093905).

• **CGIAR’s PRHPS 2012 survey** (via **International Food Policy Research Institute, IFPRI**) collected **nationally representative data** on **agricultural water use** across Punjab, Sindh, and Khyber Pakhtunkhwa to inform **climate-resilient water allocation** (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/T9GGYA).

Cambodia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, gender-responsive adaptation, and policy coherence** in **Cambodia** through participatory research, early warning systems, and cross-sectoral collaboration. Specifically:
- **CGIAR-CCAFS** advanced **Climate-Smart Villages (CSV)** as participatory testing grounds for context-specific agricultural innovations, scaling CSA via farmer-led knowledge transfer and policy integration (e.g., Cambodia’s market-driven CSA adoption) (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/115833).
- **CGIAR researchers** developed **gender-responsive climate services**, identifying barriers like ICT access and socio-cultural norms to improve women’s inclusion in rural climate information networks (10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216).
- **CGIAR-CCAFS** produced **Khmer-language primers** to equip local officials with GHG-aware agricultural practices, linking climate science to food security (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/100335).
Cambodia.adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** designed **Climate-Smart Villages (CSV)** in Cambodia as participatory platforms for testing and scaling climate-resilient agricultural practices, leveraging farmer networks and policy alignment (e.g., market-driven CSA adoption) (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/115833).

• **CGIAR researchers** analyzed **gender disparities in climate service access**, proposing ICT adaptations and women’s group inclusion to address differential needs in rural risk management (10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** introduced **pest-smart early warning systems** for rice farmers, linking climate data to disease management under shifting weather patterns (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97537).

• **CGIAR** evaluated **adaptation options for rice production** (e.g., adjusted planting dates, supplementary irrigation) to offset climate-induced yield declines in Cambodia’s lower Mekong Basin (10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** promoted **participatory agro-climate advisories**, tailoring forecasts to gender-specific needs for enhanced resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/101922).

• **CGIAR** facilitated **policy coherence analyses** to bridge livestock adaptation-mitigation gaps, ensuring climate-resilient sector growth in Cambodia (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108732).
Cambodia.mitigation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** developed **Khmer-language primers** for local officials to integrate **GHG-aware agricultural practices**, addressing trade-offs between productivity and emissions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/100335).

• **CGIAR** assessed **livestock policy gaps** in Cambodia, recommending reforms to align sector growth with **low-emission resilience** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108732).
Cambodia.water:
• **CGIAR researchers** examined **water management shifts** (e.g., flooded-to-non-flooded cropping) and their impact on **rice disease dynamics** (e.g., sheath blight) under climate variability (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/9SPT9N).

• **CGIAR** analyzed **climate-induced water cycle variability**, projecting region-specific impacts on **rainfed vs. irrigated rice yields** in Cambodia’s Mekong Basin (10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z).

Mozambique.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and socio-economic adaptation** in **Mozambique** through integrated modeling, climate-smart technologies, and community-led adaptation strategies. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) program** developed probabilistic climate impact models to evaluate adaptation options and economic risks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1294-x](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1294-x)).
- **The Center for International Forestry Research (CIFOR) and CIAT** advanced climate-smart agriculture (CSA) by testing drought-tolerant maize varieties and conservation agriculture (CA) practices in central Mozambique (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s1742170515000332](https://doi.org/10.1007/s1742170515000332)).
Mozambique.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS program** assessed climate vulnerability in Mozambique using integrated biophysical-economic models, identifying improved road design and agricultural investments as key ‘no-regret’ adaptation measures, alongside cooperative river basin management (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s11625-010-0118-9](https://doi.org/10.1007/s11625-010-0118-9)).

• **CGIAR researchers** evaluated probabilistic climate shocks on GDP, finding that Mozambique’s economy could shrink by up to 13% by 2050 due to flood risks, while 70% of scenarios projected 0–5% GDP losses, highlighting the need for targeted adaptation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1294-x](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1294-x)).

• **CGIAR’s International Food Policy Research Institute (IFPRI)** implemented the **Innovation for Agribusiness (INOVAGRO II)** program in northern Mozambique, linking smallholder farmers to markets through randomized controlled trials (RCTs) to reduce rural poverty (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/XQ2OMF](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/XQ2OMF)).

• **CGIAR’s CIP and CIAT** demonstrated that **conservation agriculture (CA) and drought-tolerant maize varieties** outyielded conventional methods by 26–46% in central Mozambique, improving food security in drought-prone regions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s1742170515000332](https://doi.org/10.1007/s1742170515000332)).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS and World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF)** mapped climate-smart agriculture (CSA) evidence in Mozambique, identifying gaps in carbon sequestration and resilience data to guide future programming (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-92798-5_12](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-92798-5_12)).

• **CGIAR’s IFPRI** analyzed smallholder farmers’ perceptions in the Chinyanja Triangle, linking climate change awareness to adoption of sustainable practices like grain legume rotations and compost use (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/socsci6010030](https://doi.org/10.3390/socsci6010030)).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** emphasized **community-led adaptation**, identifying participatory vulnerability assessments and locally tailored technologies as critical for mainstreaming climate resilience in agricultural programs (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2014.965654](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2014.965654)).

• **CGIAR’s International Institute of Tropical Agriculture (IITA)** modeled climate-induced shifts in malaria vector suitability, highlighting Mozambique’s vulnerability to expanded transmission zones and the need for adaptive malaria control strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/1476-072x-13-12](https://doi.org/10.1186/1476-072x-13-12)).
Mozambique.mitigation: • **CGIAR’s systematic mapping (CSA Compendium)** evaluated climate-smart agriculture (CSA) practices in Mozambique, including carbon sequestration potential, to inform large-scale investments in resilient farming systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-92798-5_12](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-92798-5_12)).
Mozambique.water:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS and TAMSAT collaborators** analyzed rainfall variability in Mozambique (1983–2020), revealing **declining annual trends (-0.437 mm/year)**, which threaten rain-fed agriculture and require adaptive water resource management (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/rs14010096](https://doi.org/10.3390/rs14010096)).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** integrated hydrologic and crop models to assess climate change impacts on water availability, informing cooperative river basin management strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s11625-010-0118-9](https://doi.org/10.1007/s11625-010-0118-9)).

Egypt.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, gender-inclusive adaptation, and water-resilient systems** in **Egypt** through targeted research and policy-relevant innovations. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) program** analyzed gender disparities in access to rural climate services, proposing ICT-based solutions and women’s group inclusion to address socio-cultural barriers (Source: [Adaptation]).
- **The Center** modeled biophysical yield declines in key crops (e.g., maize, sugar) due to temperature/water stress, advocating for urgent adaptation investments in agriculture (Source: [Adaptation, Mitigation]).
- **Bioversity International** explored CRISPR genome editing to enhance climate-resilient crop traits, targeting stress tolerance mechanisms for yield stability (Source: [Adaptation]).
- **ICARDA and WorldFish** developed nature-based water solutions (e.g., high-resolution climate downscaling) to improve water security amid extreme droughts, integrating local stakeholder insights (Source: [Water, Adaptation]).
Egypt.adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** reviewed gender-based inequalities in rural climate services, recommending ICTs and women’s networks to bridge access gaps and improve risk management (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216)).

• **CGIAR’s climate modeling team** projected **>10% yield declines** in maize/sugar crops by 2050 due to heat/water stress, emphasizing adaptation investments in crop traits, soil fertility, and water management (Source: [https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134318](https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134318)).

• **CGIAR’s Resilience Program** highlighted climate-resilient policies for Egypt’s agriculture, noting sectoral vulnerability to economic slowdowns and food security risks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.2499/9780896294189](https://doi.org/10.2499/9780896294189)).

• **Bioversity International** advanced **CRISPR genome editing** to develop climate-resilient crops, focusing on abiotic stress tolerance for yield stability (Source: [https://doi.org/10.4324/9781003382102-5](https://doi.org/10.4324/9781003382102-5)).

• **CGIAR-WorldFish** assessed tilapia aquaculture performance, providing baseline data for climate-adaptive interventions (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VSQWEE](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VSQWEE)).

• **ICARDA** proposed a ‘suite of technologies’ (e.g., water management, crop protection) to counteract productivity declines in fruits/vegetables, while advocating global cooperation to stabilize food markets (Source: [https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134321](https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134321)).

• **CGIAR’s Al Murunah Project** integrated **nature-based water solutions** (e.g., resilient basins) to enhance climate resilience, using high-resolution climate data for localized interventions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.5337/2025.208](https://doi.org/10.5337/2025.208)).

• **WorldFish** formulated **sustainable local fish feeds** using resilient ingredients (e.g., black soldier fly larvae) to support climate-adaptive aquaculture (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Y1HVFF](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Y1HVFF)).
Egypt.mitigation: • **CGIAR’s climate-economy research** underscored the need for **global GHG mitigation cooperation** to reduce Egypt’s exposure to climate-induced food price volatility and import disruptions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134318](https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134318)).
Egypt.water:
• **ICARDA/Al Murunah** developed **high-resolution climate downscaling** for nature-based water management, addressing droughts/heatwaves through localized, resilient solutions (e.g., Abu Al-Matamir Basin, Egypt) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.5337/2025.208](https://doi.org/10.5337/2025.208)).

• **CGIAR’s adaptation modeling** identified **water management** as critical for mitigating productivity losses in climate-responsive crops (e.g., rice, wheat) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134321](https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134321)).

Ecuador.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, Indigenous knowledge systems, and food security resilience** in **Ecuador** through participatory approaches, agroecological practices, and data-driven impact assessments. Specifically:
- **CIAT-CCAFS** developed **Climate-Smart Food Systems (CSFS) business models** for cocoa, coffee, and silvipastoral systems, integrating mitigation and adaptation practices (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116921).
- **CGIAR researchers** mapped **climate impact gradients on cocoa production** using spatial agroclimatic models (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/LXBZMU).
- **CGIAR’s Indigenous Peoples Biocultural Climate Change Assessment Initiative (IPCCA)** co-produced **resilience workshops** with the Sapara Nation to document traditional ecological knowledge (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/105603).
Ecuador.adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** assessed **climate risks to cocoa suitability** in Ecuador using baseline and future (2040–2080) agroclimatic rasters (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/LXBZMU).
• **CGIAR** implemented **Local Technical Agroclimatic Committees (MTAs)** to foster participatory climate-smart agriculture, including gender and social inclusion (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/114943).
• **CGIAR** documented **Indigenous climate resilience** in Ecuadorian Amazonia through participatory workshops, highlighting cyclical ecological knowledge and adaptive practices (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/105603).
• **CGIAR** surveyed **adoption of improved rice varieties** in Ecuador, analyzing yields and management practices among 1,028 households (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DX3F4T).
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** proposed **methods to evaluate climate impacts on food and nutrition security** in Latin America, including Ecuador (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/80948).
• **CGIAR** facilitated **South:South collaboration on climate risk management**, testing tools like the **Crop Loss Assessment Monitor (CAM)** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97015).
Ecuador.mitigation:
• **CGIAR** analyzed **hydrologic impacts of afforestation/reforestation** under the Clean Development Mechanism (CDM-AR), modeling runoff reductions (80–100%) in Ecuador’s semi-arid tropics (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2008.01.015).
• **CGIAR** evaluated **agroecology’s mitigation potential** in Latin America, including Ecuador, through stakeholder interviews and quantitative assessments (https://doi.org/10.1080/21683565.2023.2273835).
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** developed **Climate-Smart Food Systems (CSFS)** with **emissions measurement, reporting, and verification (MRV)** frameworks for agroforestry and silvipastoral systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116921).
• **CGIAR** identified **research gaps in forest-based mitigation policies**, emphasizing GHG emissions from land-use change (https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831).
Ecuador.water: • **CGIAR** examined **hydrologic impacts of afforestation/reforestation** in Ecuador, predicting **decreased runoff (54–100%)** in drier regions under CDM-AR scenarios (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2008.01.015).

Brazil.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, forestry, and land-use policy integration** in **Brazil** through **science-based mitigation, adaptation strategies, and governance frameworks**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** quantified **agroforestry’s carbon sequestration potential**, revealing Brazil’s role in global biomass carbon storage (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987)).
- **CIAT (International Center for Tropical Agriculture)** mapped **climate suitability for coffee production**, identifying adaptation risks for Brazil’s dominant Arabica regions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1306-x](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1306-x)).
- **World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF)** and **CCAFS** analyzed **multi-level governance barriers** in Brazil’s land-use mitigation and adaptation policies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2018.10.003](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2018.10.003)).
Brazil.adaptation:
• **CIAT** projected **50% global coffee suitability loss** under climate change, with Brazil’s production regions facing severe reductions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1306-x](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1306-x)).
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** modeled **Brazilian Amazon crop yield declines** (e.g., 44% soybean loss) without adaptation, emphasizing critical water and temperature thresholds (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1175/2010ei333.1](https://doi.org/10.1175/2010ei333.1)).
• **CIAT** developed **agro-ecological zoning** for Arabica coffee, identifying upslope migration needs and long-term adaptation sites in Brazil (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0140490](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0140490)).
• **CGIAR’s Forestry Research Program** assessed **tropical pine provenance trials** in Brazil, linking climate dissimilarity to growth performance for climate-resilient forestry (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/f4010155](https://doi.org/10.3390/f4010155)).
• **CCAFS** analyzed **multi-level governance gaps** in Brazil’s land-use adaptation policies, highlighting local-national power imbalances (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2018.10.003](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2018.10.003)).
• **CGIAR’s Livestock Research** demonstrated **silvopastoral systems (SPS)** as versatile adaptation tools in Brazil’s cattle sector (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2)).
Brazil.mitigation:
• **ICRAF** quantified **agroforestry’s carbon sequestration** in Brazil, showing 75%+ tree cover contribution to agricultural land biomass carbon (45.3 PgC globally) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987)).
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** modeled **GHG mitigation trade-offs** in Brazil’s livestock sector, linking pasture intensification to **19–35% lower emissions per kg beef** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2017.06.130](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2017.06.130)).
• **CCAFS** explored **soil carbon sequestration** as a mitigation strategy in Brazil, reducing food calorie loss by **65%** while storing carbon (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aa8c83](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aa8c83)).
• **CGIAR’s Livestock Research** highlighted **silvopastoral systems (SPS)** in Brazil as **NDC-aligned mitigation tools**, reducing GHGs (CH₄, N₂O) while improving carbon stocks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2)).
• **CCAFS** analyzed **cross-level governance barriers** in Brazil’s land-use mitigation policies, emphasizing national-local integration (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2018.10.003](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2018.10.003)).

Cameroon.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate change adaptation and mitigation in Cameroon** through targeted research on cocoa resilience, participatory community-based strategies, and policy-informed agricultural systems. Specifically:
- CGIAR’s **climate-smart agriculture** research identified regionally differentiated adaptation zones for cocoa, emphasizing shade trees and agronomic adjustments to counter temperature and drought stress (Sources: [1][2][3][4]).
- The Center advanced **participatory action research** in Cameroon’s Congo Basin forests to enhance local adaptive capacity, prioritizing climate-resilient livelihood strategies (Source: [5]).
- CGIAR analyzed **policy gaps** in forest-based adaptation, advocating for integration of forests into national climate strategies to bolster resilience (Source: [6]).
- The Center evaluated **climate commitments in Cameroon’s agriculture sector**, highlighting the need for low-carbon, climate-resilient practices to align with national mitigation goals (Source: [7]).
Cameroon.adaptation:
• CGIAR **assessed cocoa vulnerability** in Cameroon’s southern regions, identifying shade trees as critical for reducing temperature-related stress and proposing regional adaptation zones (intensification/diversification/crop change) based on climate projections (Sources: [1][2][3][4]).

• The Center **developed participatory adaptation strategies** in Cameroon’s Congo Basin forests (Nkol-Evodo, Yokadouma), using action research to diagnose climate impacts on agriculture and forestry, then prioritize and implement localized adaptation measures (Source: [5]).

• CGIAR **highlighted policy deficiencies** in Cameroon’s climate adaptation frameworks, emphasizing the need to integrate forests into national strategies to enhance resilience against drought and deforestation risks (Source: [6]).

• The Center **reviewed climate-smart agriculture policies** in West Africa, synthesizing adaptation strategies for crops, livestock, and fisheries—including Cameroon—to improve food security and farmer resilience (Source: [2]).

• CGIAR **analyzed COVID-19’s food security impacts** in Sub-Saharan Africa, recommending policy reforms to sustain planting seasons and reduce hunger risks in Cameroon’s agricultural systems (Source: [3]).
Cameroon.mitigation: • CGIAR **examined Cameroon’s agriculture sector** to identify barriers to low-carbon, climate-resilient practices, proposing technical/material/financial strengthening of systems to reduce emissions from deforestation and align with national mitigation commitments (Source: [7]).

Laos.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and adaptive resilience** in **Laos** through participatory, gender-inclusive, and technology-driven interventions. Specifically:
- **CGIAR-CCAFS** scaled **Climate-Smart Villages (CSV)** in Southeast Asia, including Laos, to test and disseminate context-specific CSA practices, leveraging farmer engagement and policy integration (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/115833](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035)).
- **CGIAR researchers** developed **participatory agro-climate advisory services** to enhance smallholder adaptive capacity, addressing gender-based needs in Laos and neighboring regions (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/101922](https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z)).
Laos.adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** established **Climate-Smart Villages (CSV)** in Laos as testing grounds for CSA technologies, integrating participatory research and policy linkages to scale resilience (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/115833](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035)).

• **CGIAR** advanced **gender-responsive adaptation measures** through Participatory Vulnerability Analysis, identifying resource gaps and coping mechanisms for men and women farmers in Laos and Southeast Asia (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/100189](https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z)).

• **CGIAR** provided **pest-smart interventions and Early Warning Systems** for rice farmers in Laos, addressing climate-induced pest/disease risks (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97537](https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z)).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** developed a **manual for Climate-Smart Village setup**, offering step-by-step guidance for implementing CSA in Laos and neighboring countries (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107725](https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z)).

• **CGIAR** designed a **priority-setting manual for CSA technologies** in Laos, facilitating participatory selection of climate-resilient practices (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107003](https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z)).

• **CGIAR** promoted **participatory agro-climate information services** in Laos, delivering gender-inclusive advisories to enhance adaptation (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/101922](https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z)).

• **CGIAR** explored **trans-disciplinary networks** (e.g., Direct Seeded Rice Consortium) to blur research-development boundaries, fostering climate-resilient rice systems in Laos (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035)).
Laos.mitigation: • **CGIAR-CCAFS** researched **mitigation options for greenhouse gas emissions** from agricultural lands in Southeast Asia, including Laos, alongside adaptation strategies (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107003](https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z)).
Laos.water:
• **CGIAR** analyzed **climate-induced water cycle variability** in the Lower Mekong Basin (including Laos), assessing impacts on rainfed/irrigated rice yields and evaluating adaptation options like supplementary irrigation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z)).

• **CGIAR (ICON Project)** studied **water management shifts** (e.g., flooded-to-non-flooded crops) in Laos, linking disease progress (e.g., sheath blight) to altered irrigation regimes (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/9SPT9N](https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z)).

Zimbabwe.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and adaptive systems** in **Zimbabwe** through **bioeconomic modeling, gender-inclusive climate services, and climate-smart agricultural (CSA) innovations**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s maize research** (CIMMYT) modeled biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize yields, identifying region-specific adaptation strategies (e.g., nitrogen optimization, staggered planting) to mitigate yield losses in Southern Africa (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
- **CGIAR’s gender and climate team** (e.g., CIAT, IFPRI) analyzed barriers to women’s access to climate services, recommending ICT-based solutions and mixed-gender networks to enhance rural climate resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216)).
Zimbabwe.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s maize and climate resilience team** (CIMMYT) quantified yield declines under climate change (up to 32% by 2100) and demonstrated that farm management (e.g., planting dates, fertilizer rates) could only partially offset losses, emphasizing the need for systemic cropping transformations (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13061](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13061) and [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2014.05.004](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2014.05.004) for participatory vulnerability assessments).

• **CGIAR’s water and soil team** (ICRISAT, CIAT) promoted **in-field water harvesting** as a climate adaptation tool, documenting farmer-driven innovations (e.g., labor-efficient technologies) to improve yields in semi-arid regions, despite historical uptake challenges (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/2193-1801-2-100](https://doi.org/10.1186/2193-1801-2-100)).

• **CGIAR’s CSA program** (led by CCAFS) mapped evidence for climate-smart practices (e.g., agroforestry, soil fertility management) in Zimbabwe, highlighting gaps in geographic and commodity-specific research to guide scalable investments (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97083](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97083)).

• **CGIAR’s policy and resilience team** (e.g., WLE) analyzed climate-security risks in Zimbabwe, advocating for **policy coherence** between climate adaptation and peacebuilding to reduce vulnerability among smallholder farmers (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3828/idpr.2023.9](https://doi.org/10.3828/idpr.2023.9)).

• **CGIAR’s business models team** (e.g., CIAT) proposed **inclusive and adaptive climate-smart business models** to equitably distribute resources (e.g., finance, markets) and decision-making power to rural women and men (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cosust.2019.12.005](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cosust.2019.12.005)).
Zimbabwe.mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s CSA research** (CCAFS) systematically evaluated **carbon sequestration potential** of practices like agroforestry and soil fertility management in Zimbabwe, contributing to the evidence base for climate-smart agriculture (CSA) investments (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97083](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97083)).

• **CGIAR’s climate-smart agriculture team** framed mitigation as a core pillar of CSA, emphasizing **GHG reduction** through sustainable farming systems while balancing trade-offs with productivity and adaptation goals (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99040](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99040)).
Zimbabwe.water: • **CGIAR’s water management team** (ICRISAT, CIAT) reviewed **in-field water harvesting technologies** as critical adaptations to rainfall variability, identifying labor-intensive barriers and proposing permanent structures to improve uptake in semi-arid Zimbabwe (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/2193-1801-2-100](https://doi.org/10.1186/2193-1801-2-100)).

South Africa.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, gender-responsive adaptation, and water-resilient systems** in **South Africa** through **bioeconomic modeling, provenance trials, and participatory climate services**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Maize Program** calibrated bioeconomic models to assess climate change impacts on maize yields under varying nitrogen levels and mega-environments (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
- **Bioversity International** analyzed climate analogs to select pine provenances resilient to temperature and precipitation shifts for South African plantations (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/f4010155](https://doi.org/10.3390/f4010155)).
South Africa.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) program** evaluated biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production, identifying yield reductions under medium/high-input intensification and regional disparities in Southern Africa (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

• **CGIAR’s Gender and Climate Team** reviewed gender disparities in access to rural climate services, highlighting barriers like ICT exclusion and socio-cultural norms, and recommended women’s group inclusion and tailored ICTs for equitable adaptation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216)).

• **World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF) and CGIAR partners** documented smallholder farmers’ adaptation strategies in the Free State, categorizing traditional (e.g., crop diversification, livestock shifts) and scientific (e.g., soil conservation) methods, with adoption driven by awareness and financial access (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/agriculture10090410](https://doi.org/10.3390/agriculture10090410)).

• **CGIAR’s Water Security Team** analyzed rainfall trends (1983–2020) in South Africa’s west coast basin, revealing declining trends (-0.360 mm/year) and linking findings to drought/flood risk management for rain-fed agriculture (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/rs14010096](https://doi.org/10.3390/rs14010096)).

• **CGIAR’s Health and Nutrition Program** modeled malaria vector suitability shifts under climate change, identifying South Africa as a high-risk region for absolute changes in *Anopheles* habitats and advocating adaptive malaria control measures (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/1476-072x-13-12](https://doi.org/10.1186/1476-072x-13-12)).

• **CGIAR’s Urban Resilience Team** studied vulnerable women in KwaZulu-Natal’s urban areas, documenting coping strategies (37% adoption) and advocating for gender-sensitive climate policies to enhance adaptive capacity (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1175/wcas-d-20-0180.1](https://doi.org/10.1175/wcas-d-20-0180.1)).

• **CGIAR’s Limpopo Basin Project** conducted household surveys (2005) to assess climate adaptation options, water use, and crop diversification across 5 Water Management Areas, linking findings to policy tools for water security (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/IFCZ7R](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/IFCZ7R)).

• **CGIAR’s IFPRI researchers** highlighted barriers (credit, markets) limiting smallholder adaptation in South Africa, emphasizing pro-poor agricultural technology investments to mitigate food insecurity (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1746-692x.2010.00175.x](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1746-692x.2010.00175.x)).

• **CGIAR’s Oilseed Crops Program** used DSSAT modeling to project climate change impacts on groundnut, soybean, and sunflower yields in Limpopo, identifying region-specific yield declines (soybean) and increases (sunflower) while assessing GHG trade-offs (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0301254](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0301254)).
South Africa.mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s IFPRI researchers** proposed integrating agricultural mitigation activities (e.g., ‘charismatic carbon’) into global carbon markets to fund pro-poor adaptation in South Africa, citing synergies with climate resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1746-692x.2010.00175.x](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1746-692x.2010.00175.x)).

• **CGIAR’s Oilseed Crops Program** developed a GHG calculator (Cool Farm Tool) to estimate emissions from groundnut and sunflower management, recommending targeted mitigation efforts alongside adaptation strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0301254](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0301254)).
South Africa.water:
• **CGIAR’s Water Security Team** analyzed rainfall variability (1983–2020) in South Africa’s west coast basin, providing data for drought/flood risk mitigation in rain-fed agriculture (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/rs14010096](https://doi.org/10.3390/rs14010096)).

• **CGIAR’s Limpopo Basin Project** surveyed crop water use and irrigation patterns across 5 Water Management Areas, generating datasets for adaptive water resource management (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/IFCZ7R](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/IFCZ7R)).

Benin.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and adaptive resilience** in **Benin** through multi-sectoral research and policy-informed interventions. Specifically:
- **CIAT, IWMI, and IFPRI** evaluated **land and water management** practices to enhance smallholder resilience (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV).
- **CIP and IWMI** modeled **hydrological and cropping impacts** under climate change to inform irrigation and sequential cropping strategies (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0192642).
Benin.adaptation:
• **CGIAR (multi-center)** synthesized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) strategies** for Benin’s crop, livestock, and fishery sectors, linking adaptation policies to smallholder resilience (https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3).

• **CIAT and IWMI** assessed **gender-responsive climate services**, identifying barriers (e.g., ICT access) and pathways to improve women’s inclusion in adaptation (https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216).

• **CGIAR (CIAT/IFPRI)** conducted **household surveys** on integrated land-water management, scaling successful climate-adaptive practices across West Africa (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV).

• **CGIAR (CIP)** projected **maize yield declines** under climate change, recommending input-level adjustments and trade-based mitigation (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).

• **CGIAR (local partners)** evaluated **farmers’ perceptions of CSA**, identifying 31 climate-adaptive practices and socio-demographic determinants of adoption (https://doi.org/10.3390/agronomy12061348).

• **CGIAR** developed a **Benin CSA profile** to guide national/international investments in climate-resilient agriculture (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97615).

• **CGIAR** published a **need assessment report** framing agriculture’s transformation as critical for food security, poverty reduction, and climate vulnerability (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/125660).

• **CGIAR (multi-center)** analyzed **COVID-19 lockdown impacts** on planting seasons, proposing policy reforms to sustain food security post-pandemic (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41538-020-00073-0).
Benin.mitigation: • **CGIAR (IWMI/CIAT)** modeled **GHG-induced climate signals** and land-use change interactions, highlighting mitigation potential in West African cropland expansion (https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000712).
Benin.water:
• **CGIAR (CIAT/IWMI)** used **hydrological modeling** to project **irrigation potential loss** (50–95% reduction by mid-century) and **sequential cropping limitations** due to warming in Benin’s watersheds (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0192642).

• **CGIAR (multi-center)** linked **water-use scenarios** to environmental flow requirements, emphasizing planned development for food security (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0192642).

• **CGIAR (IWMI/CIAT)** evaluated **land-water management projects** funded by the Danish government, identifying scalable practices for climate resilience (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV).

El Salvador.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agricultural adaptation** in **El Salvador** through **vulnerability assessments, climate-smart agriculture (CSA), and yield modeling**. Specifically:
- **CIAT/CIP** developed **climate vulnerability frameworks** for smallholder farmers and coffee growers, identifying high-impact adaptation strategies (Sources: [1](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1867-y), [2](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0088463), [3](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1792-0)).
- **CGIAR researchers** mapped **spatial yield risks** for drybeans and coffee, recommending crop diversification and technology transfer (Sources: [4](https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-015-9696-2), [5](https://doi.org/10.7910/dvn/heqqih)).
- **CIAT** advanced **Climate-Smart Coffee (CSC)** to enhance Arabica resilience and reduce GHG emissions (Source: [6](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103773)).
El Salvador.adaptation:
• **CGIAR (CIAT/CIP)** conducted **regional climate impact modeling** to assess smallholder farmer vulnerability, emphasizing ecosystem service dependencies and national/local adaptation responses for crop yield stability (Source: [1](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1867-y)).

• **CGIAR researchers** designed an **integrated vulnerability framework** for coffee growers in Mesoamerica, quantifying exposure (climatic suitability loss) and adaptive capacity via participatory workshops, with El Salvador facing the highest Arabica suitability decline (Sources: [2](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0088463), [7](https://doi.org/10.7910/dvn/29418)).

• **CGIAR** mapped **municipal adaptive capacity** in Central America, identifying drought-prone and subsistence-crop-dependent areas (e.g., El Salvador) as priority zones for innovation support (Source: [3](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1792-0)).

• **CGIAR (DSSAT modeling team)** simulated **spatial yield changes** for drybeans, classifying high-impact climate spots and recommending crop shifts or technology adoption (Sources: [4](https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-015-9696-2), [5](https://doi.org/10.7910/dvn/heqqih)).

• **CGIAR (Bioversity/CIAT)** evaluated **biological corridors** for plant dispersal in fragmented landscapes, highlighting PA connectivity as a biodiversity adaptation measure (Source: [8](https://doi.org/10.1002/ece3.672)).

• **CGIAR** projected **35%+ coffee-growing area loss** in El Salvador by 2050, with policy recommendations for agricultural productivity resilience (Source: [10](https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.133211)).
El Salvador.mitigation:
• **CGIAR (CIAT)** promoted **Climate-Smart Coffee (CSC)** in El Salvador to integrate productivity gains, resilience, and GHG reduction via CSA principles (Source: [6](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103773)).

• **CGIAR researchers** highlighted **forest-based mitigation** and agricultural adaptations as key policy gaps in Latin America, including El Salvador’s coffee sector (Source: [9](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).

Costa Rica.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, mitigation, and adaptation** in **Costa Rica** through high-resolution climate modeling, silvopastoral systems, and policy-integrated resilience strategies. Specifically:
- **CIAT** assessed high-resolution climate projections to evaluate water availability and drought risks under RCP4.5 (Source: [10.1371/journal.pone.0193570](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0193570)).
- **CIAT-CCAFS** advanced silvopastoral systems (SPS) as mitigation tools for GHG reductions and climate adaptation in livestock sectors (Source: [10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2)).
Costa Rica.adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** modeled climate change impacts on food and nutrition security, proposing methods to measure adaptation effects in Central America (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/80948](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/80948)).
• **CGIAR** evaluated biological corridors (BC) as adaptation measures to facilitate plant dispersal in fragmented Mesoamerican landscapes, identifying high-altitude corridors as critical for biodiversity resilience (Source: [10.1002/ece3.672](https://doi.org/10.1002/ece3.672)).
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** developed a Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) framework, scaling resilient practices via policy, institutional, and financing routes—highlighting Costa Rica’s national CSA strategy as a regional leader (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111244](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111244)).
• **CGIAR** analyzed adaptation strategies (e.g., new crop varieties, irrigation tech) to mitigate climate risks in agriculture using the IMPACT model (Source: [10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
• **CIAT-CCAFS** identified high-impact Climate-Smart Food Systems (CSFS) business models, including agroforestry and regenerative livestock, to enhance productivity and resilience (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116921](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116921)).
Costa Rica.mitigation:
• **CGIAR** researched mitigation potential of silvopastoral systems (SPS) to reduce GHG emissions (CH₄, N₂O) and increase carbon stocks, aligning with Latin American NDCs (Source: [10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2)).
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** explored Nationally Appropriate Mitigation Actions (NAMAs) in livestock sectors, emphasizing GHG reduction via sector transformation in Costa Rica and Colombia (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/81300](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/81300)).
• **CGIAR** developed a framework for Climate-Smart Food Systems (CSFS), focusing on emissions measurement (MRV), carbon markets, and scalable mitigation practices (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116921](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116921)).
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** highlighted Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) as a solution to reduce agriculture’s greenhouse gas burden while improving productivity (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/77226](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/77226)).
Costa Rica.water: • **CGIAR** downscaled climate projections to assess future water availability, projecting reduced precipitation and increased evapotranspiration, leading to decreased water excess and dry spells in Central America (Source: [10.1371/journal.pone.0193570](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0193570)).

Congo.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and food systems** in the **Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC)** through **adaptation strategies, participatory research, and policy-relevant insights**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR researchers** assessed **climate change impacts on maize production** and identified region-specific adaptation measures (e.g., improved varieties, input optimization) to mitigate yield reductions in Western and Southern Africa (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
- **CIAT (International Center for Tropical Agriculture)** evaluated **forage adaptation** in Sud-Kivu, testing improved legumes and grasses for erosion control and mixed crop-livestock systems under land scarcity (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/HBO4EC](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/HBO4EC)).
Congo.adaptation:
• **CGIAR** analyzed **COVID-19 lockdown impacts on food security** in Sub-Saharan Africa, emphasizing policy reforms to sustain agricultural planting seasons and reduce hunger (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41538-020-00073-0](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41538-020-00073-0)).

• **CGIAR researchers** mapped **climate risk hotspots** in SADC (including DRC) using IPCC frameworks, identifying vulnerabilities to extreme rainfall and drought hazards by 2050 (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/109192](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/109192)).

• **CGIAR’s EcoCrop modeling team** (likely **Bioversity or CIP**) identified **climate-proof varieties** (e.g., drought-tolerant cassava, heat-tolerant bananas) for Central Africa’s Great Lakes Region, providing spatially explicit adaptation recommendations (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2021.103246](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2021.103246)).

• **CGIAR** conducted **participatory action research** in Bukavu (DRC), documenting local adaptation strategies (e.g., erosion control, crop diversification) while highlighting barriers like land tenure insecurity (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1177/1070496514536395](https://doi.org/10.1177/1070496514536395)).

• **CGIAR’s CR4D initiative** strengthened **climate information services** in Eastern/Southern Africa through capacity-building workshops, co-producing seasonal forecasts for agriculture and food security (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116941](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116941)).

• **CGIAR** explored **gender-inclusive REDD+ strategies** in the Congo Basin, advocating for women’s participation in forest management and carbon benefit distribution (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/20865](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/20865)).
Congo.mitigation: • **CGIAR** investigated **gender disparities in REDD+ implementation** in the Congo Basin, recommending inclusive policies to address forest degradation and emissions reductions (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/20865](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/20865)).

Sri Lanka.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and adaptive strategies** in **Sri Lanka** through transdisciplinary research, policy integration, and farmer-centric innovations. Specifically:
- **CGIAR** developed **heat-tolerant maize varieties** to mitigate yield losses under projected temperature increases (https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6).
- **Bioversity International** mapped **crop wild relatives (CWR) vulnerability** to climate change, identifying conservation priorities for agricultural biodiversity (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crsust.2021.100092).
- **CGIAR** bundled **climate-smart solutions** (weather index insurance, SMS advisories, and resilient seeds) to enhance smallholder resilience to droughts (https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2023.1145147).
Sri Lanka.adaptation:
