• **CGIAR** assessed **heat stress impacts on maize** and demonstrated that heat-tolerant varieties could reduce yield losses by **36–93%** under future climate scenarios (https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6).

• **Bioversity International** analyzed **vulnerability of crop wild relatives (CWR)** to climate change, revealing **habitat loss by 2050** and prioritizing conservation zones for genetic diversity (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crsust.2021.100092).

• **CGIAR** promoted **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) bundles**—including **weather index insurance, agronomic advisories, and resilient seeds**—to protect smallholders from droughts and financial risks (https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2023.1145147).

• **CGIAR** evaluated **farmers’ perceptions of climate/land-use pressures** in Sri Lanka’s dry zone, linking **rainfall variability, deforestation, and wildlife damage** to food security threats (https://doi.org/10.3390/su16198603).

• **IWMI (CGIAR)** influenced **Sri Lanka’s National Climate Change Adaptation Strategy (2011–2016)** through **sector vulnerability profiles** and climate station initiatives (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/67169).

• **CGIAR** facilitated **South-South exchanges on CSA policies**, convening stakeholders to scale **climate-smart agriculture** through institutional partnerships (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/41813).

• **IWMI (CGIAR)** integrated **climate-resilient activities** into national food security programs, aligning research with **UNDP rural infrastructure initiatives** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/32778).

• **CGIAR** documented **scalable CSA options** (e.g., climate information services, insurance) at the **Asia workshop**, emphasizing upscaling methods for banks and development partners (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/90537).
Sri Lanka.mitigation: • **CGIAR** explored **institutional policies for scaling climate-smart agriculture (CSA)**, focusing on **risk management and greenhouse gas mitigation** through South-South collaborations (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/41813).
Sri Lanka.water:
• **IWMI (CGIAR)** analyzed **climate change impacts on water resources and agriculture**, shaping **national policies** and **household food security programs** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/32778).

• **CGIAR** tested **bundled climate-smart solutions** to address **water-related disasters**, including **satellite-based weather index insurance** for drought resilience (https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2023.1145147).

Morocco.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agricultural adaptation** in **Morocco** through targeted research on ecosystem conservation, climate-smart investment frameworks, and transformative governance. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** assessed climate threats to **Argan tree ecosystems** in southern Morocco, projecting declines in suitable habitats due to rising temperatures and reduced rainfall, while advocating for proactive adaptation measures (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s40808-024-02077-1](https://doi.org/10.1007/s40808-024-02077-1)).
- **CCAFS and partners** validated climate-smart adaptation solutions for **pastoral systems in eastern Morocco**, including water-efficient horticulture and diversified income strategies to counter drought and desertification risks (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/65723](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/65723)).
Morocco.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** modeled **climate-induced shifts in Argan tree distribution** in Morocco’s Ait Souab-Ait Mansour region, identifying a **47–81% reduction in suitable habitats by 2080** under high-emission scenarios, and recommended **conservation and adaptive management** to sustain productivity (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s40808-024-02077-1](https://doi.org/10.1007/s40808-024-02077-1)).

• **CCAFS and partners** engaged **youth in climate-smart agriculture (CSA)** through regional forums (e.g., COP22, Marrakech), addressing barriers to youth participation in sustainable agribusiness and aligning with **SDGs** (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/92979](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/92979)).

• **CGIAR validated climate adaptation measures** for **Moroccan terfass systems**, emphasizing **livestock management, soil regeneration, and income diversification** to mitigate drought and overgrazing risks (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/65723](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/65723)).

• **CGIAR developed indicators for Climate-Smart Investment Plans (CSAIPs)** in Morocco, focusing on **conservation agriculture metrics** to track adaptation progress (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/KO3LT9](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/KO3LT9)).

• **CGIAR launched the Adaptation of African Agriculture (AAA) Initiative** at COP22 (Marrakech), framing **CSAIPs** to address **higher temperatures, uncertain precipitation, and extreme events** in Moroccan agriculture (Sources: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106262](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106262), [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106259](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106259)).

• **CGIAR designed a multiscale governance framework** for **transformative adaptation** in Morocco’s **Oum Er-Rbia River Basin**, linking climate impacts to rural welfare and polycentric policy structures (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/137386](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/137386)).
Morocco.mitigation:
• **CGIAR facilitated international collaboration** on **greenhouse gas research** through CLIFF-GRADS, featuring Moroccan scientists (e.g., **Mohammed VI Polytechnic University**) in global agricultural mitigation networks (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110095](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110095)).

• **CGIAR’s CSAIP frameworks** integrated **mitigation components** (e.g., reduced GHG emissions) alongside adaptation, as demonstrated in Morocco’s **AAA Initiative** post-COP22 (Sources: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106262](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106262), [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106259](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106259)).
Morocco.water: • **CGIAR promoted water-efficient horticulture systems** as a climate adaptation strategy for **Moroccan terfass regions**, addressing drought and desertification (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/65723](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/65723)).

Argentina.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural systems and genetic resource conservation** in **Argentina** through **silvopastoral systems, crop wild relative assessments, and mitigation-focused livestock research**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** advanced **silvopastoral systems (SPS)** as a dual-purpose solution for **GHG reduction and climate adaptation** in Latin America, including Argentina, by demonstrating their potential to meet **Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs)** and improve livestock productivity (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2)).
- **CIAT** and **Bioversity** mapped **genetic diversity hotspots** of crop wild relatives (e.g., sweetpotato) across Argentina, identifying **climate-vulnerable regions** and prioritizing **ex situ conservation** to safeguard adaptive traits for future breeding (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/FLXJKU](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/FLXJKU)).
- **CGIAR researchers** quantified **nitrous oxide (N₂O) emissions** from urine patches in Argentine pastures, contributing data to **GHG mitigation strategies** in livestock systems (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BM0ILQ](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BM0ILQ)).
Argentina.adaptation:
• **CIAT** demonstrated the **versatility of silvopastoral systems (SPS)** in Argentina as adaptive strategies for **climate-resilient livestock production**, aligning with national NDCs and improving **animal welfare and productivity** under varying conditions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2)).

• **Bioversity** identified **genetic diversity hotspots** in Argentina’s Patagonian and steppe ecoregions, predicting **40–45% loss of suitable habitat** for key plant species under climate change, while proposing **conservation refuges** to preserve adaptive genetic resources (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10531-023-02770-0](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10531-023-02770-0)).

• **CIAT** prioritized **drought-tolerant bean varieties** for Argentina’s semiarid regions through the **VEF(IBN)-EP-IBYAN scheme**, distributing regionally adapted genetic material to national programs (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/S7LJBI](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/S7LJBI)).

• **CGIAR** scaled **Climate-Smart Food Systems (CSFS)** in Argentina, including **regenerative livestock and agroforestry models**, while addressing barriers like **MRV of emissions** and **access to carbon markets** (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116921](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116921)).
Argentina.mitigation:
• **CGIAR researchers** assessed **tree cover on agricultural land** in Argentina, revealing a **decline in biomass carbon stocks** (2000–2010) despite global increases, highlighting gaps in **national carbon accounting** for agroforestry systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987)).

• **CIAT** framed **silvopastoral systems (SPS)** as a **mitigation tool** in Argentina, showing potential to **reduce GHG emissions (CH₄, N₂O)** while increasing **carbon stocks** in livestock sectors (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2)).

• **CGIAR** measured **nitrous oxide (N₂O) fluxes** from urine patches in Argentine pastures, providing **emission factors** to inform **livestock GHG mitigation policies** (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BM0ILQ](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BM0ILQ)).

• **CGIAR scholars** (CLIFF-GRADS) investigated **livestock system emissions** in Argentina, contributing to **data-driven mitigation strategies** in the sector (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107928](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107928)).

Thailand.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, resilience, and policy integration** in **Thailand** through transdisciplinary research, adaptation strategies, and mitigation frameworks. Specifically:
- The Center facilitated **Climate-Smart Village (CSV) models** to enhance food security and adaptive capacity (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107725).
- Researchers evaluated **climate change impacts on rice production** and proposed adaptation measures like adjusted planting dates and supplementary irrigation (https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z).
Thailand.adaptation:
• CGIAR led the **Direct Seeded Rice Consortium (DSRC)** in Southeast Asia to improve resilience to extreme weather events and reduce yield losses (https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035).
• The Center analyzed **rainfall distribution shifts in Northeast Thailand** and assessed cropping system adaptability, highlighting local resilience (https://doi.org/10.1051/cagri/2017006).
• Researchers documented **soil fertility and erosion control (SFEC) practices** in cassava systems, studying adoption barriers and productivity impacts (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/W5V2TC).
• CGIAR promoted **scaling agricultural innovations** through conferences and policy dialogues, emphasizing sustainable change at scale (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99099).
• The Center contributed to **climate-smart agriculture frameworks** for smallholder farmers, integrating mitigation and adaptation strategies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107003).
• CGIAR supported **supply-chain resilience** via climate-yield models and the Climate Resilience Platform (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/152422).
Thailand.mitigation:
• The Center mapped **national climate change policies and GHG emission reduction targets** in Thailand, aligning with Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/174526).
• Researchers explored **mitigation options for greenhouse gas emissions** from agricultural lands in Southeast Asia, including Thailand (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107003).
Thailand.water:
• CGIAR assessed **climate-driven variability in water cycles** and its impact on rainfed/irrigated rice yields, proposing adaptation strategies (https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z).
• The Center examined **rainfall patterns and irrigation needs** in Northeast Thailand, linking water management to adaptive cropping systems (https://doi.org/10.1051/cagri/2017006).

Sudan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and water security** in **Sudan and East Africa** through **bioeconomic modeling, crop adaptation strategies, and hydrological assessments**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s maize and sorghum research centers** developed climate-adaptive crop varieties and modeling tools to assess yield impacts under future climate scenarios (Sources: [10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005), [10.1016/j.fcr.2019.107685](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fcr.2019.107685)).
- **CGIAR’s water and resilience teams** analyzed climate extremes (droughts, floods, heat stress) and their implications for agriculture and livestock, while mapping agro-climatic shifts to inform adaptation (Sources: [10.1007/s00704-025-05405-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-025-05405-2), [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/127865](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/127865)).
Sudan.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s maize and sorghum research centers** modeled climate change impacts on maize and sorghum yields across **Sudan and sub-Saharan Africa**, identifying region-specific vulnerabilities (e.g., yield reductions in Western/Southern Africa) and proposing adaptive measures like improved varieties and nitrogen management (Source: [10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

• **CGIAR’s APSIM modeling team** tested sorghum varieties and management practices in **West Africa’s Sudanian zone**, demonstrating how trait-based interventions (e.g., radiation use efficiency) could enhance adaptive capacity under RCP 8.5 scenarios (Source: [10.1016/j.fcr.2019.107685](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fcr.2019.107685)).

• **CGIAR’s climate-livelihoods research** linked climate extremes (e.g., droughts) to **youth migration trends in East Africa**, quantifying correlations between dry days and outmigration stocks (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110278](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110278)).

• **CGIAR’s Sudan-focused team** integrated climate projections into a **dynamic CGE model**, estimating **$109.5 billion in economic losses (2018–2050)** due to extreme yield variability, while identifying policy interventions to mitigate risks (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/101350](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/101350)).

• **CGIAR’s East Africa resilience hub** synthesized climate-vulnerability maps and adaptation strategies for **10 East African nations (including Sudan)**, recommending resilience-building measures for smallholder farmers (Source: [10.2499/9780896292055](https://doi.org/10.2499/9780896292055)).

• **CGIAR’s water-agriculture team** developed a **framework for climate-resilient agriculture** in Sudan, combining hydrological data (GLDAS/GRACE) with groundwater mapping to optimize water productivity (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/127865](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/127865)).
Sudan.water:
• **CGIAR’s water resources team** analyzed **climate extremes (droughts, floods, heavy precipitation)** in East Africa, projecting shifts in agro-climatic zones (e.g., wetter conditions in northern Somalia/South Sudan) and their implications for livestock and agriculture (Source: [10.1007/s00704-025-05405-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-025-05405-2)).

• **CGIAR’s Sudan SAM modeling group** constructed a **2012 Social Accounting Matrix (SAM)** for Sudan, distinguishing **irrigated/non-irrigated land, natural water resources, and water-use accounts** to inform policy (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DO3MSH](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DO3MSH)).

• **CGIAR’s hydropower-irrigation research** evaluated **climate change impacts on Nile streamflow and irrigation supplies in Sudan**, assessing trade-offs under the Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/168426](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/168426)).

• **CGIAR’s water-agriculture team** linked **water stress, drought variability, and crop yield losses** in Sudan to broader economic impacts, using stochastic yield projections in CGE models (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/101350](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/101350)).

Syria.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agricultural adaptation** in **Syria** through **ecological modeling, participatory breeding, and water productivity assessments**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR researchers** evaluated climate vulnerability of native rangeland species using ecological models to predict distribution shifts and assess grazing pressure impacts (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-013-0701-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-013-0701-z)).
- **CGIAR’s economic modeling team** linked climate projections to crop yields and household welfare, identifying welfare losses across rural/urban groups and proposing adaptation strategies (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.1142/s2010007813500024](https://doi.org/10.1142/s2010007813500024), [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/154452](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/154452)).
Syria.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s climate vulnerability team** assessed rangeland species’ resilience to climate change, highlighting high vulnerability for grazed species (e.g., *Salsola vermiculata*) and reduced competition advantages for low-palatability species (e.g., *Haloxylon* spp.), recommending grazing management as an adaptation strategy ([https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-013-0701-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-013-0701-z)).

• **CGIAR economists** modeled Syria’s economic impacts of climate change, showing declining yields would reduce economy-wide growth and urban/rural incomes, with welfare losses across all households ([https://doi.org/10.1142/s2010007813500024](https://doi.org/10.1142/s2010007813500024), [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/154452](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/154452)).

• **CGIAR’s Arab World climate team** documented rural producers’ crop/herd losses due to droughts and floods, emphasizing migration pressures and the need for disaster risk management ([10.1596/978-0-8213-9846-3](https://doi.org/10.1596/978-0-8213-9846-3)).

• **CGIAR researchers** tested **decentralized-participatory plant breeding** in Syria to develop climate-resilient barley varieties tailored to marginal environments ([https://hdl.handle.net/10568/36077](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/36077)).

• **CGIAR proposed climate-resilient agriculture** as a recovery engine for post-conflict Syria, advocating climate-sensitive investments in the agricultural sector ([https://hdl.handle.net/10568/173986](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/173986)).

• **CGIAR’s Tel Hadya case study team** demonstrated that **supplemental irrigation (SI)** could reduce yield variability in wheat, serving as a potential adaptation strategy ([https://hdl.handle.net/10568/42086](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/42086)).
Syria.water:
• **CGIAR’s Euphrates-Tigris Basin team** assessed **agricultural water productivity (WPM)** in irrigated (2008 deficit year) and rainfed (2002 surplus year) areas, mapping evapotranspiration and crop productivity to identify improvement options ([hdl:20.500.11766.1/CUZ56W](hdl:20.500.11766.1/CUZ56W), [hdl:20.500.11766.1/D1D3GI](hdl:20.500.11766.1/D1D3GI), [hdl:20.500.11766.1/EWB8JR](hdl:20.500.11766.1/EWB8JR)).

• **CGIAR’s climate-impact modeling** linked drought-induced hydrological stress to declining agricultural yields, reinforcing water management as a critical adaptation lever ([https://doi.org/10.1142/s2010007813500024](https://doi.org/10.1142/s2010007813500024)).

Guinea.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and resilience-building** in **Guinea** through policy assessments, climate risk profiling, and capacity strengthening. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s CCAFS West Africa Program** assessed the climate-smartness of the **West Africa Agricultural Productivity Programme (WAAPP)** in Guinea, strengthening stakeholder capacity to mainstream CSA (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106455).
- **CGIAR-led consortia** (e.g., ICRAF, CORAF) implemented the **CaSCIERA-TA project** to enhance climate information use for resilience in Guinea’s agricultural sector (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108355).
Guinea.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS West Africa Program** evaluated climate-smart agriculture (CSA) integration in Guinea’s WAAPP projects, focusing on stakeholder capacity to implement resilience strategies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106455).
• **CGIAR-led CaSCIERA-TA** capacitated Guinea’s agricultural stakeholders (e.g., IRAG-Guinea) to use climate information for enhanced resilience, aligning with regional CSA goals (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108355).
• **CGIAR researchers** synthesized climate change impacts and adaptation strategies for West Africa’s crop, livestock, and fishery sectors, including Guinea’s farming systems (https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3).
• **CGIAR’s World Food Program collaboration** developed **Climate Risk Profiles** for Guinea, mapping current/future climate hazards (e.g., precipitation, temperature) to inform adaptation planning (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PKZ0G0).
• **CGIAR’s modeling frameworks** projected climate-vulnerability interactions in West Africa, identifying socioeconomic and climate drivers of agricultural land-use change (https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000712).
Guinea.mitigation: • **CGIAR’s linked biogeophysical-socioeconomic modeling** analyzed **greenhouse gas (GHG) concentration changes** and their climate impacts in West Africa, including Guinea’s cropland regions (https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000712).

Germany.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and sustainable agriculture** in **Germany** through policy-influencing research, adaptive strategies, and mitigation innovations. Specifically:
- The Center advanced **forest genetic resource (FGR) management** to enhance climate adaptation in European ecosystems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).
- CGIAR researchers modeled **climate-smart agricultural adaptation** via crop variety R&D and irrigation tech upgrades (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
Germany.adaptation:
• CGIAR surveyed **European forest owners/managers** to assess perceptions of climate threats (e.g., pests, drought) and barriers to adopting **forest genetic adaptation** (e.g., non-local planting material), identifying gaps in evidence-based decision-making (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).

• The Center analyzed **climate risks to coffee production** in Germany’s industry context, proposing risk-minimization strategies for stakeholders (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/79869](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/79869)).

• CGIAR developed **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) frameworks** to balance food security, emissions reduction, and adaptive capacity, critiquing reductive interpretations of CSA (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99575](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99575)).

• Researchers modeled **adaptation via R&D (new crop varieties) and irrigation tech** in OECD contexts, estimating public/private investment needs (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).

• The **CompensAction Initiative** (funded by Germany’s BMZ) incentivized small-scale farmers to deliver **ecosystem services for climate adaptation/mitigation** via payments (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/132335](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/132335)).

• CGIAR contributed to **African climate adaptation research** (e.g., Ethiopia/South Africa) under Germany-funded projects, linking hydropower, drought resilience, and policy tools (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/154495](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/154495)).
Germany.mitigation:
• CGIAR’s **CIAT** presented on **agricultural emissions mitigation**, addressing cost/implementation barriers at Berlin’s 2018 conference (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99574](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99574)).

• Researchers explored **soil carbon accumulation via forage cultivation** in pasture systems, presented at COP23 (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/89525](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/89525)).

• The Center piloted **low-emission dairy mitigation options** in China (via Germany-funded CGIAR projects), evaluating feasibility for scaling (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111194](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111194)).

• CGIAR’s **IFPRI** critiqued **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA)** at Berlin’s 2018 conference, emphasizing its dual role in **mitigation and food security** (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99575](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99575)).

Israel.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural adaptation and water-sensitive disease modeling** in **Israel** through targeted research and simulation tools. Specifically:
- CGIAR’s **climate modeling and policy analysis** advanced adaptation strategies for agriculture, focusing on crop resilience and irrigation innovation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
- The **Center for International Forestry Research (CIFOR) and CIAT** facilitated international knowledge exchange on pasture and agronomy systems, fostering collaborative research networks (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110096](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110096)).
Israel.adaptation:
• CGIAR used the **IMPACT model** to evaluate climate change impacts on agricultural yields and food security, proposing **new crop varieties and irrigation technology upgrades** as key adaptation strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en) ).
• The **CLIFF-GRADS program (CIAT/CGIAR)** hosted thematic sessions on **pasture and agronomy systems**, enabling cross-institutional collaboration to refine climate-resilient agronomic practices (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110096](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110096)).
Israel.water: • CGIAR’s **CIP (International Potato Center)** validated and expanded the **LATEBLIGHT model** to simulate *Phytophthora infestans* epidemics in Israel (Mivtakhim, Nir-Eliyahu, Nir-Oz), demonstrating its sensitivity to **weather variables** and extending its applicability beyond Peru (Sources: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/P3/GTV7Y4](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/P3/GTV7Y4), [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/P3/UZVDW6](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/P3/UZVDW6), [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/P3/XD4APA](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/P3/XD4APA)).

Chile.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, genetic conservation, and silvopastoral systems** in **Chile** through targeted research on adaptation and mitigation. Specifically:
- **CIAT** and **Bioversity** advanced **climate-smart food systems** (e.g., agroforestry, silvipastoral systems) to enhance resilience and productivity (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116921).
- **Bioversity** mapped **genetic diversity hotspots** and assessed climate threats to plant species, informing conservation policies (10.1007/s10531-023-02770-0).
- **CIAT** and **CIFOR** analyzed **local adaptation strategies** among Mapuche communities, linking Indigenous knowledge to climate monitoring (10.1038/s44168-024-00124-2).
Chile.adaptation:
• **CGIAR (CIAT, Bioversity)** developed **climate-smart agriculture frameworks** (e.g., Local Technical Agroclimatic Committees) to integrate mitigation and adaptation practices, including new crop varieties and irrigation technologies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/114943).
• **Bioversity** identified **genetic diversity hotspots** in southern Chile/Argentina and modeled climate impacts on plant species, proposing **conservation policies** to preserve adaptive genetic resources (10.1007/s10531-023-02770-0).
• **CGIAR (CIAT)** conducted cross-cultural research with **Mapuche communities** to validate climate change impact reports, emphasizing **site-specific adaptation strategies** for pastoralism and crop production (10.1038/s44168-024-00124-2).
• **CGIAR (CIAT-CCAFS)** scaled **Climate-Smart Food Systems (CSFS)** business models, including **regenerative livestock and agroforestry**, to improve resilience and productivity (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116921).
Chile.mitigation:
• **CGIAR (CIFOR)** analyzed **silvopastoral systems (SPS)** in Latin America (including Chile) as a **mitigation tool**, demonstrating their potential to **reduce GHG emissions (CH₄, N₂O)** and **increase carbon stocks**, while aligning with **Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs)** (10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2).
• **CGIAR (CIFOR, CIAT)** researched **forest landscape restoration** in Latin America, quantifying its **climate mitigation potential** through aboveground biomass storage and Bonn Challenge compliance (10.3390/f10060510).
• **CGIAR (CIAT-CCAFS)** leveraged **carbon markets and MRV (Measurement, Reporting, Verification)** to support **emissions reductions** in Climate-Smart Food Systems, including silvipastoral and agroforestry models (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116921).

Australia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptive capacity** in **Australia** through **livestock-based livelihoods, Indigenous knowledge integration, and climate-smart agricultural strategies**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Global Rangelands Program** modeled climate impacts on Australian rangelands, projecting **declines in livestock productivity (7.5–9.6%) and soil carbon shifts** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13995](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13995)).
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) program** engaged Indigenous communities in **Arnhem Land to integrate Yolngu perspectives into adaptation policies**, emphasizing **self-sufficiency and resilience** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2010.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2010.05.002)).
Australia.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Global Rangelands Program** quantified **climate-induced declines in Australian rangeland productivity (17% drop in NPP)** and **livestock economic losses ($9.7–$12.6 billion)**, while assessing **adaptation policy costs/benefits** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13995](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13995) and [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395) [Water/Adaptation])

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** facilitated **gender-transformative climate-smart agriculture (CSA) workshops in Canberra**, identifying **research gaps for socially inclusive adaptation** (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/105556](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/105556))

• **CGIAR’s Bioversity International** mapped **pigeonpea wild relatives in northern Australia** to enhance **drought/heat tolerance traits** for breeding (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/GU5AJW](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/GU5AJW))

• **CGIAR’s System Dynamics Research** analyzed **Australian semi-arid rangelands**, linking **drought recovery times (years–decades) to farming practices** and **methane emissions trade-offs** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14669](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14669))

• **CGIAR’s IMPACT Model Team** evaluated **adaptation strategies (new crop varieties, irrigation tech)** for Australian agriculture under climate change (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en))

• **CGIAR’s Climate Resilience Platform** (via PepsiCo collaboration) modeled **climate risks for potato supply chains**, prioritizing **adaptation practices in 12 countries** (including Australia) (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/152422](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/152422))

• **CGIAR’s Indigenous Knowledge Program** developed **community vulnerability models** in Arnhem Land, linking **ecological changes to poverty and policy barriers** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2010.05.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2010.05.002))
Australia.mitigation: • **CGIAR’s Green Growth Research** assessed **Australia’s national climate-smart agriculture (CSA) integration** within **Green Growth strategies**, highlighting **sustainability investments** (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/33278](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/33278))
Australia.water:
• **CGIAR’s Agro-IBIS Modeling** projected **15% drying in southeastern Australia’s growing season**, linking **hydroclimatic shifts to 70% wheat yield declines** under RCP scenarios (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1175/jcli-d-13-00326.1](https://doi.org/10.1175/jcli-d-13-00326.1))

• **CGIAR’s Global Rangelands Program** identified **increased inter-annual (21.3%) and intra-annual (8.2%) vegetation productivity variability** in Australian rangelands, threatening **grazing availability** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395))

Somalia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agricultural adaptation** in **Somalia** through targeted research on climate impacts, livelihood security, and institutional capacity-building. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate and Food Security research** analyzed climate change impacts on pastoral livelihoods, revealing potential productivity gains under future rainfall increases but requiring agile farm management (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/2976-601x/ad6fa0](https://doi.org/10.1088/2976-601x/ad6fa0)).
- **CGIAR’s East Africa Agroclimate team** assessed climate extremes (droughts, floods) and their implications for agro-climatic zones, highlighting regional heterogeneity in climate stress (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-025-05405-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-025-05405-2)).
Somalia.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Resilience team** evaluated climate change impacts on livestock smallholders in Somalia, projecting increased rainfall and temperatures could boost pasture productivity and farm profits under low- and high-emission scenarios, contingent on adaptive management (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/2976-601x/ad6fa0](https://doi.org/10.1088/2976-601x/ad6fa0) and [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106796](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106796)).

• **CGIAR’s Policy and Security Research** analyzed climate-related security risks in Somalia, identifying research gaps in pathways linking climate hazards (e.g., droughts, floods) to conflict and displacement, and proposing frameworks for policy coherence (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/137400](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/137400)).

• **CGIAR’s Migration and Livelihoods team** linked climate extremes (e.g., droughts) to increased youth migration in East Africa, including Somalia, with projections of longer droughts exacerbating vulnerability (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110278](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110278)).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Information Services** facilitated capacity-building workshops in Eastern Africa to strengthen institutional linkages for climate services, improving seasonal forecasting for agriculture and food security (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116941](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116941)).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Risk Profiling** developed thematic risk assessments for Somalia, mapping current and future climate hazards (e.g., precipitation, temperature) to inform adaptation strategies (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PKZ0G0](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PKZ0G0)).

• **CGIAR’s East Africa Agroclimate team** projected shifts in agro-climatic zones (e.g., wetter northern Somalia) and climate extremes (e.g., heavy precipitation), with critical implications for livestock and agriculture (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-025-05405-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-025-05405-2)).
Somalia.mitigation: • **CGIAR’s Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) research** reviewed livestock production systems in Eastern Africa, including Somalia, to assess GHG emissions and CSA opportunities, though no Somalia-specific mitigation interventions were detailed in this sample (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106995](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106995)).

Burundi.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and food security** in **Burundi** through **crop improvement, gender-inclusive climate-smart technologies, and scenario-based adaptive planning**. Specifically:
- CGIAR’s **crop improvement programs** (via CGIAR Centers and NARS partnerships) documented the adoption and impact of improved bean varieties, linking variety turnover to income growth for rural households (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/MOCXUN).
- Researchers developed **spatially explicit recommendations** for adapting root, tuber, and banana crops (e.g., drought-tolerant varieties) to future climates in the Great Lakes Region, including Burundi (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2021.103246).
- The **Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) Program** explored **gender gaps in climate-smart agricultural (CSA) adoption**, identifying barriers and opportunities for joint decision-making among smallholder bean farmers in Kirundo and Muyinga provinces (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/KQJUEP).
Burundi.adaptation:
• CGIAR’s **scenario-guided adaptive action framework** addressed food security vulnerabilities in East Africa, including Burundi, by developing **multi-actor, long-term strategic planning capacities** to enable resilience through participatory back-casting and collaborative proposals (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2014.03.001).

• Researchers at **CCAFS and CIAT** assessed **climate hazards (precipitation/temperature)** for Burundi, integrating findings into **Climate Risk Profiles** to inform adaptive planning (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PKZ0G0).

• CGIAR’s **East African Agriculture and Climate Change analysis** (IFPRI-led) mapped **future food security threats** for Burundi, using scenario models to recommend policy interventions for extreme-event resilience (https://doi.org/10.2499/9780896292055).

• The Center **evaluated adaptive capacity** of food systems in the Great Lakes Region by identifying **‘climate-proof’ crop varieties** (e.g., drought/heat-tolerant cassava, sweetpotato) and optimizing planting schedules to improve suitability under future climates (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2021.103246).

• CGIAR’s **bean variety adoption database** (SSA-wide) included Burundi, tracking uptake patterns to support **Green Revolution goals** and rural livelihood resilience (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/MOCXUN).

• The **CCAFS Program** analyzed **political/socio-economic scenarios** for East Africa, including Burundi, to model **vulnerability to climate change** and propose concerted action for food-environment integration (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/27955).
Burundi.mitigation:
• CGIAR researchers conducted a **literature review on soil organic carbon (SOC) sequestration potential** in East African agricultural systems (including Burundi), assessing **best management practices (BMPs)** for SOC stock increases and net sequestration (e.g., 0–30 cm soil depth) (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/3BLW7E).

• A **cross-sectoral study** (CGIAR/IFPRI) highlighted **land cover/land use change (LCLUC) and greenhouse gas (GHG) interactions** as critical drivers of regional climate dynamics, with implications for mitigation strategies in Burundi’s food production systems (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-011-0116-7).

Canada.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural adaptation and mitigation** in **Canada** through policy support, resilience-building, and global synthesis of land-based mitigation strategies. Specifically:
- The Center contributed to **scaling innovations in smallholder agriculture** via the **Canadian International Food Security Research Fund (CIFSRF)**, emphasizing socio-ecological dynamics and sustainable agri-food systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2019.05.012](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2019.05.012)).
- CGIAR’s **Climate-Smart Village (CSV) approach** was supported by Canada’s **International Development Research Centre (IDRC)** to enhance household resilience and livelihoods in climate-vulnerable regions (Sources: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123688](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123688), [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123689](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123689)).
- The Center synthesized **global land-based mitigation opportunities** in agriculture, including Canadian case studies, to accelerate emissions reduction through farming systems (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/16715](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/16715)).
Canada.adaptation:
• CGIAR analyzed **global rangeland ecosystem services**, projecting **21% increased productivity in northern regions (e.g., Canada)** under RCP 8.5, while quantifying economic losses ($9.7–$12.6 billion) from livestock declines due to climate change, and proposed adaptation policy frameworks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13995](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13995)).

• The Center developed **scaling principles for smallholder agriculture** through the **Canadian International Food Security Research Fund (CIFSRF)**, addressing climate variability and socio-ecological complexities to foster sustainable system outcomes (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2019.05.012](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2019.05.012)).

• CGIAR modeled **adaptation strategies** (e.g., new crop varieties, irrigation tech) using the **IMPACT model**, estimating public/private R&D investment needs for OECD countries, including Canada (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).

• Researchers supported **PepsiCo’s Climate Resilience Platform**, integrating climate-yield models to prioritize adaptation practices and supply-chain resilience across 12 countries, including Canadian-linked supply chains (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/152422](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/152422)).

• CGIAR’s **Climate-Smart Village (CSV) approach**, funded by Canada’s **IDRC**, enhanced **household resilience and livelihoods** in Myanmar’s Dry Zone, with lessons applicable to Canada’s northern agricultural systems (Sources: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123688](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123688), [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123689](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123689)).

• The Center facilitated **APEC’s Multi-Year Action Plan on Climate Change and Food Security (2018–2021)**, organizing stakeholder consultations to align priorities with Canadian and global food security goals (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121197](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121197)).
Canada.mitigation: • CGIAR synthesized **land-based climate mitigation strategies**, highlighting Canada’s role in reducing agricultural greenhouse gas emissions through agroecological farming systems (e.g., livestock, crops, agroforestry) and economic incentives (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/16715](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/16715)).

Netherlands.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart adaptation and mitigation strategies** in the **Netherlands** through collaborative research, policy engagement, and financial innovation. Specifically:
- The Center facilitated **farm-household modeling** to evaluate climate adaptation, risk management, and mitigation options (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/24766**).
- Researchers developed **climate services** (e.g., PICSA) to enhance agricultural resilience and drought forecasting (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/92851**).
Netherlands.adaptation:
• CGIAR modeled **adaptation strategies** (e.g., new crop varieties and irrigation tech) to mitigate climate risks in agriculture using the IMPACT model (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en**).
• The Center enhanced **local access to crop genetic diversity** in Benin (via participatory methods) to support climate-resilient maize farming, with Dutch policy support (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/96090**).
• CGIAR organized a **workshop in Amsterdam** to refine farm-household models for climate adaptation and risk management (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/24766**).
• Researchers designed a **SME self-assessment tool** to accelerate climate-smart investment readiness, funded by the Netherlands (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/114725**).
• The Center catalyzed **€34M in private-sector finance** for climate-resilient agribusiness in East Africa, targeting 237,000 farmers (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121858**).
• CGIAR promoted **climate services (PICSA)** and drought monitoring tools in East Africa, with Dutch research funding (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/92851**).
Netherlands.mitigation: • CGIAR piloted **low-emission dairy farming options** in China (collaborating with Wageningen University) to reduce greenhouse gas emissions (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111194**).

Madagascar.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptive agriculture** in **Madagascar** through targeted research, policy tools, and crop diversity strategies. Specifically:
- **Bioversity International** piloted a **seed resilience strategy** to enhance farmers’ access to climate-adapted crop varieties (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/78539).
- **CGIAR researchers** developed **decision-making tools** for national laws and guidelines to implement the **Plant Treaty**, identifying adapted materials for climate-stressed agricultural systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121359).
Madagascar.adaptation:
• **CGIAR (via Bioversity International)** implemented a **comprehensive seed resilience strategy** in Madagascar, combining climate modeling and participatory methods to strengthen farmers’ access to diverse, climate-adapted crop varieties (e.g., maize, banana, cassava) (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/78539).

• **CGIAR researchers** provided **national partners with decision-making tools** to adopt laws and guidelines (2018) for implementing the **Plant Treaty**, enabling the identification of adapted materials for climate-stressed local agricultural systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121359).

• **CGIAR (IFPRI-led)** analyzed **climate vulnerability in Madagascar**, highlighting risks from droughts, cyclones, and extreme heat, and proposed **adaptation measures** for food security (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/154082).

• **CGIAR (CCAFS)** contributed to **climate services and capacity development** in East Africa (including Madagascar) through policy support and curriculum deployment (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121837).

• **CGIAR (via SADC Rapid Risk Assessment)** identified **northern Madagascar as a climate risk hotspot** for extreme rainfall and drought under RCP4.5/8.5 scenarios, mapping vulnerability to inform regional resilience strategies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/109192).
Madagascar.water: • **CGIAR (CIAT/CGIAR)** collected and provided **daily weather data for Ambohibary, Madagascar (2019–2020)**, supporting localized climate and agricultural modeling (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/GWCGGB).

Bolivia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, indigenous knowledge, and resilient food systems** in **Bolivia** through targeted research on adaptation, mitigation, and livelihood strategies. Specifically:
- **CIAT-CCAFS** developed frameworks for **Climate-Smart Food Systems (CSFS)** to scale agroforestry, regenerative livestock, and silvipastoral systems, addressing mitigation and adaptation in coffee, cocoa, and potato production (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116921).
- **CIP** validated climate threats to potato agriculture (e.g., temperature extremes, drought) and promoted **drought- and temperature-resilient potato varieties**, improved water management, and genetic diversity conservation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/65725).
- **Bioversity International** assessed **on-farm conservation projects** in the High Andes, documenting smallholder farmers’ use of native crop diversity for climate adaptation (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/26678).
Bolivia.adaptation:
• **CIAT-CCAFS** analyzed **site-specific climate change impacts** among indigenous Tsimane’ communities in Bolivia, revealing high consistency in reports of pastoralism-related changes and identifying livelihood activities as key factors for adaptation planning (https://doi.org/10.1038/s44168-024-00124-2).

• **CIP** validated **ASAP solutions** for potato agriculture, including **hardy potato varieties**, improved water management, and ex situ/in situ genetic conservation to counter temperature increases and extreme weather (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/65725).

• **Bioversity International** surveyed **2,400 smallholder farmers** in Bolivia to demonstrate how **agricultural biodiversity** enhances adaptive capacity, with findings used to design resilience-building strategies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/52109).

• **CGIAR researchers** conducted **gender-disaggregated surveys** on crop diversity for climate adaptation, assessing indigenous seed systems and resistance to temperature extremes among Cochabamba and La Paz farmers (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/XYKYDW).

• **CGIAR** developed **risk-prediction models** for potato pests (e.g., *Phthorimaea operculella*) to mitigate infestation risks in Bolivia (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111454).

• **CGIAR** proposed **methods to measure climate impacts on food/nutrition security** in Latin America, including Bolivia, with tools for monitoring productivity and livelihood resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/80948).
Bolivia.mitigation:
• **CGIAR researchers** examined **hydrologic impacts of afforestation/reforestation** under the **Clean Development Mechanism (CDM-AR)**, modeling **runoff reductions (54–100%)** in Bolivia’s semi-arid regions due to increased evapotranspiration (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2008.01.015).

• **CIAT-CCAFS** identified **high-impact business models** for **climate mitigation** (e.g., agroforestry, regenerative livestock), emphasizing **MRV of emissions** and carbon market access to scale Climate-Smart Food Systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116921).

• **CGIAR** analyzed **policy gaps** in Latin America, highlighting research on **forest-based GHG mitigation** while noting limited focus on rural energy/industry sectors (https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831).
Bolivia.water:
• **CGIAR researchers** quantified **hydrologic trade-offs** of afforestation in Bolivia, showing **local-to-regional runoff declines** (up to 100% in arid zones) due to CDM-AR projects, with downstream/upstream dynamics as critical factors (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2008.01.015).

• **CIP** integrated **improved water management practices** into potato adaptation strategies to offset climate-induced growing season disruptions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/65725).

Sierra Leone.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, resilience, and carbon-based mitigation** in **Sierra Leone** through **agroforestry, cocoa adaptation strategies, and integrated land-water management**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** assessed **agroforestry’s carbon sequestration potential** on agricultural lands, highlighting regional trends (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987)).
- **CIP/World Agroforestry Centre** evaluated **shade trees as adaptation strategies** for cocoa resilience under rising temperatures (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2016.03.024](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2016.03.024)).
- **CIAT/IWMI/IFPRI** conducted **household surveys** on **land-water management** to enhance climate resilience (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV)).
Sierra Leone.adaptation:
• **CIAT, CIP, and IWMI** analyzed **cocoa vulnerability to climate change** in West Africa, recommending **shade trees and agronomic adjustments** to reduce temperature stress (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2016.03.024](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2016.03.024)).
• **CGIAR (via CORAF/WECARD)** implemented **integrated land-water management projects** to improve smallholder resilience to climate variability (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV)).
• **CGIAR researchers** proposed **regional adaptation zones** for cocoa, classifying areas by need for intensification, diversification, or crop change (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-016-9707-y](https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-016-9707-y)).
• **CGIAR (BIODEV Program)** developed **climate-smart adaptation strategies** linking mitigation and livelihood improvements in Sierra Leone (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34725/DVN/ZDW61D](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34725/DVN/ZDW61D)).
• **CGIAR** reviewed **climate-smart agriculture policies** for crops, livestock, and fisheries, emphasizing resilience-building in West Africa (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3)).
Sierra Leone.mitigation:
• **CGIAR (via remote sensing)** quantified **biomass carbon stocks** on agricultural lands, including Sierra Leone’s declining trends, to inform national carbon budgets (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987)).
• **CGIAR (BIODEV Program)** explored **biocarbon approaches** to integrate climate mitigation and adaptation in rural landscapes (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34725/DVN/ZDW61D](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34725/DVN/ZDW61D)).
• **CGIAR researchers** modeled **land-use change and GHG interactions**, showing agricultural expansion’s climate impacts in West Africa (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000712](https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000712)).
Sierra Leone.water:
• **CGIAR (CIAT/IWMI/IFPRI)** evaluated **land-water management practices** in Sierra Leone to enhance climate resilience (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV)).
• **CGIAR researchers** linked **hydrological cycles and crop yields** to project climate-induced land-use shifts in West Africa (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000712](https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000712)).

Gambia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and adaptive resilience** in **The Gambia** through systematic stocktaking of adaptation options, farmer-centered research, and policy-relevant assessments. Specifically:
- **CIAT, IWMI, and IFPRI** evaluated **land and water management practices** to enhance farmer resilience (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV).
- **Bioversity and CGIAR centers** mapped **28 CSA technologies** (e.g., drought-tolerant crops, agroforestry) to build climate resilience and reduce GHG emissions (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111156).
Gambia.adaptation:
• **CGIAR researchers** applied **Protection Motivation Theory** to analyze farmers’ adoption of water conservation techniques in the Central River Region, identifying barriers like cost and soil degradation (Source: https://doi.org/10.3390/cli7010013).

• **CIAT and IWMI** conducted a **systematic stocktaking of 24 adaptation options** (e.g., climate-resilient crop varieties, weather-indexed insurance) to inform Gambia’s National Adaptation Plan (Source: https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2022.834867).

• **CGIAR’s West Africa team** documented **farm-level coping strategies** (e.g., drought-tolerant crops, chemical fertilizers) in response to perceived climate variability, with 95% of farmers citing dryness as the primary threat (Source: https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos10070423).

• **CGIAR synthesized 28 CSA technologies** (e.g., System of Rice Intensification, agroforestry) to increase productivity while reducing vulnerability (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111156).

• **CGIAR’s Green Climate Fund (GCF) data team** categorized Gambia’s **FP011 project** under the **IPCC Risk Framework**, linking climate hazards to adaptive responses (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PY3YOZ).

• **CGIAR’s modeling framework** projected **future yield drops** in West Africa, emphasizing climate-smart policies for maize systems (Source: https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
Gambia.mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s regional modeling** assessed **GHG-induced climate changes** and **land-use-induced GHG signals**, highlighting mitigation potential in agricultural expansion (Source: https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000712).

• **CGIAR’s CSA synthesis** identified **soil and water conservation practices** as mitigation-positive, while fertilizers showed neutral/negative impacts (Source: https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2022.8348667).

• **CGIAR’s adaptation planning** framed **agroforestry and System of Rice Intensification** as triple-impact solutions (productivity, adaptation, mitigation) (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111156).
Gambia.water:
• **CGIAR’s land-water management projects** (funded by Denmark via CORAF/WECARD) evaluated **site-specific practices** in Gambia to enhance resilience through improved soil storage and water conservation (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV).

• **CGIAR’s maize study** analyzed **biophysical water-nitrogen interactions** under climate change, projecting yield reductions linked to water scarcity (Source: https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).

Tajikistan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agrifood systems innovation** in **Tajikistan** through **modeling, stakeholder coordination, and genetic conservation**. Specifically:
- **IFPRI** developed **bio-economic farm models** to assess sub-national climate change impacts on small-scale farms in arid zones (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2014.02.033](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2014.02.033)).
- **Bioversity** conserved **Juglans regia (walnut) genetic diversity** in wild and cultivated populations across Central Asia, including Tajikistan (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VUP7OH](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VUP7OH)).
Tajikistan.adaptation:
• **IFPRI** used the **bio-economic farm model (BEFM)** to evaluate climate change risks and adaptation strategies for small-scale farms in Tajikistan’s arid zones, highlighting income volatility and water scarcity threats (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2014.02.033](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2014.02.033)).

• **CGIAR** mapped **stakeholder institutions** to strengthen **NDC implementation** and vertical/horizontal coordination for climate actions in Tajikistan (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/138814](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/138814)).

• **IFPRI** analyzed **climate change effects on food security** (2015–2050) using the **IMPACT model**, linking policy priorities to Tajikistan’s **National Development Strategy** (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/141633](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/141633)).

• **IFPRI** developed a **Social Accounting Matrix (SAM)** for Tajikistan to improve **agrifood systems modeling**, disaggregating agricultural land and labor for policy analysis (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ASZFUC](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ASZFUC)).

• **CGIAR** studied **transboundary water cooperation** in the **Khojabakirgansai basin**, assessing community-led adaptation to droughts and flash floods (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/34723](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/34723)).
Tajikistan.mitigation: • **CGIAR** contributed to Tajikistan’s **NDC Implementation Plan**, identifying **GHG mitigation challenges** in agriculture and energy sectors while supporting the **Committee for Environmental Protection (CEP)** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.136837](https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.136837)).
Tajikistan.water:
• **IFPRI** conducted **household surveys** under the **USAID Feed the Future Tajikistan Agriculture and Water Activity (TAWA)** to assess nutrition-sensitive value chains in Khatlon Province (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BMKEMG](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BMKEMG)).

• **IFPRI** collected **baseline and panel data** on water use, migration, and remittances in USAID’s **Zone of Influence (ZOI)** for adaptive water management (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DHRV46](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DHRV46)).

• **CGIAR** analyzed **water scarcity and canal infrastructure** in transboundary basins, proposing **cooperative adaptation strategies** for climate-exacerbated droughts (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/34723](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/34723)).

Tunisia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptive strategies** in **Tunisia** through targeted research on rangeland restoration, agricultural productivity, and climate risk assessment. Specifically:
- **CGIAR researchers** evaluated vulnerability of native rangeland species to climate change, identifying grazing pressure as a key factor in species resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-013-0701-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-013-0701-z)).
- **The Center** analyzed climate risks in potato and dairy value chains, recommending innovations to manage climate-related hazards (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111410](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111410)).
Tunisia.adaptation:
• **CGIAR researchers** assessed climate vulnerabilities in Tunisia’s rangelands, highlighting species-specific resilience (e.g., *Haloxylon* spp.) due to reduced competition for water/nutrients under grazing pressure, while recommending grazing management as an adaptation strategy (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-013-0701-z](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-013-0701-z) and [hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/2ZQCH3](hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/2ZQCH3) for rangeland restoration).

• **The Center** documented economic impacts of climate change on Tunisia’s agriculture, proposing policy measures to minimize yield declines and leverage global agricultural price trends (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1353/jda.2015.0002](https://doi.org/10.1353/jda.2015.0002)).

• **CGIAR** developed the **TACCS tool** to quantify climate change perception and adaptation readiness in organizations, enabling targeted resilience-building programs (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/148773](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/148773)).

• **Researchers** analyzed climate risks in livestock sectors, creating spatial risk hotspot maps for Tunisia to prioritize adaptation actions (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/132196](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/132196)).

• **CGIAR** simulated climate change impacts on farm productivity, testing adaptation strategies (e.g., increased irrigation/fertilization for hard wheat) under varying temperature/precipitation scenarios (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/154919](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/154919)).

• **The Center** conducted a phone survey on COVID-19’s impact on smallholder crop-livestock systems, capturing farmers’ perceptions of productivity shifts and future land-use adaptations (Source: [hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/1NXHGT](hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/1NXHGT)).

• **CGIAR** synthesized climate vulnerabilities in the Arab region, including Tunisia, emphasizing rural migration and food/water shortages as adaptation challenges (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1596/978-0-8213-9846-3](https://doi.org/10.1596/978-0-8213-9846-3)).
Tunisia.water:
• **CGIAR researchers** explored water-related adaptation strategies, such as increased irrigation for hard wheat, to offset climate-induced productivity declines, though success depended on water availability (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/154919](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/154919)).

• **The Center** analyzed irrigation-crop-livestock systems, documenting farmers’ perceptions of productivity impacts under COVID-19, with implications for water management under climate stress (Source: [hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/1NXHGT](hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/1NXHGT)).

Togo.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and resilience-building** in **Togo** through participatory, value-chain-specific interventions and capacity strengthening. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s West Africa Program** analyzed climate vulnerabilities in peanut, cashew, and soybean value chains to inform adaptation priorities (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111345**).
- **CGIAR (via CaSCIERA-TA)** trained stakeholders in Togo to mainstream CSA using climate information, fostering resilience in agricultural sectors (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108355**).
Togo.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s West Africa Program** conducted a climate vulnerability analysis for Green Innovation Centres (GICs) in Togo’s key commodity value chains (peanuts, cashews, soybeans), identifying hazards and adaptation priorities to manage climate risks (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111345**).

• **CGIAR (CCAFS West Africa, ICRAF, and partners)** assessed the climate-smartness of WAAPP projects in Togo, strengthening stakeholder capacity to implement CSA through the **CaSCIERA-WA** initiative (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106455**).

• **CGIAR (Alliance Bioversity-CIAT)** developed participatory, village-based CSA training manuals for Togo, integrating field visits to demonstrate practical climate-resilient agricultural practices (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/144087**).

• **CGIAR’s modeling framework** (via linked biogeophysical-socioeconomic models) projected climate-cropland interactions in West Africa, highlighting transient dynamics for adaptation strategies—including implications for Togo’s cropland expansion under climate variability (Sources: **https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000712**, **https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000721**).

• **CGIAR (via WAAPP)** synthesized climate change impacts and adaptation strategies for West Africa’s crop, livestock, and fishery sectors, with direct relevance to Togo’s farming systems and policy development (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3**).
Togo.mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s research** explored the role of agricultural land use change (LUC) in greenhouse gas (GHG) concentration changes, using West Africa as a case study to assess mitigation potential through climate-cropland modeling (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000712**).

• **CGIAR (AICCRA)** capacitated West African civil society organizations—including those in Togo—to mobilize climate finance (e.g., Green Climate Fund) for locally relevant mitigation and adaptation actions (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179563**).
Togo.water:
• **CGIAR researchers** analyzed trends in **reference evapotranspiration (ETo)** and aridity indices in Togo (1961–2011), revealing significant increases in ETo at Tabligbo and Sokode, which threaten rainfed agriculture—critical for 90% of Togolese farmers (Source: **https://doi.org/10.2166/wcc.2015.111**).

• **CGIAR’s modeling framework** examined how **surface mass/energy fluxes** (hydrological cycles) interact with agricultural land use in West Africa, including water productivity impacts relevant to Togo’s climate-smart agriculture (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000712**).

• **CGIAR’s transient climate-cropland models** projected future rainfall declines in western West Africa (including Togo), informing water-resilient adaptation strategies (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000721**).

Bhutan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate change adaptation in Bhutan** through collaborative research, capacity-building, and climate-resilient agricultural strategies. Specifically:
- **CIAT** assessed climate suitability for key crops (rice, maize, potato, chili, tomato) and diversification crops (quinoa, kiwi, cardamom) under future climate scenarios, identifying vulnerable regions and potential expansion zones (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/80918).
- **CIAT** and **MoAF** developed climate-resilient crop management practices and suitability maps for Bhutan’s agricultural sector, integrating gender-sensitive case studies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/HGECWU).
Bhutan.adaptation:
• **CIAT** conducted climate projections for Bhutan’s agricultural sector, mapping loss of suitability for staple crops (rice, maize, potato) and identifying test sites for diversification crops (quinoa, kiwi, cardamom) under high-emission scenarios (RCP 8.5) by 2050 (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/80918).

• **CIAT** and **UNEP** collaborated with **MoAF** to build technical capacity in climate modeling, producing suitability maps and introducing climate-resilient crop management practices (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/HGECWU).

• **Bioversity International** piloted a seed resilience strategy in Bhutan, combining climate modeling tools and participatory methods to enhance farmers’ access to climate-adapted crop diversity (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/78539).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** facilitated trans-disciplinary networks (e.g., Direct Seeded Rice Consortium) to integrate local and scientific knowledge for climate adaptation in Bhutan’s highland agriculture (10.3390/cli8020035).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** and **IFPRI** supported Bhutan’s **MoAF** in integrating Climate Smart Agriculture (CSA) technologies into the **Agricultural Development Plan to 2040**, aligning with evolving environmental dynamics (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121307).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** assessed climate vulnerability in Bhutan, highlighting gaps in historical climate data and national capacity for modeling, while emphasizing the need for adaptive germplasm strategies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/42124).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** developed a novel strategy to discover and deploy climate-adapted germplasm, training Bhutanese researchers in climate analogue tools to address future crop needs (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/68477).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** organized regional meetings (e.g., South Asia CCAFS) to scale up climate-smart agricultural practices, including big-data analytics and South-South collaboration for Bhutan (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106626).

• **CGIAR** evaluated hybrid adaptive knowledge systems in Bhutan’s Asian Highlands, advocating for state support to integrate local and scientific expertise for climate resilience (10.1007/s10584-014-1090-7).

Iran.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient wheat adaptation** in **Iran** through participatory breeding and stress-adapted trait nurseries. Specifically:
- CGIAR’s **Wheat Program** (via ITPGRFA and CIMMYT) developed drought- and heat-tolerant wheat landraces through participatory selection, marker-assisted breeding, and disease-resistant crosses, promoting on-farm adoption and biodiversity conservation (hdl:11529/10548355).
- The **Stress Adapted Trait Yield Nurseries (SATYN)** program phenotyped and distributed drought/heat-tolerant wheat lines across Iran’s mega-environments, leveraging traits like canopy temperature depression, chlorophyll stability, and accelerated maturity (hdl:11529/10548400, hdl:11529/10548397, hdl:11529/10548398, hdl:11529/10548426, hdl:11529/10548395, hdl:11529/10548396, hdl:11529/10548394, hdl:11529/10548534, hdl:11529/10548401).
Iran.adaptation:
• CGIAR’s **Wheat Program** (CIMMYT) conducted a **Genome-Wide Association Study (GWAS)** on drought/heat-tolerant wheat landraces in Iran (Maragheh, 2019), integrating participatory selection with disease resistance (yellow/leaf/stem rust) and biodiversity-based climate resilience (hdl:11529/10548355).

• CGIAR’s **SATYN program** (via IWIN/CSISA) phenotyped **9 iterations of stress-adapted wheat nurseries** in Iran’s drought/heat-prone regions, testing traits like canopy temperature depression, chlorophyll retention, and accelerated maturity across 136 global environments (hdl:11529/10548400, hdl:11529/10548397, hdl:11529/10548398, hdl:11529/10548426, hdl:11529/10548395, hdl:11529/10548396, hdl:11529/10548394, hdl:11529/10548534, hdl:11529/10548401).

Gabon.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and climate science capacity-building** in **Gabon** through **bioeconomic modeling, adaptation strategies, and negotiator training**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s maize research (likely CIMMYT or CIAT)** modeled biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production, identifying region-specific yield reductions and adaptation measures for sub-Saharan Africa, including Gabon’s agroecological zones (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
- **CGIAR’s AGNES program** strengthened African climate negotiators’ technical capacity in attribution science, linking greenhouse gas emissions to climate extremes—a critical tool for Gabon’s climate policy discussions (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443)).
Gabon.adaptation: • **CGIAR’s maize research (CIMMYT/CIAT)** assessed climate change impacts on maize yields in Gabon’s maize mega-environments (MMEs), quantifying yield reductions under varying nitrogen inputs and proposing adaptation strategies for medium/high-input systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
Gabon.mitigation: • **CGIAR’s AGNES program** developed training modules to enhance Gabon’s climate negotiators’ expertise in **attribution science**, linking industrialized nations’ emissions to climate extremes—a foundational step for evidence-based mitigation strategies (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443)).

Japan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural adaptation and mitigation strategies in Japan** through targeted research on crop resilience, socio-ecological systems, and greenhouse gas reduction in rice production. Specifically:
- **Adaptation:** CGIAR advanced **genetic resistance in rice** and **irrigation technologies** to bolster climate resilience (Source A).
- **Mitigation:** CGIAR led **GHG reduction strategies for rice farming** and **climate-smart agriculture training** to align food security with climate goals (Source C, E, F).
- **Socio-Ecological Systems:** CGIAR analyzed **traditional landscapes (satoyama)** to inform **biodiversity conservation and ecosystem service trade-offs** (Source H).
Japan.adaptation:
• CGIAR modeled **climate-adaptive strategies**—including **new crop varieties** and **irrigation technology upgrades**—to enhance agricultural resilience using the IMPACT model (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).
• CGIAR **mapped and pyramided BPH-resistance genes (BPH25, BPH26)** in rice to counter virulent pest strains, demonstrating **durable resistance** through genetic stacking (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/23611).
• CGIAR supported **APEC economies (including Japan)** in developing a **Multi-Year Action Plan for Climate Change and Food Security (2018–2021)** via stakeholder consultations (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121197).
• CGIAR contributed to **vulnerability assessments and adaptation responses** in Asia, including high-resolution climate projections for **agricultural planning** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/34939).
• CGIAR studied **satoyama landscapes** to integrate **traditional ecological knowledge** into **sustainable management**, addressing trade-offs between **biodiversity, cultural heritage, and ecosystem services** (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34725/DVN/M2OEZT).
Japan.mitigation:
• CGIAR led the **CCAC Paddy Rice Component**, documenting **GHG mitigation challenges and opportunities** in rice systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/100227).
• CGIAR presented the **rice–food security–climate change nexus** and the **IRRI Climate Change Mitigation Strategy** at Japan’s National Agriculture and Food Research Organization (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/125979).
• CGIAR facilitated **Climate-Smart Agriculture training** in Tokyo, focusing on **mitigation options for agricultural GHG reduction** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97790).

Panama.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, biodiversity conservation, and policy innovation** in **Mesoamerica (including Panama)** through **spatially explicit modeling, agroforestry resilience, and participatory climate risk management**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** developed **climate-smart food systems (CSFS) frameworks** to scale mitigation and resilient practices in agroforestry (e.g., cocoa/coffee) and silvipastoral systems, addressing emissions measurement and carbon markets (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116921](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
- **Bioversity/CIAT** assessed **vulnerability of cocoa and agroforestry trees** to climate change using **Species Distribution Modeling (SDM)** to inform agroforestry transitions in Panama-Mexico (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/0O1GW1](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
