- **CCAFS** implemented **Local Technical Agroclimatic Committees (MTAs)** as participatory platforms for climate risk dialogue, reducing vulnerability in Latin American farming communities (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/114943](https://doi.org/10.1002/ece3.672)).
Panama.adaptation:
• **Bioversity/CIAT** evaluated **biological corridors (BCs) as adaptation tools** for plant dispersal in fragmented Mesoamerican landscapes, identifying high-altitude corridors as critical for species migration under climate change (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1002/ece3.672](https://doi.org/10.1002/ece3.672)).

• **CIAT/CCAFS** assessed **climate suitability for cocoa/coffee agroforestry** using **ensemble SDM**, revealing only fast-dispersing plant functional types (PFTs) could adapt to warming in Panama’s agroecosystems (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/0O1GW1](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).

• **CCAFS** scaled **Climate Risk Management (CRM) strategies** across 11 Latin American countries (including Panama), reaching **500,000+ farmers** via climate services and governance structures for rural resilience (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121217](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).

• **CIAT** facilitated **Local Technical Agroclimatic Committees (MTAs)** to foster **multi-stakeholder dialogue** on climate variability risks, with gender/social inclusion considerations (Sources: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/114943](https://doi.org/10.1002/ece3.672), [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108780](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).

• **CCAFS** evaluated the **Sustainable Climate-Adapted Agriculture Strategy (EASAC)** in the **SICA region**, including Panama, to synthesize climate-resilient farming practices (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/119642](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).

• **IITA/CGIAR** analyzed **banana corm transcriptomics** to identify **Fusarium-resistant genes** in Panama-relevant varieties (e.g., *Pahang*), enabling disease-resilient crop breeding (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/W2M6WG](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
Panama.mitigation:
• **CIAT/CCAFS** researched **greenhouse gas mitigation in forests** and **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) practices**, highlighting gaps in policy integration for rural development (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).

• **CIAT** integrated **mitigation considerations** into **Local Technical Agroclimatic Committees (MTAs)**, including **emissions monitoring, carbon markets, and MRV tools** for CSA scaling (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/114943](https://doi.org/10.1002/ece3.672)).

• **CCAFS** developed a **Climate-Smart Food Systems (CSFS) framework** to identify **high-impact business models** (e.g., agroforestry, regenerative livestock) with **carbon market potential** in Latin America (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116921](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).

Russia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agricultural adaptation and food security** in **Russia** through scenario modeling, risk assessment, and stakeholder-driven policy support. Specifically:
- **CGIAR (IFPRI/CCAFS)** analyzed climate-induced risks (e.g., drought, wildfires, floods) to Russian agriculture and their implications for food security by 2050, emphasizing the need for farmer adaptation (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/33400**).
- **CGIAR (IFPRI/CCAFS)** simulated regional climate impacts (e.g., expanded growing seasons in the north vs. water scarcity in the south) and proposed adaptive measures for crop and livestock systems (Sources: **https://doi.org/10.5018/economics-ejournal.ja.2013-39**, **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/152724**).
Russia.adaptation:
• **CGIAR (IFPRI)** conducted long-term scenario analyses (2050) to evaluate how climate change—including extreme heat, drought, and floods—threatens Russian agricultural productivity and food security, highlighting challenges for farmer adaptation and risk management (Sources: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/33400**, **https://doi.org/10.2499/9780896291874**).
• **CGIAR (CCAFS)** supported the **APEC Multi-Year Action Plan (2018–2021)** on climate change and food security through stakeholder consultations and policy input, though this was **global/regional** rather than Russia-specific (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121197**).
Russia.water: • **CGIAR (IFPRI)** modeled climate-induced **water scarcity** in southern Russia, linking drought risks to reduced crop yields and livestock productivity, and proposed adaptive water-management strategies (Sources: **https://doi.org/10.5018/economics-ejournal.ja.2013-39**, **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/152724**).

Finland.narrative:
CGIAR’s research in **Finland** (via aligned international programs) focused on **climate resilience and rural development**, with no direct evidence of water-specific interventions in this sample. Specifically:
- **Bioversity International** (under CGIAR’s forestry and genetic resources portfolio) surveyed European forest managers to assess perceptions of **forest genetic resource (FGR) management** as an adaptation strategy for climate change, identifying barriers to adopting non-local genotypes for resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).
- **CGIAR’s modeling teams** (e.g., via IMPACT) analyzed **agricultural adaptation strategies**—such as developing climate-resilient crop varieties and improving irrigation technologies—to mitigate climate risks in Finland-aligned OECD contexts (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
Finland.adaptation:
• **Bioversity International** conducted a pan-European survey (including Finland-aligned priorities) to evaluate forest managers’ attitudes toward **active forest genetic resource (FGR) adaptation**, revealing preferences for local over non-local planting material despite recognition of its climate-resilience potential (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).

• **CGIAR’s agricultural modeling experts** used the **IMPACT framework** to simulate the efficacy of **crop variety R&D and irrigation upgrades** as adaptation measures for Finland’s climate-aligned agricultural systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
Finland.mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s Biocarbon and Rural Development Program (BIODEV)**—aligned with Finland’s development priorities—researched **biocarbon approaches** to link **climate mitigation and adaptation** in West Africa, with findings intended to inform global scaling (Sources: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34725/DVN/ZDW61D](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34725/DVN/ZDW61D), [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34725/DVN/K44TXF](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34725/DVN/K44TXF), [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34725/DVN/BWGWTR](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34725/DVN/BWGWTR), [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34725/DVN/DZQ3AQ](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34725/DVN/DZQ3AQ), [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34725/DVN/LNYOZP](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34725/DVN/LNYOZP), [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34725/DVN/WGEFIB](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34725/DVN/WGEFIB)).

• The program emphasized **GHG reduction via biocarbon systems** and institutional capacity-building to bridge mitigation-adaptation gaps, though no Finland-specific fieldwork was documented (Sources: *same as above*).

Dominican Republic.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and policy integration** in the **Dominican Republic** through **science-policy frameworks, climate exposure mapping, and resilient production systems**. Specifically:
- **CIAT-CCAFS** developed climate exposure maps and climate gradients for cocoa and coffee, enabling site-specific CSA recommendations (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123192).
- **CIAT-CCAFS** supported the formulation of a **regional CSA strategy** for Central America and the Dominican Republic, integrating adaptation and mitigation into public policy (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116170).
Dominican Republic.adaptation:
• **CIAT-CCAFS** developed an **Atlas of Climate Change Impacts on cocoa** in Central America and the Caribbean, including agro-climatic zones and climate-smart practices to improve farm-level resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103775).
• **CIAT-CCAFS** identified **climate-smart cocoa value chains** and presented strategies to minimize climate risks for the cocoa sector in the Dominican Republic (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/79873).
• **CIAT-CCAFS** advanced the **SICA regional CSA strategy (2018–2030)**, using future scenarios methodology to ensure policy robustness despite climate variability (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121429).
• **CIAT-CCAFS** documented **site-specific CSA practices** for coffee and cocoa, aligned with climate exposure maps, and facilitated adoption by industry partners (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123193).
• **CIAT-CCAFS** leveraged **Climate-Smart Food Systems (CSFS)** business models in Latin America, including agroforestry and silvipastoral systems, to enhance productivity and resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116921).
Dominican Republic.mitigation:
• **CGIAR researchers** analyzed **greenhouse gas mitigation from forests** and **climate-smart agriculture** as key policy levers in Latin America, including the Dominican Republic (10.3390/su9101831).
• **CIAT-CCAFS** supported **emissions measurement, reporting, and verification (MRV)** and access to carbon markets for CSFS companies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116921).

Solomon Islands.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate change adaptation and resilience in Solomon Islands** through participatory action research and economic modeling to safeguard food security and livelihoods. Specifically:
- The Center developed **community-based adaptation assessments** to guide local decision-making for resilient fishing and farming (Sources: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69488, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69490, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69493, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69494, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69495).
- Researchers modeled **economic impacts of climate adaptation strategies** (e.g., fish aggregating devices, aquaculture) to project food security risks and welfare gains by 2050 (Sources: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2016.01.004, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.011).
Solomon Islands.adaptation:
• CGIAR **assessed economic impacts of climate change on fisheries** in Solomon Islands, projecting per capita fish consumption declines by 2050 without adaptation, and modeled **low-cost inshore FADs** as a viable strategy to augment tuna supplies (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2016.01.004).

• The Center **developed a participatory adaptation framework** for coastal communities, including **Landscape Function Analysis** and **ecosystem services mapping**, to evaluate climate resilience in fishing and farming livelihoods (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69494, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69495).

• Researchers **documented climate vulnerability** (e.g., tropical cyclones, droughts) and **designed community visioning workshops** to identify adaptation actions for fishers and farmers (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69490, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69493).

• CGIAR **analyzed fishing patterns and trophic signatures** in Malaita Province to inform **community-based resource management**, linking local environmental features to sustainable livelihood strategies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SFZNWM).

• The Center **highlighted limited adaptive capacity** in Solomon Islands due to high dependence on agriculture (68% employment, 39% GDP) and **proposed rapid, qualitative assessment methods** for resource-constrained settings (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/145066, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75610).

France.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and adaptive resilience** in **France** as a global hub for policy and research, particularly during COP21 (2015). Specifically:
- CGIAR led the **Climate-Smart Agriculture Investment Plan (CSAIP) framework**, aligning with the **Paris Agreement** to integrate adaptation and mitigation in agricultural systems (Sources: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106262](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6), [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106259](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
- The **Montpellier Statement (2015)** mobilized global stakeholders to advance CSA as a **triple-pillar solution** (food security, adaptation, mitigation) through policy and field innovations (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/67193](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature.2015.17112)).
France.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Change Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** surveyed **European forest managers** to assess perceptions of **forest genetic resources (FGR) adaptation**, revealing demand for non-local genotypes to match future climate conditions while prioritizing local resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).

• **CGIAR modeled agricultural adaptation strategies** using **IMPACT**, quantifying the efficacy of **new crop varieties** and **irrigation technology** to offset climate risks in OECD contexts (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).

• **CGIAR’s CSAIP framework** was **formalized in France (COP21)**, linking global climate goals to **national agricultural investment plans (NAIPs)** and **climate-smart villages** to enhance resilience (Sources: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106262](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6), [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106259](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).

• **CGIAR’s East Africa Quarterly Newsletter (2015)** highlighted **climate-smart livestock innovations** (e.g., goat/sheep productivity) and **indigenous knowledge integration**, aligning with COP21’s global agenda (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/68858](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature.2015.17112)).

• **CGIAR’s climate-smart villages (CSVs)** demonstrated **60% reductions in hunger** in East African test sites, with findings presented at the **Montpellier CSA Conference (2015)** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/nature.2015.17112](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature.2015.17112)).
France.mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s Green Growth review** included **France’s national policies**, emphasizing **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) investments** as critical for **greenhouse gas (GHG) reductions** and **sustainable development** (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/33278](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).

• **The Montpellier Statement (2015)** framed **CSA as a mitigation tool**, uniting researchers and policymakers to reduce agricultural GHG emissions while securing food systems (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/67193](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature.2015.17112)).

• **CGIAR’s CSAIP framework** explicitly tied **adaptation and mitigation** to **Paris Agreement goals**, advocating for **low-emission agricultural practices** in investment planning (Sources: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106262](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6), [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106259](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).

New Zealand.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and low-emission development** in **New Zealand** through **policy integration, research partnerships, and greenhouse gas (GHG) mitigation strategies**. Specifically:
- The Center advanced **climate-resilient agricultural systems** by removing subsidies and fostering **public-private R&D partnerships** to enhance efficiency and resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
- CGIAR’s **New Zealand Agricultural Greenhouse Gas Research Centre (NZAGRC)** led global research on **ruminant GHG emissions**, collaborating with CLIFF-GRADS alumni to strengthen international climate action (Sources: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/109873](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/109873), [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110095](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110095)).
New Zealand.adaptation:
• CGIAR modeled **adaptation strategies** (e.g., **new crop varieties and irrigation tech**) to mitigate climate risks in agriculture using the **IMPACT model**, estimating public/private R&D investment needs (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
• The Center hosted **Adaptation Futures 2025** in Ōtautahi Christchurch, advancing **locally led, equitable adaptation** in food systems through partnerships with African and Asian stakeholders (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/177760](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/177760)).
New Zealand.mitigation:
• CGIAR’s **NZAGRC** researched **low-emission dairy and ruminant systems**, contributing to global **GHG reduction strategies** (Sources: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/109873](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/109873), [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110095](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110095)).
• The Center facilitated **international climate action** by presenting **dairy sector GHG mitigation options** at global workshops (e.g., China’s low-emission dairy pilot) (Sources: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111194](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111194), [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110571](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110571)).
• CGIAR advanced **agricultural climate action** through partnerships with the **Global Research Alliance on Agricultural GHGs**, linking New Zealand’s expertise to East African livestock emissions reduction efforts (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121310](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121310)).

Chad.narrative: CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptation** in **Chad** through evidence-based policy assessments, climate-smart agriculture (CSA) implementation, and integrated food security monitoring. Specifically:
Chad.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) West Africa** assessed climate-smartness of the **West Africa Agricultural Productivity Programme (WAAPP)** in Chad, strengthening stakeholder capacity to mainstream CSA through the **CaSCIERA-WA** project (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106455).

• **CGIAR researchers** synthesized climate change impacts, adaptation strategies, and policy frameworks for **crop, livestock, and fishery sectors** in West Africa, including Chad, to improve resilience of farming systems (https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3).

• **CGIAR’s Sahel Adaptive Social Protection Programme** analyzed alignment between **social protection and climate resilience** in Chad, identifying opportunities to enhance adaptive capacities of vulnerable households through existing schemes (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/135122).

• **CGIAR developed an integrated dataset** on **food security and household coping strategies** in Chad (2018–2023), linking **climate hazards (e.g., extreme weather, rainy season performance)** to vulnerability and resilience at the administrative level (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SQA143).

• **CGIAR’s AGNES initiative** conducted capacity-building trainings for African negotiators on **attribution science**, equipping Chad’s representatives with tools to link **greenhouse gas emissions to climate extremes** and inform climate negotiations (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443).

• **CGIAR’s policy briefs** examined how **climate shocks (droughts, floods) and conflict** disrupt education systems in Chad, proposing climate-resilient education frameworks to build long-term adaptation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179801).

• **CGIAR’s maize systems research** (via CCAFS) modeled **biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production** in sub-Saharan Africa, including Chad, to inform adaptation measures for food security (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
Chad.mitigation: • **CGIAR’s AGNES initiative** advanced **attribution science** to strengthen Chad’s capacity in **climate change mitigation negotiations**, focusing on the role of greenhouse gas emissions in driving climate extremes (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443).

Papua New Guinea.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient food systems and gender-inclusive adaptation** in **Papua New Guinea (PNG)** through transdisciplinary research, household surveys, and climate-smart agricultural interventions. Specifically:
- CGIAR’s **Food Systems and Climate Adaptation Research** assessed gender disparities in access to rural climate services, identifying barriers like ICT limitations and socio-cultural norms to inform inclusive policy design (Source: [Adaptation]).
- The **International Food Policy Research Institute (IFPRI)** conducted large-scale rural household surveys (2018, 2023) to map agricultural productivity, food security, and socio-economic vulnerabilities across PNG’s agro-ecological zones, linking findings to child nutrition and climate resilience (Sources: [Adaptation]).
- CGIAR’s **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) programs** modeled crop resilience (e.g., taro) under projected climate variability, recommending drought- and flood-tolerant varieties to mitigate yield losses in high-precipitation regions (Source: [Water, Adaptation]).
Papua New Guinea.adaptation:
• **IFPRI (CGIAR Center)** led a **2018 Rural Household Survey** across East Sepik, Madang, and Bougainville to analyze food systems, gender roles, and climate vulnerability, informing programs like World Vision’s agricultural productivity initiatives (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ZXRD6N).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Adaptation Research** reviewed gender-responsive climate services, highlighting women’s limited access to ICTs and group processes, and proposed mixed-gender networks and tailored communication tools to enhance adaptation (https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216).

• **IFPRI expanded its 2023 Rural Household Survey** to 14 PNG provinces, linking resilient local food systems to diversified livelihoods and socio-economic outcomes, with modules on economic shocks and dietary quality (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BYZMZ6).

• **CGIAR’s Pacific Food Systems Research** projected climate impacts (e.g., cyclones, droughts) on fisheries and agriculture, developing policy scenarios to strengthen resilience among farmers and fishers (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75610).

• **Climate Resilience Studies** emphasized PNG’s limited adaptive capacity due to high agricultural dependence (69% employment), recommending climate-smart practices to counter extreme weather risks (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/145066).
Papua New Guinea.water: • **CGIAR’s Crop Modeling Research** analyzed historical and projected precipitation trends in PNG (increasing rainfall since 1995), using DSSAT simulations to assess taro yield reductions (up to 10% in Southern regions) and recommending climate-resilient crop varieties (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/173654).

Iraq.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient development and water management** in **Iraq** through evidence-based assessments and cross-border collaboration. Specifically:
- The Center advanced **integrated climate-peace-security programming** to address food/water scarcity and inequality (Source 1).
- Researchers mapped **agricultural water productivity** across irrigated/rainfed systems in the **Euphrates-Tigris Basin** to identify efficiency gaps (Sources 2–9).
Iraq.adaptation: • CGIAR developed **integrated climate-peace-security frameworks** to mitigate conflict risks from climate-induced food/water scarcity, emphasizing resilience in Iraq’s socio-economic systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/132837).
Iraq.water:
• CGIAR’s **Supporting Coordination and Cooperation in Water Management in the Euphrates-Tigris Area** project quantified **agricultural water productivity (WPM)** for irrigated (2002, 2006, 2008) and rainfed areas (2002, 2006, 2008) to benchmark efficiency under varying precipitation conditions (hdl:20.500.11766.1/CUZ56W, hdl:20.500.11766.1/D1D3GI, hdl:20.500.11766.1/EWB8JR, hdl:20.500.11766.1/L9USGL, hdl:20.500.11766.1/LDJRMX, hdl:20.500.11766.1/NLMS9P).
• Researchers compiled **agricultural statistics** (crop yield, water sources, irrigation types) for Iraq’s major crops under different water management regimes (2002–2008) to support transboundary policy (hdl:20.500.11766.1/WWZNPN).

South Sudan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptive strategies** in **South Sudan and East Africa** through evidence-based interventions. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s research** assessed climate change impacts on maize production and food security, identifying region-specific yield reductions and adaptation measures (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
- **CGIAR’s climate services** strengthened institutional linkages and capacity building for seasonal forecasting in agriculture and food security sectors (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116941](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116941)).
South Sudan.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s maize research** quantified biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize yields in South Sudan’s agro-ecological zones, highlighting adaptation needs for medium/high-input systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005) and [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-025-05405-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-025-05405-2) for drought/flood projections).

• **CGIAR’s climate security analysis** developed a methodological framework to assess policy coherence between climate and peace/security sectors, identifying gaps in South Sudan’s climate-related security risk mitigation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3828/idpr.2023.9](https://doi.org/10.3828/idpr.2023.9)).

• **CGIAR’s migration-climate nexus research** linked climate extremes (e.g., droughts) to increased youth outmigration in South Sudan, emphasizing livelihood vulnerabilities (Sources: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110278](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110278) and [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106796](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106796)).

• **CGIAR’s situational analysis** in the Karamoja Cluster (bordering South Sudan) identified climate hazards (droughts, floods, heat stress) and invasive species threats, recommending targeted resilience interventions (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/169628](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/169628)).

• **CGIAR’s capacity-building workshops** enhanced seasonal forecasting and climate information services for agriculture/food security in South Sudan’s regional context (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116941](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116941)).

• **CGIAR’s livestock research** supported climate-adaptive pastoralism strategies in IGAD (including South Sudan) through regional partnerships (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128053](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128053)).

• **CGIAR’s Regional Climate Action Plan** (2023–2028) integrated climate adaptation measures for displaced populations in South Sudan, aligning with IGAD’s climate frameworks (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/137370](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/137370)).
South Sudan.mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s attribution science training** strengthened African negotiators’ capacity to link greenhouse gas emissions to climate extremes, supporting evidence-based mitigation advocacy (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443)).

• **CGIAR’s situational analysis** in Karamoja highlighted sustainable resource management as a mitigation strategy for invasive species and locust threats (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/169628](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/169628)).
South Sudan.water: • **CGIAR’s climate projections** analyzed water stress (drought/flood indicators) in East Africa, noting heterogeneous shifts toward wetter zones in northern Somalia/South Sudan (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-025-05405-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-025-05405-2)).

Azerbaijan.narrative:
CGIAR’s Climate Impact Platform and GENDER Impact Platform advanced climate resilience and gender-inclusive solutions in Azerbaijan through strategic COP29 engagement and capacity-building initiatives. Specifically:
- The **Climate Impact Platform** coordinated CGIAR’s participation at COP29 in Baku, aligning agricultural innovation with global climate goals through stakeholder convening, evidence-based advocacy, and high-profile events (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/169582**).
- The **GENDER Impact Platform** collaborated with MESHA to amplify climate solutions from Africa through gender-sensitive journalism training, focusing on nature-based solutions, renewable energy transitions, and climate finance (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/174717**).
Azerbaijan.adaptation:
• CGIAR’s **Climate Impact Platform** enhanced climate resilience by strategically coordinating CGIAR’s participation at COP29 in Baku, ensuring alignment with global adaptation goals through stakeholder engagement and evidence-based advocacy (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/169582**).
• The **Climate Impact Platform** and **GENDER Impact Platform** promoted climate-smart agriculture and nature-based solutions via gender-inclusive journalism training, addressing energy poverty and locally led climate adaptation (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/174717**).
• CGIAR contributed to COP29 negotiations on the **New Collective Quantified Goal (NCQG)**, advocating for climate finance mechanisms to support sustainable agroecological food systems and adaptation strategies (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179970**).
Azerbaijan.mitigation:
• CGIAR’s **Climate Impact Platform** engaged in COP29 discussions to **limit global warming**, mobilizing financing for mitigation efforts under the Paris Agreement (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/172620**).
• The Platform actively participated in negotiations for the **NCQG**, pushing for climate finance allocations to mitigate emissions in agrifood systems while ensuring food security (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179970**).

Korea.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture and food system resilience** in **Korea** through policy analysis, adaptation strategies, and international collaboration. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** supported APEC economies—including Korea—in developing a **Multi-Year Action Plan for Climate Change and Food Security (2018–2021)**, facilitating stakeholder consultations and policy alignment (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121197).
- **CGIAR researchers** modeled **climate change adaptation strategies** for Korea using the **IMPACT model**, projecting food security outcomes under climate shocks and assessing the adoption of climate-resilient agricultural practices (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/146482).
Korea.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** facilitated the **APEC Multi-Year Action Plan (2018–2021)** for climate change and food security in Korea, coordinating stakeholder consultations and policy priorities (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121197).
• **CGIAR analyzed Korea’s climate change adaptation strategies** using the **IMPACT model**, evaluating the impact of climate-smart agricultural practices on food security and domestic production trends (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/146482).
• **CGIAR examined Korea’s extension and technology dissemination programs** as key tools for building **climate-resilient food systems**, highlighting cross-sectoral strategies to counteract climate impacts and improve farmer incomes (https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.133382).
• **CGIAR’s modeling work** assessed **new crop varieties and irrigation technologies** as adaptation measures to enhance agricultural resilience in Korea (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).
Korea.mitigation: • **CGIAR integrated climate-smart agriculture into Korea’s Green Growth strategies**, emphasizing **sustainability and low-emission agricultural practices** as part of broader national policies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/33278).

Timor-Leste.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate change adaptation** in **Timor-Leste** through participatory, community-driven approaches and economic modeling to enhance resilience in fisheries and agriculture. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) and WorldFish** developed **economic modeling tools** to assess the economic costs and benefits of adaptation strategies—such as aquaculture development, marine protected areas, and low-cost fish aggregating devices—to improve coastal and freshwater fish production under climate change (2035–2050) (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.010).
- **CGIAR’s Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** designed a **four-step participatory action research framework** for coastal communities in Timor-Leste, enabling community-level decision-making for adaptation through activities like climate analysis, impact workshops, ecosystem services mapping, and implementation planning (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69488, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69495, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69490, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69494, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69489, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69493).
Timor-Leste.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s WorldFish and CCAFS** modeled **economic impacts of climate change on fisheries** in Timor-Leste, demonstrating that **without adaptation**, domestic fish production would stagnate, leading to increased reliance on fish imports, while **adaptation strategies** (e.g., aquaculture, marine protected areas, fish aggregating devices) could significantly boost production and exports by 2050 (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.010, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.011).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** developed a **community-based adaptation assessment toolkit** for Timor-Leste, guiding local stakeholders through **scoping, climate analysis, impact workshops, ecosystem services mapping, and implementation planning** to build resilient fishing and farming communities (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69488, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69495, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69490, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69494, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69489, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69493).

• **CGIAR’s WorldFish** conducted a **randomized controlled trial** testing **fish aggregating devices (FADs) and social behavior change (SBC) activities** to improve fish consumption and dietary diversity in rural, inland Timor-Leste, addressing malnutrition through enhanced fish supply and demand (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VZJYIZ).

Belgium.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate adaptation in agriculture and forest ecosystems** in Belgium through policy integration and technological innovation. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Environment and Production Technology Division** advocated for the inclusion of agriculture in climate change policies, emphasizing resilience strategies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/132941).
- **Bioversity International** surveyed European forest managers to assess perceptions of forest genetic resources (FGR) as adaptive tools for climate resilience, identifying barriers to adoption of non-local genotypes (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6).
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change and Food Security Program** modeled adaptation strategies (e.g., new crop varieties and irrigation tech) to mitigate climate risks in agriculture, quantifying investment needs via the IMPACT model (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).
- **CIMMYT and Université catholique de Louvain** developed radar-based remote sensing (Sentinel-1A) in Belgium to detect conservation agriculture adoption, achieving 94% accuracy in tillage recognition for resilience monitoring (hdl:11529/10548137).
Belgium.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Environment and Production Technology Division** highlighted the need to integrate agriculture into climate policies, framing adaptation as critical for food security (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/132941).
• **Bioversity International** conducted a pan-European survey (15 countries) to evaluate forest managers’ attitudes toward forest genetic resources (FGR) as adaptive tools, revealing preferences for local sources but openness to non-local genotypes for climate-matching resilience (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6).
• **CGIAR’s Climate Change and Food Security Program** analyzed adaptation strategies (crop variety R&D and irrigation tech upgrades) using the IMPACT model, estimating public/private investment costs to enhance resilience (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).
• **CIMMYT and Université catholique de Louvain** pioneered radar satellite imaging (Sentinel-1A) in Belgium to monitor conservation agriculture adoption, with 94% accuracy in tillage detection for resilience tracking (hdl:11529/10548137).

Sweden.narrative:
CGIAR’s research in **Sweden** focused on **adaptation strategies** for climate-resilient systems, with no direct evidence of water-specific or mitigation-focused studies in this sample. Specifically:
- **Adaptation**: CGIAR explored **sustainability transitions** through bottom-up initiatives (e.g., gender equity, governance) in Northern Europe (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s11625-019-00714-8](https://doi.org/10.1007/s11625-019-00714-8)).
- **Adaptation**: The Center analyzed **forest genetic resource (FGR) management** to enhance climate resilience, surveying European forest owners/managers on adaptive planting strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).
- **Adaptation**: CGIAR modeled **agricultural adaptation** (e.g., new crop varieties, irrigation tech) using IMPACT, estimating R&D investment needs for OECD regions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
Sweden.adaptation:
• CGIAR developed **sustainability scenarios** rooted in bottom-up transformative initiatives (e.g., gender equity, behavioral change) to explore transitions toward positive futures in Northern Europe (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.1007/s11625-019-00714-8).
• The Center surveyed **European forest owners/managers** to assess perceptions of forest genetic resources (FGR) as adaptive tools, identifying preferences for non-local genotypes to match future climate conditions (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6).
• CGIAR modeled **climate-adaptive agricultural strategies** (e.g., R&D for drought-resistant crops, irrigation upgrades) via IMPACT, quantifying investment needs for OECD countries (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).

Central African Republic.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient maize systems and climate negotiation capacity** in the **Central African Republic** through **bioeconomic modeling, adaptation strategies, and negotiator training**. Specifically:
- CGIAR researchers developed **fine-resolution bioeconomic models** to assess maize yield impacts under climate change (2050/2080) and evaluated adaptation measures for improved varieties (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
- The Center strengthened **African climate negotiator capacity** via AGNES-led trainings on attribution science, linking GHG emissions to climate extremes (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443)).
Central African Republic.adaptation: • CGIAR researchers modeled **biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production** (2050/2080) under varying nitrogen levels, identifying region-specific yield reductions and adaptation needs for **Western/Central African maize mega-environments** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
Central African Republic.mitigation: • CGIAR’s **AGNES program** trained African negotiators (including CAR representatives) in **attribution science** to strengthen evidence-based climate negotiations, focusing on **GHG-emission impacts on climate extremes** (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443)).

Italy.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate adaptation in Italy** through participatory and genetic strategies. Specifically:
- **Bioversity International** developed a **Resilient Seed Systems Handbook** to support participatory research and capacity building for climate-adaptive seed systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/73256).
- **CGIAR researchers** surveyed European forest owners/managers to assess perceptions of **forest genetic resource (FGR) management** as an adaptation strategy, identifying barriers to non-local planting material adoption (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6).
Italy.adaptation:
• **Bioversity International** created a **participatory Resilient Seed Systems Handbook** (8 modules) to guide research and capacity-building for climate-adaptive seed production/distribution in Italy (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/73256).
• **CGIAR researchers** analyzed forest owner/manager perceptions of **forest genetic resources (FGR) as adaptation tools**, revealing preferences for local sources but openness to non-local genotypes for climate resilience (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6).
• **CGIAR’s IMPACT model** evaluated **agricultural adaptation strategies** (e.g., new crop varieties, irrigation tech) to mitigate climate risks in OECD regions, including Italy (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).

Uzbekistan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture, water-efficient technologies, and genetic adaptation** in **Uzbekistan** through targeted research on rangeland productivity, irrigation optimization, and stress-tolerant crop varieties. Specifically:
- **ICARDA** assessed climate risks to rangeland biomass and grazing availability, projecting declines in herbaceous biomass and increased variability under RCP 8.5 (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395)).
- **CIAT** evaluated irrigation water use efficiency under climate change, modeling crop-water allocation strategies to mitigate revenue losses in Western Uzbekistan (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2016.05.004](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2016.05.004)).
Uzbekistan.adaptation:
• **CGIAR** (via Bioversity) conducted genetic studies on *Juglans regia* (walnut) populations in Uzbekistan, Tajikistan, and Kyrgyzstan to assess wild and cultivated biodiversity for climate-resilient traits (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VUP7OH](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VUP7OH)).

• **CGIAR** (via CIP) developed early-maturing, virus-resistant, and stress-tolerant crop varieties for subtropical and temperate agroecologies, including Uzbekistan, through multi-environment trials in Peru (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/XLWBWM](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/XLWBWM)).

• **CGIAR** analyzed climate finance for adaptation in Uzbekistan (2010–2012), highlighting investments in resilient agricultural practices (USD 155–314 million) and low-emissions development (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/68686](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/68686)).

• **CGIAR** simulated climate impacts on agricultural productivity in Uzbekistan (Karshi site) using long-term weather data (1936–2018) to assess adaptation technologies (Source: [hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/FEQGGW](hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/FEQGGW)).

• **CGIAR** studied grazed vegetation responses in Uzbekistan’s Kyzylkum Desert rangelands, using grazing gradients to quantify livestock-induced vegetation changes (Source: [hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/L7XSN8](hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/L7XSN8)).

• **CGIAR** evaluated integrated soil-land-crop-water management technologies in Uzbekistan through household surveys on chickpea production (Source: [hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/E26O2A](hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/E26O2A)).

• **CGIAR** modeled crop productivity in irrigated areas of Uzbekistan (1951–2050) to inform climate-adaptive irrigation strategies (Source: [hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/HMKJR8](hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/HMKJR8)).

• **CGIAR** examined cooperative adaptation strategies for transboundary water management in the Syr Darya basin, focusing on droughts and flash floods in Uzbekistan and Tajikistan (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/34723](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/34723)).
Uzbekistan.mitigation: • **CGIAR** analyzed climate finance trends (2010–2012) in Uzbekistan, identifying carbon-market investments in N₂O emission reductions and low-emissions agricultural practices (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/68686](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/68686)).
Uzbekistan.water:
• **CGIAR** assessed irrigation water use efficiency in Western Uzbekistan under climate change, using stochastic models to optimize crop-water allocation and address shallow groundwater challenges (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2016.05.004](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2016.05.004)).

• **CGIAR** evaluated water scarcity and flash flood risks in transboundary tributaries (e.g., Khojabakirgansai) through community-level cooperation studies in Uzbekistan and Tajikistan (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/34723](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/34723)).

• **CGIAR** simulated crop productivity in irrigated areas of Uzbekistan using climatic and soil-crop data (1951–2050) to inform water management (Source: [hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/HMKJR8](hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/HMKJR8)).

Paraguay.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, silvopastoral systems, and resilient food systems** in **Paraguay** through **multi-stakeholder dialogue, policy integration, and scalable business models**. Specifically:
- **CIAT-CCAFS** developed **Local Technical Agroclimatic Committees (MTAs)** to foster climate risk dialogue and adaptive practices (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/114943).
- **CCAFS** scaled **Climate Risk Management (CRM) strategies**, reaching **500,000+ farmers** across Latin America, including Paraguay, via climate services and governance frameworks (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121217).
Paraguay.adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** implemented **Local Technical Agroclimatic Committees (MTAs)** to reduce climate variability risks through farmer-scientist dialogue and context-specific recommendations (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108780, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/114943).
• **CCAFS** supported **disaster risk management** in Paraguay by integrating MTAs into the **Regional Strategy for Agriculture and Food Security**, aligning with national capacities (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121200).
• **CIAT-CCAFS** leveraged **Climate-Smart Food Systems (CSFS)** to scale **resilient practices** (e.g., agroforestry, regenerative livestock) and **productivity-enhancing adaptations** in Paraguay’s food systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116921).
Paraguay.mitigation:
• **CGIAR researchers** highlighted **silvopastoral systems (SPS)** as a mitigation strategy in Paraguay, demonstrating their potential to **reduce GHG emissions (CH₄, N₂O)**, **increase carbon stocks**, and contribute to **Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs)** (https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2).
• **CIAT-CCAFS** developed frameworks to **measure, report, and verify (MRV) emissions** for CSFS companies, including **carbon market access** and **technical guidance** for SPS adoption (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116921).
• **CGIAR** identified **policy gaps** in Paraguay’s mitigation efforts, emphasizing the need for **cross-sectoral approaches** (e.g., forestry, agriculture) to align with NDC targets (https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831).

Lesotho.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and adaptive resilience** in **Lesotho** through **sustainable productivity, resource management, and participatory climate services**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate-Smart Agriculture program** advanced CSA pillars (productivity, adaptation, mitigation) to enhance food security and reduce GHG emissions (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97084](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
- **CGIAR’s Southern Africa research** analyzed climate change impacts on maize systems, identifying yield reductions and adaptation strategies for Lesotho’s agroecological zones (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
Lesotho.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate-Smart Agriculture program** designed resilience-building strategies in Lesotho, integrating **conservation agriculture (minimum tillage, crop rotations, soil cover)** to sustain natural resources and smallholder livelihoods (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34725/DVN/1YJTEC](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34725/DVN/1YJTEC)).
• **CGIAR’s PICSA (Participatory Integrated Climate Services for Agriculture)** scaled climate-responsive methodologies to Lesotho, reaching farmers through innovation and institutional partnerships (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121373](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121373)).
• **CGIAR’s Southern Africa analysis** highlighted Lesotho’s vulnerability to rising temperatures and shifting precipitation, emphasizing **adaptation as critical for poverty alleviation** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.2499/9780896292086](https://doi.org/10.2499/9780896292086)).
• **CGIAR’s maize research** modeled **adaptive measures** (e.g., improved varieties, input optimization) to mitigate yield losses in Lesotho’s maize mega-environments (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
Lesotho.mitigation: • **CGIAR’s Climate-Smart Agriculture program** framed Lesotho’s agricultural strategies to **reduce/remove GHGs** while balancing productivity and adaptation trade-offs (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97084](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97084)).
Lesotho.water: • **CGIAR’s ICRISAT researchers** evaluated **nitrogen-use efficiency (NUE) in sorghum** under variable nitrogen regimes (0%, 50%, 100% of recommended dose), including Lesotho-derived genotypes, to optimize resource use (Sources: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21421/D2/WCAHRK](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21421/D2/WCAHRK), [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21421/D2/XTDY2R](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21421/D2/XTDY2R)).

Jordan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience, water security, and socio-economic adaptation** in **Jordan** through evidence-based policy recommendations, nature-based solutions, and livelihood interventions. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s FCM Initiative** advanced climate-resilient livelihoods for refugees and host communities (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/170241**).
- **The Center** integrated climate vulnerability assessments and GIS mapping to strengthen social protection programs (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/135118**).
- **Al Murunah Project** (led by CGIAR partners) developed high-resolution climate downscaling for resilient nature-based water solutions in Wadi Seer (Jordan) (Source: **https://doi.org/10.5337/2025.208**).
Jordan.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s FCM Initiative** designed financing mechanisms to enable refugees and host communities in Jordan to adopt climate-resilient livelihoods, addressing food and water scarcity risks (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/170241**).

• **The Center** identified barriers to adaptive social protection in Jordan—such as siloed approaches and lack of community engagement—and recommended climate vulnerability assessments, shock-responsive programming, and training in climate-resilient agricultural techniques to build long-term resilience (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/135118**).

• **CGIAR’s Climate, Peace, and Security programming** analyzed how climate-induced food/water scarcity exacerbates inequalities and security risks in Jordan, proposing integrated policies to mitigate conflict over dwindling resources (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/132837**).

• **The Alliance** advanced the **Climate, Peace, and Resilience Nexus** in the MENA region, including Jordan, through research and policy advocacy (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179790**).
Jordan.water:
• **CGIAR’s Al Murunah Project** (in collaboration with regional partners) generated high-resolution climate data to inform **resilient nature-based water solutions** in Jordan’s Wadi Seer basin, addressing droughts and extreme weather through proactive water management strategies (Source: **https://doi.org/10.5337/2025.208**).

• **The Center** quantified **water footprints** of Jordanian irrigated agriculture (fruits/vegetables, 2009–2019), providing data to optimize water use in high-demand sectors (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/CGXT8H**).

• **CGIAR researchers** analyzed climate threats to Jordan’s **groundwater/aquifer reserves**, highlighting evapotranspiration and reduced rainfall as critical stressors, and proposed policy integration of climate-security frameworks to mitigate water-related risks (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/132897**).

• **Socio-economic Studies Directorate (NARC-CGIAR)** surveyed farmers’ attitudes toward **treated sludge (biosolids) as fertilizer**, assessing soil fertility practices in Jordan’s Badia region to improve sustainable water-use efficiency (Source: **hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/F7QQYB**).

Lebanon.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and sustainable agricultural systems** in **Lebanon** through **data-driven field experiments and nature-based water solutions**. Specifically:
- **Bioversity International** (under DIVERSify) advanced **crop resilience** via multi-year field experiments on wheat and faba bean systems (Sources: *Kafardan 2018*, *Kafardan 2019*, *Tal Amara 2020*).
- **ICARDA** (via Al Murunah) integrated **high-resolution climate downscaling** to design **resilient nature-based water solutions** for the Ras Baalbeck Basin (Source: *Al Murunah Webinar*).
Lebanon.adaptation: • **Bioversity International** conducted **multi-location field experiments (2018–2020)** to evaluate **diverse wheat-faba bean cropping systems** for climate resilience in Kafardan and Tal Amara, Lebanon (Sources: *hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/MHOHHL*, *hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/O5YFWX*, *hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/HTBOKU*).
Lebanon.water: • **ICARDA** (Al Murunah Project) developed **high-resolution climate downscaling models** to inform **nature-based water management strategies** for drought and heatwave resilience in the **Ras Baalbeck Basin**, Lebanon (Source: *https://doi.org/10.5337/2025.208*).

Qatar.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and policy synthesis** in **Qatar** through **multi-stakeholder dialogues and thematic expertise**. Specifically:
- The **CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** and **ICARDA** synthesized **risk management strategies for dryland agriculture** to address water scarcity and climate variability (Source **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/24913**).
- CGIAR researchers contributed to **high-level policy discussions** on adaptation, food security, and REDD+ at COP18 (Sources **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/74955**, **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/74852**, **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/74853**).
Qatar.adaptation:
• **CGIAR (CCAFS, ICARDA, Dryland Systems)** facilitated the **Doha Declaration** to reduce climate risks in dryland agriculture, emphasizing **water-efficient productivity** and policy recommendations for governments (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/24913).
• **CGIAR researchers** presented **scalable solutions for climate-resilient agriculture** at the **Agriculture, Landscapes and Livelihoods Day**, highlighting gaps in adaptation strategies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/74853).
• **CCAFS** documented **national adaptation programs in West Africa** (Burkina Faso, Mali, Niger) as case studies for climate-vulnerable regions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/74865).
• **CGIAR experts** underscored **agriculture’s role as a critical adaptation factor** in climate negotiations (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/74870).
Qatar.mitigation: • **CGIAR (via IUFRO collaboration)** advanced **REDD+ forest management** discussions at **All Forest Day, COP18**, linking **biodiversity conservation** and **carbon sequestration** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/74955).

Malaysia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart adaptation** in **Malaysia** through transdisciplinary networks, resilient agrifood systems, and policy-aligned research. Specifically:
- The Center advanced **climate-resilient rice systems** via the Direct Seeded Rice Consortium (DSRC) to enhance adaptation to extreme weather (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035)).
- Researchers developed a **local fish feed database** using 13 sustainable ingredients (e.g., palm kernel cake, rubber seed meal) to support resilient aquaculture in Malaysia (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Y1HVFF](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Y1HVFF)).
Malaysia.adaptation:
• CGIAR facilitated **transdisciplinary networks** (e.g., Direct Seeded Rice Consortium) to strengthen climate-smart agriculture and resilience to extreme weather in Malaysia’s rice systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035) ).

• The Center scaled **agricultural innovations** through conferences (e.g., Hanoi 2018) to embed sustainable change in complex systems, including Malaysia’s regional partnerships (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99099](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99099)).

• Researchers at **WorldFish (Penang, Malaysia)** curated an **ingredient database** for sustainable local fish feeds, integrating 13 ingredients (e.g., banana peel, sugarcane bagasse) to enhance aquaculture resilience (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Y1HVFF](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Y1HVFF)).

• CGIAR supported **Climate-Smart Villages (CSV)** in ASEAN, including Malaysia, by developing an 8-step guide for rural resilience (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123058](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123058)).

• The Center contributed to **ASEAN Regional Guidelines for Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA)**, aligning Malaysia’s practices with regional climate resilience frameworks (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/92487](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/92487)).

• CGIAR conducted **farmer surveys in Sarawak (Malaysia)** to assess perceptions of climate change, crop diversity, and seed systems for resilience, partnering with SIAS (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/XYKYDW](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/XYKYDW)).

• The **CCAFS SEA program** outlined a 2019–2021 research framework to help Malaysia **cope with climate change** and sustain food security through integrated interventions (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99018](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99018)).

• CGIAR supported **APEC economies (including Malaysia)** in adopting a **Multi-Year Action Plan (2018–2021)** for climate change and food security via stakeholder consultations (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121197](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121197)).

Ireland.narrative:
CGIAR’s contributions in **Ireland** focused on **adaptation** through climate-resilient agricultural strategies and gender-inclusive innovation. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** modeled adaptation strategies—such as **new crop varieties and improved irrigation technologies**—to enhance resilience and food security under climate change (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).
- **CCAFS** evaluated **monitoring and evaluation frameworks** to assess how climate data and tools contribute to development outcomes (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/82622).
- **CCAFS** explored **smallholder women’s empowerment** via participatory design of labor-saving tools, linking resilience to gender equity in farming (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/74993).
Ireland.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** used the **IMPACT model** to analyze adaptation strategies, including **R&D for climate-resilient crop varieties** and **irrigation technology upgrades**, estimating investment needs for OECD-aligned interventions (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).
• **CCAFS** developed **evaluative approaches** to track the impact of climate data/tools on development outcomes, balancing IPG production with programmatic results (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/82622).
• **CCAFS** highlighted **gender-responsive innovation** (e.g., farmer-led tool design) to address climate-induced vulnerabilities among smallholder women in Malawi, with Irish-led presentations at CGIAR events (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/74993).

Uruguay.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, silvopastoral systems, and data-driven decision-making** in **Uruguay** through **science-based interventions in livestock, agronomy, and digital tools**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** advanced **climate-smart food systems (CSFS)** via silvopastoral systems (SPS) and regenerative livestock, linking them to **carbon markets and Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs)** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116921).
- **FLAR** enabled **digital decision-support tools** for **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA)** in irrigated rice systems, scaling to **2 million hectares** across Latin America, including Uruguay (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121433).
Uruguay.adaptation:
• **CIAT** promoted **data-driven agronomy** through **Climate-Smart Management Systems (CSMS)** and digital platforms, improving yields for **100,000+ farmers** in Uruguay and Latin America (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121856).
• **CIAT** highlighted **versatile silvopastoral systems (SPS)** as adaptive strategies for **climate-resilient livestock production**, aligning with Uruguay’s NDCs (10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2).
• **CLIFF-GRADS** facilitated **international collaboration** in **pasture and agronomy systems**, fostering research on climate-adaptive forages (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110096).
Uruguay.mitigation:
• **CGIAR researchers** assessed **livestock greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions** in Uruguay, providing **Tier 2 policy guidance** for UNFCCC reporting (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99714).
• **CIAT** demonstrated **SPS potential for carbon sequestration**, positioning them as **key mitigation tools** in Latin American NDCs, including Uruguay (10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2).
• **CGIAR** evaluated **soil organic carbon (SOC) sequestration** as a critical **climate mitigation lever** for agricultural NDCs (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108259).
• **CIAT** developed **MRV (Measurement, Reporting, Verification) frameworks** for emissions in **Climate-Smart Food Systems (CSFS)**, supporting carbon market integration (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116921).
Uruguay.water: • **CGIAR’s CMIP5-based research** projected **increased monsoon precipitation (30% amplitude rise) over southern Brazil, Uruguay, and northern Argentina** by 2045–50, using gridded climate models (10.1175/jcli-d-12-00412.1).

Eritrea.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agricultural adaptation** in **Eritrea** through **bioeconomic modeling, climate-smart agriculture (CSA) frameworks, and regional research-for-development partnerships**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR researchers** calibrated bioeconomic models to assess maize yield reductions under climate change and evaluated adaptation measures for Eastern Africa’s agroecological zones (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
- **ILRI and CGIAR centers** collaborated on climate adaptation strategies for livestock and pastoral systems in the IGAD region, including Eritrea (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128053](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128053)).
Eritrea.adaptation:
• **CGIAR researchers** modeled biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production in sub-Saharan Africa (including Eritrea’s maize mega-environments) and proposed adaptation measures for future climate scenarios (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

• **CGIAR and IFPRI** analyzed climate change vulnerabilities in East Africa, including Eritrea, and developed scenario-based recommendations for food security and resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.2499/9780896292055](https://doi.org/10.2499/9780896292055)).

• **ILRI (CGIAR Center)** led research-for-development partnerships to support climate adaptation in livestock and pastoral systems across the IGAD region, including Eritrea (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128053](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128053)).

• **CGIAR contributed to the East and Horn of Africa Climate Action Plan (2023–2028)**, aligning with IGAD’s regional strategy to integrate climate adaptation measures for displaced populations (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/137370](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/137370)).

• **CGIAR researchers** linked climate extremes (e.g., droughts) to migration risks in East Africa, highlighting youth vulnerability and the need for adaptive livelihood strategies (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110278](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110278)).

• **CGIAR and partners** documented climate change impacts on agricultural production in Eastern Africa, emphasizing risks from extreme events and variability (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106796](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106796)).
Eritrea.mitigation: • **CGIAR researchers** reviewed greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions in Eastern Africa’s agricultural systems, including Eritrea’s livestock production, and promoted climate-smart agriculture (CSA) as a mitigation strategy (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106995](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106995)).

Mauritania.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptive governance** in **Mauritania** through participatory research, scenario-based policy tools, and community-led conservation. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Sahel Adaptive Social Protection Program** aligned social protection with climate resilience to strengthen adaptive capacities of vulnerable households (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/135122).
- **CGIAR researchers** developed participatory approaches to implement the **Pastoral Code**, assessing decentralization conflicts and bottlenecks in pastoral resource management (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/36082).
- **CGIAR’s foresight workshops** (e.g., *Envisioning 2050*) engaged youth and stakeholders to co-design climate-adaptive fisheries and aquaculture scenarios for Mauritania, Senegal, and Ghana (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/34904).
Mauritania.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) program** synthesized climate-smart agriculture strategies for West Africa, including livestock, fisheries, and crop adaptation policies tailored to Mauritania’s farming systems (https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3).

• **CGIAR researchers** evaluated climate change impacts on maize production in sub-Saharan Africa, identifying region-specific adaptation measures for Mauritania’s **maize mega-environments** (MMEs) under varying nitrogen inputs (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).

• **CGIAR’s fisheries team** constructed climate-resilient scenarios for Senegalese and Mauritanian fisheries, linking localized productivity changes to adaptation policy frameworks (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/34905).

• **CGIAR’s participatory research** in Mauritania integrated **water and soil conservation** with livestock management to enhance rangeland resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/36079).

• **CGIAR’s Sahel program** analyzed adaptive social protection systems in Mauritania, highlighting gaps and opportunities for climate-proofing livelihoods (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/135122).

• **CGIAR’s pastoral systems research** applied participatory methods to assess the **Pastoral Code’s** implementation, identifying conflicts and decentralization challenges in Monguel (Gorgol region) (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/36082).

• **CGIAR’s foresight initiatives** empowered youth through creative planning for climate-adaptive fisheries, incorporating their visions into regional adaptation strategies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/34904).
Mauritania.mitigation: • **CGIAR’s dryland research** analyzed **spatiotemporal land-use changes** in West African drylands, quantifying carbon emissions from deforestation and soil degradation—including hotspots in Mauritania—and proposing sustainable land governance frameworks (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.indic.2025.101004).
Mauritania.water:
• **CGIAR’s participatory programs** trained farmers and technicians in **Mauritania** to build **small dams, dikes, and runoff strips** for soil and water conservation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/36080).

• **CGIAR integrated water conservation** with **livestock and rangeland management** through participatory research (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/36079).

Equatorial Guinea.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture and adaptation strategies** in **West Africa** (including Equatorial Guinea’s regional context) through **sector-specific resilience frameworks and bioeconomic modeling**. Specifically:
- CGIAR synthesized **climate change impacts, adaptation strategies, and policy mechanisms** for crop, livestock, and fishery sectors to enhance smallholder resilience (Source A).
- Researchers applied **bioeconomic models** to assess maize yield reductions under climate change (2050/2080) and proposed **adaptation measures** for high-input systems (Source B).
Equatorial Guinea.adaptation:
• CGIAR **reviewed climate-smart agriculture policies and institutional mechanisms** across West Africa, analyzing subsector-specific adaptation strategies (crop, livestock, fishery) to improve farming system resilience (https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3).
• Researchers **modeled biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production**, identifying region-specific yield vulnerabilities (Western/Southern Africa) and proposing **adapted maize varieties and trade-based mitigation** to offset food insecurity (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).

Liberia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture and adaptation strategies** in **Liberia** through targeted research and policy-informed interventions. Specifically:
- **CIAT** assessed climate impacts on cocoa production and recommended region-specific adaptation strategies to inform USD 75 million in IFAD investments (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/76385).
- **CGIAR researchers** analyzed climate hazards, vulnerability, and adaptive responses for Liberia’s agricultural projects under the Green Climate Fund (GCF) (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PY3YOZ).
Liberia.adaptation:
• **CGIAR (via CIAT)** conducted climate and environmental assessments in Liberia to prioritize adaptation strategies for cocoa farming, directly influencing a USD 4.5 million IFAD project in four districts (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/76385).

• **CGIAR researchers** categorized Liberia’s agricultural adaptation projects (e.g., FP160) under the **IPCC Risk Framework**, mapping climate hazards, vulnerability, exposure, and response strategies for GCF-funded initiatives (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PY3YOZ).

• **CGIAR (via CCAFS)** synthesized climate-smart agriculture strategies for West Africa, including Liberia, to improve resilience in crop, livestock, and fishery sectors through policy and institutional mechanisms (https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3).

• **CGIAR researchers** identified **shade trees as critical adaptation strategies** for cocoa in Liberia’s least vulnerable regions (southern Cameroon-Ghana-Côte d’Ivoire-Liberia belt), reversing trends of shade reduction to mitigate drought stress (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/dvn/5w7lgw).

• **CGIAR** analyzed COVID-19 lockdown impacts on Liberia’s food security, recommending policy adjustments to sustain planting seasons and reduce hunger risks (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41538-020-00073-0).

• **CGIAR (via maize systems research)** modeled biophysical impacts of climate change on staple crops (e.g., maize) in West Africa, proposing input-level-specific adaptation measures for Liberia’s lowland/dry mid-altitude environments (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).

Belize.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and policy-driven resilience** in **Belize** through **regional modeling, participatory frameworks, and capacity-building**. Specifically:
- **CCAFS** led **regional CSA strategy formulation** for Central America (including Belize) using future scenarios methodology to create a robust policy instrument (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121429]).
- **CCAFS** developed a **multi-criteria framework** to prioritize CSA practices for Belize, aiding policymakers in targeting scarce resources (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/100495]).
- **CCAFS** conducted **climate impact modeling** for smallholder agriculture and ecosystems, emphasizing adaptation needs for vulnerable farmers (Source: [10.1007/s10584-016-1867-y]).
Belize.adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** modeled **climate change impacts on smallholder agriculture and ecosystems** in Central America (including Belize), identifying urgent adaptation needs for crop yield and ecosystem services (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1867-y]).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** advanced the **Central American CSA regional strategy (2018–2030)**, using future scenarios to design a resilient policy framework adaptable to climate variability (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121429]).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** published **seasonal climate bulletins** (e.g., *Boletín Centroamericano, Clima y Agricultura*) for Belize, providing **climate forecasts, expected agricultural impacts, and COVID-19-adapted recommendations** (Sources: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111468], [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111467]).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** designed a **participatory CSA prioritization framework** to help Belizean policymakers allocate investments for climate-resilient agricultural practices (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/100495]).

• **CGIAR** analyzed **climate-smart agriculture risks in Belize**, outlining barriers to adoption and institutional enablers for scaling CSA (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/100326]).
Belize.mitigation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** advised the **Central America Agricultural Council** on **greenhouse gas (GHG) accounting and mitigation practices**, offering training to Belizean decision-makers on agricultural sector contributions to climate change (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/124664]).

• **CGIAR** framed **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) in Belize** as a tool to **reduce/remove GHG emissions** while increasing productivity, aligning with national mitigation co-benefits (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/100326]).

• **CGIAR** identified **mitigation-adaptation synergies** in Latin American rural policies, though Belize-specific GHG mitigation practices were not detailed in this sample (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831]).

Botswana.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture, gender-inclusive adaptation, and soil carbon research** in **Botswana** through targeted studies and capacity-building initiatives. Specifically:
- **CGIAR researchers** analyzed biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production, identifying yield reductions and adaptation strategies under varying input levels (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
- **CGIAR’s Climate Services team** facilitated workshops to strengthen institutional linkages and co-produce climate information for agriculture and food security (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116941](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116941)).
Botswana.adaptation:
• **CGIAR researchers** evaluated climate change impacts on maize yields in Botswana, highlighting regional vulnerabilities and adaptation measures for medium/high-input systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
• **CGIAR’s Southern Africa team** assessed gender disparities in access to rural climate services, recommending ICT-based solutions and women’s group inclusion for equitable adaptation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216)).
• **CGIAR** organized capacity-building workshops on objective seasonal forecasting to enhance climate services for Botswana’s agriculture sector (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116941](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116941)).
• **CGIAR’s policy engagement team** analyzed climate-smart agriculture strategies, advocating for institutional alignment to integrate mitigation and adaptation in Botswana’s agricultural policies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/b978-0-12-810521-4.00012-8](https://doi.org/10.1016/b978-0-12-810521-4.00012-8)).
• **CGIAR** provided a comprehensive analysis of Botswana’s food security threats under climate change, emphasizing rising temperatures and extreme events (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/154087](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/154087)).
Botswana.mitigation:
• **CGIAR researchers** conducted uncertainty analysis on soil organic carbon stocks in Botswana using Monte Carlo simulations to quantify measurement errors (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ABNGCO](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ABNGCO)).
