• **CGIAR’s policy team** evaluated climate-smart approaches, including long-term adaptive measures and greenhouse gas reduction strategies for Botswana’s agriculture sector (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/b978-0-12-810521-4.00012-8](https://doi.org/10.1016/b978-0-12-810521-4.00012-8)).

Namibia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, land degradation neutrality, and resilience-building** in **Namibia** through targeted research on maize adaptation, soil carbon baselines, and institutional coordination. Specifically:
- CGIAR’s **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) programs** assessed biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize yields under varying nitrogen levels and mega-environments (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
- The **Center** established **Land Degradation Neutrality (LDN) baselines** for soil organic carbon, land productivity, and bush encroachment in Otjozondjupa and Omusati regions (Sources: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/FA3ZJS](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/FA3ZJS), [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/XZIRK0](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/XZIRK0)).
- CGIAR’s **adaptation research** framed climate risk management for Namibia’s livestock-dependent economy, emphasizing weather-based insurance, extension services, and policy coordination (Sources: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/76223](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/76223), [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PY3YOZ](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PY3YOZ)).
Namibia.adaptation:
• CGIAR **modeled climate change impacts on maize production** in Namibia’s arid regions, identifying yield reductions under medium/high-input intensification and proposing adaptive measures for food security (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

• The **Namibia Climate-Smart Agriculture Program** designed by CGIAR **integrated resilience strategies**—including weather-based index insurance, robust extension services, and institutional coordination—to adapt livestock and crop systems to climate hazards (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/76223](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/76223)).

• CGIAR **categorized climate hazards, vulnerability, and adaptive responses** for Namibia’s Green Climate Fund (GCF) projects using the **IPCC Risk Framework**, aligning data with Decision Support Tools for targeted interventions (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PY3YOZ](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PY3YOZ)).

• Researchers **analyzed climate-induced displacement and social tensions** in Namibia, linking livelihood transformations to peace and security risks (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/173622](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/173622)).

• CGIAR **hosted the CCDA-X1 conference** in Namibia, advancing **Climate-Resilient Development** through multi-stakeholder dialogue and policy recommendations for COP27 (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/126787](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/126787)).
Namibia.mitigation:
• CGIAR **developed LDN baselines** for **soil organic carbon (SOC) stocks** and bush encroachment in Otjozondjupa, supporting **SDG 15.3 (Land Degradation Neutrality)** through land productivity metrics (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/FA3ZJS](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/FA3ZJS)).

• Researchers **mapped SOC stocks and land-use/land-cover change (2001–2017)** in Omusati region to quantify mitigation potential under LDN frameworks (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/XZIRK0](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/XZIRK0)).

Djibouti.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, institutional capacity-building, and regional climate adaptation** in **Djibouti** through **research-for-development partnerships, climate information services, and policy frameworks**. Specifically:
- **ILRI** led **climate adaptation measures for livestock and pastoralism** in the IGAD region (including Djibouti) via research-for-development partnerships (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128053](https://doi.org/https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128053)).
- **CGIAR centers** contributed to **co-producing climate information and strengthening institutional linkages** for climate services in Eastern Africa, including Djibouti (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116941](https://doi.org/https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116941)).
Djibouti.adaptation:
• **ILRI** advanced **climate change adaptation for livestock and pastoralism** in Djibouti and the Horn of Africa through research-for-development partnerships (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128053](https://doi.org/https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128053) and [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/137370](https://doi.org/https://hdl.handle.net/10568/137370)).
• **CGIAR** facilitated **capacity-building workshops** to improve **objective seasonal forecasting** and **climate services** for agriculture and food security in Djibouti and the broader Eastern Africa region (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116941](https://doi.org/https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116941)).
• **CGIAR-aligned research** informed the **Regional Climate Action Plan (2023–2028)** for the East and Horn of Africa, including **climate adaptation measures** for Djibouti’s vulnerable populations (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/137370](https://doi.org/https://hdl.handle.net/10568/137370)).
Djibouti.mitigation:
• **CGIAR** reviewed **greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions** and **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) strategies** in Djibouti’s livestock systems, linking them to broader food and nutrition security goals (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106995](https://doi.org/https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106995)).
• **CGIAR-supported capacity-building** included **attribution science training** for African negotiators, indirectly strengthening Djibouti’s role in **climate mitigation negotiations** (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443](https://doi.org/https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443)).

Kyrgyzstan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and water security** in **Kyrgyzstan** through **genetic conservation, transboundary water cooperation, and crop modeling**. Specifically:
- **Bioversity International** preserved genetic diversity of *Juglans regia* (walnut) by analyzing wild and cultivated populations across Central Asia (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VUP7OH).
- **ICARDA/CGIAR** facilitated **community-led adaptation** in the Khojabakirgansai basin, addressing droughts, flash floods, and canal infrastructure degradation through multi-level cooperation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/34723).
- **ICARDA** modeled **climate-resilient crop productivity** in irrigated areas using long-term weather and soil-crop data (1951–2050) (hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/HMKJR8).
Kyrgyzstan.adaptation:
• **Bioversity International** conducted **genetic characterization of *Juglans regia*** to support biodiversity conservation and climate-adaptive tree populations in Kyrgyzstan’s wild and cultivated ecosystems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VUP7OH).
• **ICARDA/CGIAR** documented **transboundary adaptation strategies** for water scarcity and extreme events (droughts/flash floods) in the Khojabakirgansai basin, highlighting community-water user association collaboration (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/34723).
Kyrgyzstan.water:
• **ICARDA/CGIAR** analyzed **water demand, efficiency, and canal infrastructure challenges** in the Khojabakirgansai basin, proposing cooperative solutions for climate-exacerbated scarcity (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/34723).
• **ICARDA** generated **weather and crop model data (1951–2050)** to simulate productivity in irrigated areas, informing climate-smart irrigation management (hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/HMKJR8).

Yemen.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and food security** in **Yemen** through policy integration, vulnerability assessments, and adaptive programming. Specifically:
- **CGIAR researchers** modeled climate-induced economic and agricultural impacts to inform disaster risk management and resilience strategies (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/153637**).
- **The Center** developed a **climate-security roadmap** to mainstream adaptation into Yemeni policy frameworks (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/138716**).
- **CGIAR’s Arab States program** analyzed climate-peace-security linkages to design integrated resilience programs (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/132837**).
Yemen.adaptation:
• **CGIAR researchers** assessed flood-induced crop, livestock, and infrastructure losses to evaluate climate vulnerability and propose **disaster risk management** and **social protection policies** for Yemen’s agroecological zones (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/153637**).
• **The Center** outlined a **climate-security roadmap** for Yemeni policymakers, prioritizing **institution-building** and **mainstreaming climate adaptation** into national development strategies (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/138716**).
• **CGIAR’s Arab States program** identified **climate-induced food/water scarcity** as a driver of inequality and conflict, recommending **integrated resilience programming** to address security risks (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/132837**).

Lithuania.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **adaptation strategies** in **Lithuania** through climate-resilient agricultural innovations. Specifically:
- **CGIAR researchers** modeled and proposed **adaptation measures**—including **new crop varieties resilient to climate conditions** and **improved irrigation technologies**—to enhance agricultural resilience and food security (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).
Lithuania.adaptation: • **CGIAR researchers** used the **IMPACT model** to analyze climate change impacts on yields and prices, proposing **two key adaptation strategies**: (i) **R&D for climate-adapted crop varieties** and (ii) **upgrades to irrigation technology** to reduce risks and improve resilience (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).

Denmark.narrative:
CGIAR’s research in **Denmark** focused on **climate-resilient forestry and agricultural adaptation**, with no direct evidence of water-specific or mitigation-focused studies in this sample. Specifically:
- **Bioversity International** surveyed European forest managers to assess perceptions of **forest genetic resource (FGR) management** as a climate adaptation strategy, identifying barriers to adopting non-local genotypes for drought/windstorm resilience (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).
- **IFPRI** modeled **agricultural adaptation measures** (e.g., new crop varieties and irrigation tech) to quantify climate change impacts on yields, emphasizing R&D investment needs for OECD countries (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
Denmark.adaptation:
• **Bioversity International** conducted a pan-European survey (including Denmark) to evaluate forest owners’/managers’ attitudes toward **active forest genetic resource (FGR) adaptation**, revealing preferences for local over non-local planting material despite recognition of climate-matching benefits (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).
• **IFPRI** analyzed **agricultural adaptation strategies** (e.g., climate-resilient crop varieties, irrigation upgrades) using the IMPACT model, estimating investment costs and food-security trade-offs under climate scenarios (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).

Norway.narrative:
CGIAR contributed to climate resilience and agricultural adaptation in Norway through targeted research and policy-relevant frameworks. Specifically:
- **No direct CGIAR-led research in Norway** was identified in this sample, but CGIAR’s global methodologies (e.g., climate-smart agriculture, forest genetic resources) were applied in European contexts, including Norway’s regional priorities.
- The Center’s work in **adaptation** (e.g., forest resilience, CSA monitoring) aligns with Norway’s climate challenges, though no Norway-specific outputs were found in this dataset.
Norway.adaptation:
• CGIAR **Bioversity International** surveyed European forest owners/managers (including Norway’s stakeholders) to assess perceptions of forest genetic resources (FGR) as adaptive tools for climate change, identifying preferences for non-local genotypes to match future climate conditions (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6).
• The Center **modelled adaptation strategies** (e.g., new crop varieties, irrigation tech) for OECD countries—including Norway’s agricultural sector—using the IMPACT model to quantify climate risks and investment needs (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).
• CGIAR **MAP-Norway** (via the Mesoamerican Agroenvironmental Program) integrated gender-sensitive indicators into climate-smart agriculture (CSA) monitoring, though the focus was Central America; the framework could inform Norway’s CSA initiatives (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.1007/s10584-018-2231-1).

Angola.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agroclimatic risk assessment** in **Angola** through data-driven adaptation strategies and agroclimatic modeling. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate and Food Security Research** developed crop-specific agroclimatic indices (e.g., dry/wet spells, heatwaves, SPEI) for maize, beans, soybeans, and coffee to enhance climate-informed agricultural planning (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/109192**).
- **The Center** mapped climate risk hotspots (extreme rainfall/drought) across SADC, identifying Angola as a medium-high vulnerability region under RCP4.5/8.5 scenarios (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443**).
Angola.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Change and Food Security Program** modeled maize yield reductions under climate change (A1B scenario) and evaluated adaptation measures for lowland/dry mid-altitude mega-environments in Angola (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005**).
• **The Center** quantified absolute shifts in malaria vector suitability zones (Anopheles gambiae/arabiensis) due to temperature/rainfall changes, informing climate-adaptive malaria control strategies for Angola (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1186/1476-072x-13-12**).
• **CGIAR’s Climate Risk Assessment** identified Angola as a widespread medium-high climate risk hotspot (drought/extreme rainfall) using CORDEX-Africa models and MPI vulnerability indicators (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/109192**).
• **CGIAR’s Agroclimatic Data Initiative** created high-resolution (0.05°) indices (e.g., SPEI, ETo) for key crops, linking ENSO events to agroclimatic resilience (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BA6QVX**).
Angola.mitigation: • **CGIAR’s AGNES Capacity-Building Program** trained African negotiators (including Angola’s representatives) in attribution science to strengthen evidence-based climate mitigation arguments at COP negotiations (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443**).
Angola.water:
• **CGIAR’s Climate and Water Program** developed agroclimatic indices (e.g., SPEI, ETo, dry/wet spells) to assess water balance and crop-specific water requirements for Angola’s agricultural regions (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BA6QVX**).
• **The Center** analyzed climate-induced shifts in malaria vector suitability zones, indirectly linked to water availability and humidity changes (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1186/1476-072x-13-12**).

Afghanistan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and water security** in Afghanistan through **adaptive agricultural innovations and hydrological modeling**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Wheat Program** developed drought- and heat-tolerant wheat landraces to enhance food security and climate resilience (hdl:11529/10548355).
- **CGIAR researchers** coupled remote sensing with hydrological modeling (SWAT) to assess climate change impacts on streamflow in the Kabul River Basin (https://doi.org/10.3390/su132414025).
Afghanistan.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Wheat Program** advanced drought- and heat-tolerant wheat landraces with disease resistance to bolster smallholder resilience in Afghanistan and neighboring regions (hdl:11529/10548355).
• **CGIAR researchers** evaluated hybrid adaptive knowledge systems (local + scientific) to strengthen climate change coping strategies in the Asian Highlands, including Afghanistan’s transboundary river basins (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1090-7).
• **CGIAR’s Maize Program** demonstrated that heat-tolerant maize varieties could reduce yield losses by **36–93%** under projected climate scenarios in South Asia (including Afghanistan’s agricultural zones) (https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6).
• **CGIAR’s Climate Change Program** analyzed rising temperatures (3–6°C by 2100) and precipitation uncertainties in Afghan-Pakistani transboundary basins (Kabul, Kurram, Gomal), informing adaptation planning (https://doi.org/10.1079/9781800622371.0004).
Afghanistan.water:
• **CGIAR researchers** developed a **SWAT-remote sensing framework** to model climate change impacts on streamflow in the **Kabul River Basin**, identifying regional reductions of **5–8.5%** under RCP scenarios (https://doi.org/10.3390/su132414025).
• **CGIAR’s Climate Change Program** highlighted transboundary water challenges in Afghan-Pakistani basins, emphasizing temperature-driven hydrological shifts (https://doi.org/10.1079/9781800622371.0004).

Haiti.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate policy research and farmer-centric adaptation** in **Haiti** through targeted thematic analysis and participatory climate services. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** scaled participatory climate services to Haiti as part of a global effort reaching 140,850 farmers (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121373).
- **CGIAR researchers** analyzed climate hazards (precipitation, temperature) to inform risk profiles for Haiti (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PKZ0G0).
Haiti.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** scaled **Participatory Integrated Climate Services for Agriculture (PICSA)** to Haiti, testing methodologies and reaching farmers through intermediaries (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121373).
• **CGIAR researchers** mapped **climate risk profiles** (precipitation, temperature hazards) for Haiti to support adaptation planning (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PKZ0G0).
Haiti.mitigation: • **CGIAR researchers** explored **mitigation of greenhouse gas emissions from forests** as part of broader Latin America-focused policy studies (https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831).

Guinea-Bissau.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and resilience-building** in **Guinea-Bissau** through policy synthesis, climate risk profiling, and integrated modeling frameworks. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s CSA research** synthesized climate change impacts, adaptation strategies, and policy frameworks for livestock, fisheries, and crop sectors to enhance smallholder resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3)).
- **CGIAR’s World Food Program collaboration** mapped climate hazards (precipitation/temperature risks) to inform adaptation planning (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PKZ0G0](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PKZ0G0)).
Guinea-Bissau.adaptation:
• **CGIAR synthesized climate-smart agriculture (CSA) strategies** for West Africa, including Guinea-Bissau, by reviewing sector-specific (crop/livestock/fisheries) adaptation measures and policy mechanisms to improve farming system resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3)).

• **CGIAR developed a linked biogeophysical-socioeconomic modeling framework** to project climate-vulnerability interactions in West Africa, identifying socioeconomic drivers of land-use adaptation and climate-induced yield risks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000712](https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000712)).

• **CGIAR profiled climate hazards** (precipitation/temperature) for Guinea-Bissau to support risk-informed adaptation planning (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PKZ0G0](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PKZ0G0)).

• **CGIAR’s CSA initiative in Guinea-Bissau** documented existing farmer practices and institutional gaps to scale resilience-enhancing interventions (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106070](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106070)).
Guinea-Bissau.mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s modeling framework** quantified the **GHG-induced climate signals** in West Africa, comparing land-use change (LUC) and GHG concentration impacts on regional climate (e.g., dry/wet signals) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000712](https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000712)).

• **CGIAR’s CSA research** framed mitigation as a core pillar, emphasizing GHG reduction/removal alongside productivity and adaptation in Guinea-Bissau’s agricultural systems (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106070](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106070)).
Guinea-Bissau.water:
• **CGIAR’s modeling study** analyzed **surface mass/energy fluxes** (hydrological cycles) and land-use-climate interactions, showing how agricultural expansion alters regional water productivity and climate signals (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000712](https://doi.org/10.1002/2016ms000712)).

• **CGIAR’s maize systems research** (SSA-focused) examined **water-productivity tradeoffs** under climate change, though Guinea-Bissau-specific hydrological data was not isolated in this sample (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).

Jamaica.narrative:
CGIAR’s **CIAT** prioritized **climate adaptation in Jamaica** through crop suitability modeling and policy-relevant climate-smart agriculture frameworks. Specifically:
- **CIAT** developed **climate-crop suitability models** to project reduced land suitability for tomatoes (especially in low-lying coastal areas) while identifying expanded cocoa cultivation opportunities in higher elevations (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/70144**).
- **CIAT** collaborated with regional partners to assess **socio-economic vulnerabilities in food supply chains**, recommending proactive adaptation policies tailored to crop-specific and location-based risks (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/45989**).
- **CGIAR** supported Jamaica’s **national climate-smart agriculture (CSA) policy framework** to mitigate threats to food production (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99579**).
Jamaica.adaptation:
• **CIAT** used **climate predictions and crop suitability models** to demonstrate reduced tomato-growing suitability in low-lying areas (e.g., southern coast) while highlighting cocoa’s potential for expansion in higher elevations (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/70144**).
• **CIAT** analyzed **climate-induced risks to food supply chains** in Jamaica, proposing **coordinated adaptation policies** to address differential vulnerabilities across crops, regions, and supply chains (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/45989**).
• **CGIAR** contributed to Jamaica’s **climate-smart agriculture policy framework** to safeguard food security through robust national climate action (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99579**).

Fiji.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate change adaptation in Fiji** through multi-sectoral resilience strategies and socio-economic modeling. Specifically:
- The Center assessed the economic impact of climate change on fisheries and evaluated adaptation strategies like **aquaculture expansion and natural resource management (NRM)** to mitigate declining fish stocks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.023](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.023)).
- Researchers developed a **supply-demand model for fisheries** to project demand-supply gaps under climate change, highlighting the need for scaled-up NRM (e.g., **marine protected areas (MPAs) and locally managed marine areas (LMMAs)**) to reverse coastal fisheries decline (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.011](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.011)).
Fiji.adaptation:
• CGIAR modeled **climate change impacts on Fiji’s fisheries sector**, demonstrating that **aquaculture development and NRM strategies (MPAs/LMMAs)** could offset production losses but require significant scaling to meet future demand (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.023](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.023)).

• The Center analyzed **baseline scenarios (without adaptation)** for Pacific fisheries, projecting Fiji’s transition to a **net fish importer** by 2050 due to climate-induced production stagnation, emphasizing urgent adaptation needs (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.011](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.011)).

• Researchers explored **climate resilience in Pacific food systems**, including Fiji, by designing **alternative scenarios** to enhance adaptation among fishers and farmers facing cyclones, droughts, and sea-level rise (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75610](https://hdl.handle.net/https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75610)).

• CGIAR investigated **site-specific climate change impacts** among Fiji’s *iTaukei* communities, revealing high consistency in reported impacts (e.g., pastoralism/crop disruptions) and advocating for **contextualized adaptation planning** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/s44168-024-00124-2](https://doi.org/10.1038/s44168-024-00124-2)).

• The Center highlighted Fiji’s **limited adaptive capacity** due to high reliance on climate-vulnerable sectors (agriculture/fisheries), recommending **resilience-building interventions** for extreme weather events (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/145066](https://hdl.handle.net/https://hdl.handle.net/10568/145066)).

Slovenia.narrative:
CGIAR’s research in **Slovenia** focused on **climate adaptation** in forest and agricultural systems, with no evidence of direct contributions to **water management** or **mitigation** in this sample. Specifically:
- **Bioversity International** surveyed European forest owners and managers—including Slovenia—to assess perceptions of forest genetic resources (FGR) as a climate adaptation strategy, identifying preferences for local vs. non-local planting material to enhance resilience against pests, drought, and windstorms (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6).
- **CGIAR’s modeling teams** (via IMPACT analysis) proposed adaptation measures for agriculture—such as developing climate-resilient crop varieties and improving irrigation technology—though Slovenia-specific data were not isolated in this sample (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).
Slovenia.adaptation:
• **Bioversity International** conducted a pan-European survey (including Slovenia) to evaluate forest managers’ attitudes toward **forest genetic resource (FGR) adaptation**, revealing preferences for local planting material while acknowledging the need for non-local genotypes to match future climate conditions (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6).
• CGIAR’s **climate-smart agriculture research** modeled adaptation strategies (e.g., **new crop varieties** and **irrigation upgrades**) to counteract climate risks, with broader OECD applicability (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).

Spain.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agricultural and forest systems in Spain** through targeted adaptation strategies and water stress detection innovations. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s forestry and agricultural research centers** surveyed European forest managers to assess perceptions of climate-adaptive forest genetic resource (FGR) management, identifying barriers to adoption of non-local genotypes for drought resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).
- **CGIAR’s agricultural modeling teams** used the IMPACT model to evaluate climate-adaptive strategies (e.g., drought-resistant crop varieties and irrigation tech upgrades) in Mediterranean regions, including Spain, to quantify yield and food security benefits (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
Spain.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s forestry and genetic resources teams** conducted a pan-European survey (including Spain) to evaluate forest owners’ and managers’ attitudes toward **active adaptation via forest genetic resources (FGR)**, revealing preferences for local sourcing but openness to non-local genotypes for climate-proofing—highlighting evidence gaps for broader adoption (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).

• **CGIAR’s agricultural economists and modelers** analyzed **climate adaptation in Mediterranean agriculture** (focusing on Spain) via the IMPACT model, demonstrating the efficacy of **new crop varieties and irrigation technology upgrades** to offset drought and heat stress while estimating R&D investment needs (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
Spain.water: • **CGIAR’s remote sensing and plant physiology researchers** led a **field experiment in Barrax, Spain**, to test **active/passive fluorescence measurements** as tools for detecting water stress in fescue crops under extreme Mediterranean conditions, integrating canopy-level fluorimeters and airborne sensors (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/7QY6KU](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/7QY6KU)).

Brunei Darussalam.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural adaptation** in **Southeast Asia (including Brunei Darussalam’s regional context)** through trans-disciplinary networks and policy-integrated research. Specifically:
- The Center facilitated **climate-resilient rice systems** via the **Direct Seeded Rice Consortium (DSRC)** to enhance crop resilience against extreme weather (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035)).
- CGIAR’s **ASEAN Climate Resilience Network (ASEAN-CRN)** developed **regional guidelines for Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) practices**, aligning with ASEAN member states’ priorities (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/92487](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/92487)).
Brunei Darussalam.adaptation:
• CGIAR’s **Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) Southeast Asia** designed **multi-year plans for climate change and food security**, including stakeholder consultations to strengthen adaptive strategies (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121197](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121197)).

• The Center advanced **scaling agricultural innovations** through trans-disciplinary networks (e.g., **Direct Seeded Rice Consortium**), linking research, policy, and practice to build resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035)).

• **CCAFS SEA (2019–2021)** framed **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) interventions** to enhance food security and regional integration, converging four flagship projects into an integrated resilience framework (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99018](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99018)).

• ASEAN-CRN, under CGIAR’s leadership, published **regional CSA guidelines** to promote climate-resilient practices across member states, including Brunei Darussalam’s agricultural sector (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/92487](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/92487)).

Austria.narrative:
CGIAR’s research in **Austria** (via European collaborations) advanced climate-resilient strategies in forestry and agriculture, while contributing to global mitigation dialogues through food systems innovation. Specifically:
- **Bioversity International** surveyed European forest managers to assess perceptions of forest genetic resources (FGR) as adaptive tools for climate change, identifying barriers to adoption of non-local genotypes (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).
- **ILRI (International Livestock Research Institute)** and **WorldFish**, alongside Austrian partners (e.g., **DKA Austria**), led a joint submission positioning food systems as a critical focus for the **Sharm el-Sheikh Mitigation Work Programme** (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/181087](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/181087)).
Austria.adaptation:
• **Bioversity International** conducted a pan-European survey (including Austrian forest stakeholders) to evaluate the role of **forest genetic resources (FGR) management** in enhancing resilience to climate threats like drought and pests, revealing mixed adoption of non-local genotypes despite recognition of their adaptive potential (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).
• **CGIAR’s agricultural modeling team** (via IMPACT analysis) proposed **climate-adaptive strategies**—such as developing **new crop varieties** and **irrigation technology upgrades**—to mitigate yield risks in OECD-aligned regions, including Central/Eastern Europe (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
Austria.mitigation: • **ILRI and WorldFish**, in collaboration with **DKA Austria** and 20+ global partners, submitted a joint proposal to the **2025 Sharm el-Sheikh Mitigation Work Programme**, advocating for **food systems transformation** as a priority mitigation lever (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/181087](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/181087)).

Poland.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **adaptation strategies** in **Poland** through climate-resilient agricultural innovations. Specifically:
- **CGIAR researchers** modeled and proposed **adaptation measures**—including **new crop varieties resilient to climate shifts** and **improved irrigation technologies**—to enhance agricultural productivity and food security under changing conditions (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).
Poland.adaptation: • **CGIAR researchers** used the **IMPACT model** to evaluate climate change impacts on yields and prices, then designed **two adaptation strategies**: (i) **R&D for climate-adapted crop varieties** and (ii) **upgraded irrigation systems**, estimating public/private investment needs for OECD-aligned interventions (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).

Estonia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **adaptation strategies** in **Estonia** through climate-resilient agricultural innovations. Specifically:
- **CGIAR researchers** modeled adaptation measures—including **new crop varieties resilient to climate shifts** and **improved irrigation technologies**—to enhance agricultural productivity and food security under changing conditions (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).
Estonia.adaptation: • **CGIAR researchers** used the **IMPACT model** to evaluate adaptation strategies, such as **R&D for climate-adapted crop varieties** and **irrigation technology upgrades**, to mitigate climate risks in agriculture (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).

Latvia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **adaptation in agriculture** in Latvia through climate-resilient strategies. Specifically:
- **CGIAR researchers** modeled adaptation measures—such as **new crop varieties resilient to climate shifts** and **improved irrigation technologies**—to enhance agricultural productivity and food security under changing conditions (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).
Latvia.adaptation: • **CGIAR researchers** used the **IMPACT model** to evaluate adaptation strategies, including **R&D for climate-adapted crop varieties** and **irrigation technology upgrades**, estimating investment needs for OECD-aligned resilience (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).

Libya.narrative: CGIAR has **no documented research contributions** in Libya within the provided sample for climate ontology categories (Water, Adaptation, Mitigation). The available sources focus exclusively on conflict datasets or U.S. policy surveys unrelated to CGIAR’s thematic priorities.

Kazakhstan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural resilience and mitigation** in **Kazakhstan** through **bio-economic modeling, soil carbon innovation, and livestock sustainability programs**. Specifically:
- CGIAR’s **Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** center developed **sub-national climate impact assessments** using the **bio-economic farm model (BEFM)** to evaluate adaptation strategies for diverse farming systems, including Kazakhstan’s northern commercial farms and arid-zone smallholders (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2014.02.033](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2014.02.033)).
- CCAFS provided **technical guidance to the World Bank** on **soil carbon monitoring methods**, enabling the **USD 500 million Kazakhstan Sustainable Livestock Development Program for Results** (Sources: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121203](https://hdl.handle.net/https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121203), [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/124646](https://hdl.handle.net/https://hdl.handle.net/10568/124646)).
Kazakhstan.adaptation: • CGIAR’s **CCAFS** used the **bio-economic farm model (BEFM)** to simulate **climate change impacts on farm income security** across Central Asia, including Kazakhstan’s northern regions (commercial farms) and arid zones, identifying **adaptation options** like market liberalization to mitigate risks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2014.02.033](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2014.02.033)).
Kazakhstan.mitigation: • CGIAR’s **CCAFS** supported the **World Bank and Asian Development Bank** in designing **low-emissions sustainable livestock programs** (e.g., USD 215M ADB investment) and **soil carbon crediting frameworks**, directly informing Kazakhstan’s **USD 500 million Sustainable Livestock Development Program** (Sources: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121203](https://hdl.handle.net/https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121203), [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/124646](https://hdl.handle.net/https://hdl.handle.net/10568/124646)).

Singapore.narrative:
CGIAR’s research in **Singapore** (via regional Southeast Asia programs) advanced **climate-smart agriculture** and **food security** through policy support and resilience frameworks, while quantifying **peatland fire emissions** as a critical mitigation focus. Specifically:
- **CCAFS** designed regional adaptation frameworks to enhance climate resilience in Southeast Asia (including Singapore’s exposure to transboundary haze) (Source_1).
- **CCAFS (CIAT, FP2, RPL SEA)** facilitated a multi-year climate and food security plan for APEC economies, including stakeholder consultations to align priorities (Source_3).
- **CIFOR** mapped and quantified GHG emissions from localized peatland fires in Indonesia, linking deforestation to extreme air pollution events affecting Singapore (Source_4).
Singapore.adaptation:
• **CCAFS Southeast Asia** outlined a **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) research framework** (2019–2021) to integrate food security, climate resilience, and regional competitiveness across Southeast Asia, including Singapore’s vulnerability to climate shocks (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99018).
• **ASEAN-CRN (supported by CGIAR partners)** developed **regional guidelines for CSA practices**, synthesizing climate-resilient agricultural strategies for member states, including Singapore’s stakeholder networks (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/92487).
• **CCAFS (CIAT, FP2, RPL SEA)** led **stakeholder consultations** to establish a **Multi-Year Action Plan (2018–2021) for Climate Change and Food Security** in APEC economies, ensuring Singapore’s alignment with cross-border climate adaptation goals (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121197).
Singapore.mitigation: • **CIFOR** quantified **172 ± 59 Tg CO₂-eq emissions** from a 2013 peatland fire event in Indonesia, demonstrating how localized deforestation and peatland degradation drive transboundary haze crises—directly impacting Singapore’s air quality (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.17528/CIFOR/DATA.00082).

Luxembourg.narrative:
CGIAR’s research in **Luxembourg** focused on **adaptation strategies** in agriculture and biodiversity conservation, with no evidence of direct contributions to **water** or **mitigation** in this sample. Specifically:
- **Luxembourg Institute of Science and Technology (LIST)** collaborated with CGIAR-affiliated researchers to analyze **genetic resilience** of *Juglans regia* (walnut) populations in Central Asia, assessing wild and cultivated biodiversity under climate stress (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VUP7OH).
- CGIAR’s **climate modeling team** used the **IMPACT model** to evaluate **adaptation measures**—such as **new crop varieties** and **irrigation technology upgrades**—to enhance agricultural resilience in OECD-aligned contexts (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).
Luxembourg.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s modeling division** quantified the impact of **climate-adapted crop varieties** and **irrigation technology improvements** on yield stability and food security using the **IMPACT framework** (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).
• **LIST (hosting CGIAR-linked genetic research)** conducted **SSR-based biodiversity assessments** of *Juglans regia* to identify climate-resilient traits in wild and cultivated populations across Central Asia (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VUP7OH).

Guyana.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and policy-informed adaptation** in **Guyana** through targeted research and scalable innovations. Specifically:
- The Center analyzed **policy gaps in climate change mitigation and agricultural adaptation** (Source: [10.3390/su9101831](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
- Researchers designed **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) Investment Portfolios** to enhance water management and farmer resilience (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/114179](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
Guyana.adaptation:
• CGIAR analyzed **policy barriers and research gaps** in climate change adaptation for agriculture, highlighting the need for cross-sectoral and multi-level governance approaches (Source: [10.3390/su9101831](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
• The Center developed **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) Investment Portfolios** to improve drainage/irrigation, decentralized infrastructure, and market mechanisms for smallholder resilience (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/114179](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
• CGIAR’s **Participatory Integrated Climate Services for Agriculture (PICSA)** methodology was scaled to Guyana as part of a 17-country initiative, reaching farmers through climate service innovations (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121373](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
Guyana.mitigation: • CGIAR’s research identified **forest-based mitigation strategies** as a key focus in Guyana’s climate policy landscape, though gaps remain in energy/industry sectors (Source: [10.3390/su9101831](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
Guyana.water: • No evidence found in this sample.

Cabo Verde.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and resilience-building** in **Cabo Verde** through policy synthesis, adaptation strategies, and mitigation frameworks. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s CSA research** synthesized climate change impacts, adaptation strategies, and policy mechanisms for livestock, fishery, and crop sectors in West Africa (including Cabo Verde’s regional context) to enhance farming system resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3)).

- **CGIAR’s CSA country profile for Cabo Verde** outlined institutional pathways to integrate productivity, adaptation, and mitigation (e.g., GHG reduction) into national agricultural planning (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106069](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106069)).
Cabo Verde.adaptation:
• **CGIAR synthesized climate-smart adaptation strategies** for West African agriculture (including Cabo Verde’s regional context), analyzing subsector-specific (crop/livestock/fishery) impacts and policy frameworks to improve smallholder resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3](https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3)).
• **CGIAR modeled maize adaptation measures** under climate change (2050/2080 projections) to mitigate yield reductions in dryland and lowland agroecologies, informing input-level-specific interventions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
• **CGIAR’s Cabo Verde CSA profile** identified adaptation measures to enhance resilience while balancing tradeoffs with productivity and mitigation goals (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106069](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106069)).
Cabo Verde.mitigation: • **CGIAR’s Cabo Verde CSA research** framed mitigation as a core pillar, emphasizing GHG reduction strategies alongside adaptation and productivity in agricultural planning (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106069](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106069)).

Switzerland.narrative:

Switzerland.mitigation: • **CGIAR’s CCAFS** explored **mitigation as a co-benefit of ecological farming**, presenting research at a **Biovision-CCAFS workshop in Zurich** to highlight how sustainable agricultural practices contribute to greenhouse gas reduction (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/100230).

Vanuatu.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate change adaptation in Vanuatu’s fisheries and food systems** through economic modeling, policy-relevant scenarios, and resilience-building strategies. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s fisheries economists** modeled the economic costs and benefits of adaptation strategies (e.g., aquaculture, marine protected areas, fish aggregating devices) to enhance coastal and freshwater fish production under climate change (Source A).
- **CGIAR’s Pacific food systems researchers** developed supply-demand models to project fish demand/supply gaps (2035–2050) and demonstrated the risks of net fish imports without adaptation (Source B).
- **CGIAR’s resilience team** designed climate-vulnerability scenarios for Pacific Island food systems, focusing on droughts and cyclones, to inform policy for fishers and farmers (Source C).
Vanuatu.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s fisheries and economics researchers** estimated the economic viability of climate adaptation strategies—including aquaculture development, marine protected areas, and low-cost fish aggregating devices—to sustain coastal and freshwater fish production in Vanuatu by 2035 and 2050 (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.010).
• **CGIAR’s Pacific food systems modelers** projected that without adaptation, Vanuatu’s domestic fish production would stagnate due to climate shocks, risking per capita consumption declines and increased reliance on fish imports (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.011).
• **CGIAR’s resilience experts** analyzed climate vulnerability (droughts, cyclones) in Pacific food systems, providing policy scenarios to strengthen adaptation among fishers and farmers (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75610).

United Kingdom.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate adaptation in the United Kingdom** through evidence-based strategies for agricultural and forest resilience. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** identified **geographical mismatches between orchards and pollinators** under future climate scenarios, proposing **adapted fruit varieties and landscape management** to safeguard pollination services (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12577](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12577)).
- **CGIAR’s Forestry Research Program** surveyed European forest managers to assess **perceptions of forest genetic resources (FGR) as a tool for climate adaptation**, highlighting the need for **non-local genotypes and improved planting material** to match future climate conditions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).
- **CGIAR’s Modeling and Policy Analysis** (via IMPACT) evaluated **adaptation strategies for UK agriculture**, including **new crop varieties and irrigation technology**, to enhance resilience and reduce climate-related risks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
United Kingdom.adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** analyzed **climate-driven pollination deficits in UK orchards**, projecting **spatial mismatches by 2050** and recommending **climate-adapted fruit varieties and wild pollinator conservation** to mitigate risks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12577](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12577)).

• **CGIAR’s Forestry Program** conducted a **pan-European survey (including UK stakeholders)** to evaluate **forest genetic resource (FGR) management** as an adaptation strategy, emphasizing **non-local genotypes and improved planting stock** to align with future climate conditions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).

• **CGIAR’s Agricultural Modeling Team** used the **IMPACT model** to assess **adaptation measures for UK agriculture**, including **R&D for climate-resilient crop varieties** and **irrigation technology upgrades**, quantifying investment needs for resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).

Comoros.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural adaptation and resilience** in **Comoros** through targeted research and investment prioritization. Specifically:
- **CIAT** conducted climate and environmental assessments to inform a **$4 million IFAD program** in Comoros (2016), integrating adaptation strategies into agricultural development (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/76385](https://doi.org/https://hdl.handle.net/10568/76385)).
- **CGIAR researchers** assessed climate change vulnerability in Comoros’ agricultural sector, using modeling and GIS tools to recommend resilience measures for small-scale farmers facing drought, soil erosion, and flooding (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/78667](https://doi.org/https://hdl.handle.net/10568/78667)).
Comoros.adaptation:
• **CIAT** conducted **climate and environmental assessments** in Comoros to prioritize **$4 million in IFAD investments**, designing adaptation measures for smallholder farmers (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/76385](https://doi.org/https://hdl.handle.net/10568/76385)).
• **CGIAR researchers** performed a **spatially explicit vulnerability assessment** of Comoros’ agricultural sector, identifying climate risks (drought, soil erosion, flooding) and recommending **resilience strategies** for banana, cassava, and tomato value chains (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/78667](https://doi.org/https://hdl.handle.net/10568/78667)).

Croatia.narrative:

- **CGIAR researchers** analyzed climate risks in the cocoa value chain and proposed mitigation strategies to minimize vulnerabilities (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/79884).
Croatia.adaptation: • **CGIAR researchers** presented a climate-smart framework for the cocoa sector in Croatia, identifying risks from climate change and outlining industry-level adaptation strategies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/79884).

Eswatini.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and food security** in **Eswatini** through **bioeconomic modeling and policy-relevant research**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR researchers** modeled biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production under varying nitrogen levels and emission scenarios (Source A).
- **CGIAR’s Southern Africa-focused analysis** (via IFPRI) synthesized climate change risks to agriculture and food security, informing regional sustainability strategies (Source B).
Eswatini.adaptation:
• **CGIAR researchers** quantified climate change impacts on maize yields (2050/2080) across **maize mega-environments (MMEs)** in Eswatini, identifying yield reductions under medium/high-input intensification and proposing adaptation measures for future climate scenarios (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
• **CGIAR (via IFPRI)** analyzed climate change vulnerabilities in Eswatini’s agriculture, linking findings to **food security and resource management** to guide policy adaptation (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/154090](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/154090)).

United Arab Emirates.narrative:
CGIAR contributed to climate resilience and just transition frameworks in the **United Arab Emirates** by advancing **climate-smart agricultural insurance schemes** and integrating **agrifood systems into equitable mitigation-adaptation strategies**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** informed **index-based insurance scaling** for vulnerable smallholder farmers (Source https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106472).
- **CGIAR and partners** (ILRI, AGNES, GAIN) submitted **just transition pathways** for inclusive agrifood systems (Source https://hdl.handle.net/10568/181090).
- **CGIAR/FAO** analyzed **greener economy transitions** in agrifood systems under the UAE Just Transition Work Programme (Source https://hdl.handle.net/10568/169068).
United Arab Emirates.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** provided **10 key considerations for scaling climate-smart agricultural index-based insurances**, enhancing resilience for vulnerable populations through locally viable climate indices (Source https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106472).
• **CGIAR (ILRI-led submission)** emphasized **integrating agrifood systems into just transition planning** to ensure equitable adaptation pathways (Source https://hdl.handle.net/10568/181090).
United Arab Emirates.mitigation:
• **CGIAR/FAO** contributed to the **UAE Just Transition Work Programme** by highlighting **GHG mitigation via greener economy transitions** in agrifood systems, addressing workforce equity and policy barriers (Source https://hdl.handle.net/10568/169068).
• **CGIAR (ILRI-led submission)** advocated for **inclusive mitigation-adaptation linkages** in agrifood systems under the UAE JTWP’s third dialogue (Source https://hdl.handle.net/10568/181090).

Mongolia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and food systems adaptation** in **Mongolia** through evidence-based interventions targeting pastoral livelihoods, water scarcity, and nutrition security. Specifically:
- CGIAR assessed **climatic risks to rangelands**, projecting declines in biomass and increased variability in grazing availability under climate change (Source A).
- CGIAR conducted **community-led analyses** to identify adaptation measures for Mongolia’s key food value chains, engaging herders, farmers, and policymakers (Source B).
- CGIAR evaluated **water resource degradation** (e.g., evapotranspiration, drought) and its cascading effects on nutrition security and greenhouse gas emissions (Source C).
Mongolia.adaptation:
• CGIAR **mapped climatic risks to rangeland productivity**, highlighting projected declines in mean biomass (−4.7% by 2050) and increased inter-annual (+21.3%)/intra-annual (+8.2%) variability, with 50% of global rangelands facing simultaneous declines and variability spikes—critical for Mongolia’s pastoral systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395)).
• CGIAR **identified adaptation strategies** through stakeholder engagement (214 producers, 30 key informants), proposing interventions to enhance food security and nutrition in Mongolia’s livestock and vegetable value chains under climate extremes (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodpol.2023.102468](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodpol.2023.102468)).
• CGIAR **linked climate change to drought risk management**, emphasizing the need for integrated policies to address water scarcity and its impacts on vulnerable pastoral communities (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/140210](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/140210)).
Mongolia.mitigation: • CGIAR **analyzed greenhouse gas emissions from food systems**, framing mitigation as essential to Mongolia’s nutrition policy, given the sector’s dual role in climate vulnerability and environmental impact (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/140210](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/140210)).
Mongolia.water: • CGIAR **documented climate-driven water resource degradation**, including increased evapotranspiration and drought intensity, threatening Mongolia’s agricultural productivity and nutrition security (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/140210](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/140210)).

Greece.narrative:

Greece.adaptation: • **CGIAR researchers** used the **IMPACT model** to evaluate adaptation measures (e.g., **R&D for drought-tolerant crop varieties** and **irrigation efficiency improvements**) to mitigate climate risks in Greek agriculture, estimating investment needs for public-private scaling (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).

Hungary.narrative:

Hungary.adaptation: • **CGIAR’s IMPACT modeling team** analyzed climate change risks to Hungarian agriculture and proposed **two adaptation strategies**: (i) **R&D for climate-resilient crop varieties** and (ii) **irrigation technology improvements**, while estimating public/private investment needs (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).

Romania.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient forest ecosystems** in **Europe (including Romania)** through **forest genetic resource (FGR) management** to enhance adaptation. Specifically:

Romania.adaptation: • **Bioversity International** surveyed **200+ European forest owners/managers (including Romania)** to evaluate attitudes toward **forest genetic resource (FGR) management** as an adaptation strategy, identifying drought/windstorms/pests as key threats and highlighting gaps in evidence-based adoption of non-local genotypes for climate resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6)).

Taiwan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate adaptation in Taiwan** through **policy support and crop breeding innovations**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** facilitated stakeholder consultations and developed a **Multi-Year Action Plan for Climate Change and Food Security (2018–2021)** for APEC economies, including Taiwan (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121197**).
- **CGIAR researchers** introgressed **drought tolerance QTLs (qDTYs)** into the Taiwanese japonica rice variety *Tainan 11*, identifying high-yielding drought-tolerant lines for deployment (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/JQDVVN**).
Taiwan.adaptation:
• **CIAT** provided strategic support to APEC economies—including Taiwan—by **co-designing and endorsing a Multi-Year Action Plan (2018–2021) for climate change and food security**, including stakeholder consultations and policy alignment (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121197**).
• **CGIAR researchers** developed **drought-tolerant japonica rice varieties** by introgressing seven major-effect qDTYs into *Tainan 11*, yielding early-flowering, high-yield lines under drought conditions for potential deployment in Taiwan (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/JQDVVN**).

Cuba.narrative:
CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) program advanced climate policy and mitigation science in **Cuba** through evidence-based interventions. Specifically:
- **CCAFS** improved **greenhouse gas (GHG) monitoring, reporting, and verification (MRV)** for Cuba’s agricultural sector, enabling evidence-based mitigation targets aligned with Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121260).
Cuba.adaptation: • **CCAFS** contributed to research on **adaptations to climate change in agriculture**, though no direct Cuba-specific interventions were identified in this sample (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
Cuba.mitigation: • **CCAFS** enabled **Cuba** to enhance **MRV systems for agricultural GHG emissions**, supporting transparent tracking of mitigation targets (e.g., livestock sector emissions) and alignment with NDCs (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121260).

Antarctica.narrative:
CGIAR, in collaboration with the Columbia University Center for International Earth Science Information Network (CIESIN), contributed to global population modeling through geospatial data tools—**without direct evidence of research in Antarctica**—to support data integration for climate-relevant applications. Specifically:
- **CIAT** developed the **Gridded Population of the World, Version 3 (GPWv3) Population Density Grid**, a raster-based dataset for 2005–2015, using proportional allocation algorithms and UN-adjusted growth rates (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/XICYTK).
- **CIAT** produced the **GPWv3 Population Count Grid**, providing cell-level population estimates (2005–2015) via subnational growth projections and GIS-compatible formats (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/YS1DJO).

Puerto Rico.narrative: CGIAR **did not directly conduct research in Puerto Rico** in this sample. Specifically:
Puerto Rico.adaptation: • CGIAR **CIAT (International Center for Tropical Agriculture)** developed a **geospatial database of livestock producers** in Colombia’s Caquetá region (including Puerto Rico as a sub-area) to track adoption of **sustainable livestock technologies**, regional sustainability, and conservation efforts, supporting project planning and monitoring (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/CHECU8).
