French Guiana.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate policy research in Latin America, including French Guiana’s rural sectors**, through thematic analysis of policy gaps and sectoral focus. Specifically:
- The Center analyzed **policy barriers and research gaps** in forest-based mitigation and agricultural adaptation, proposing a framework for multi-level governance (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
French Guiana.adaptation: • CGIAR examined **adaptations to climate change in agriculture** within Latin America’s rural contexts, identifying sector-specific vulnerabilities and research gaps (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
French Guiana.mitigation: • CGIAR investigated **mitigation of greenhouse gas emissions from forests** as a key focus area in Latin American climate policy research, highlighting gaps in cross-sectoral approaches (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).

Suriname.narrative:
CGIAR’s research in **Suriname** (Latin America) focused on climate policy gaps and sector-specific interventions in rural development. Specifically:
- The Center analyzed **policy barriers** in climate change mitigation and adaptation, emphasizing **forest-based GHG mitigation** and **agricultural adaptation** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
Suriname.adaptation: • CGIAR explored **adaptations to climate change in agriculture**, identifying sector-specific vulnerabilities and research gaps (e.g., multi-level governance) to inform policy design (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).
Suriname.mitigation: • CGIAR investigated **mitigation of greenhouse gas emissions from forests**, highlighting the need for cross-sectoral policy integration in rural development (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831](https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831)).

Turkmenistan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate vulnerability and adaptation in Turkmenistan’s rangelands** through **global rangeland modeling and socio-economic risk assessments**. Specifically:
- The Center analyzed **climatic risks to livestock production**, projecting **declines in mean biomass (−4.7% by 2050)** and **increased inter-annual (+21.3%) and intra-annual (+8.2%) variability** under RCP 8.5, with Turkmenistan’s rangelands facing heightened vulnerability due to **low livestock productivity and economic development** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395)).
Turkmenistan.adaptation: • CGIAR **assessed adaptation needs for pastoral systems** by quantifying **vegetation productivity trends** (biomass decline, variability increases) and their **harmful impacts on livestock grazing availability**, identifying Turkmenistan’s rangelands as a high-risk zone for **climate-induced livelihood disruptions** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395)).
Turkmenistan.water: • CGIAR **linked water-vegetation productivity dynamics** to **climatic risk**, highlighting **increased intra-annual variability in rangeland water/vegetation systems** as a critical factor for Turkmenistan’s pastoral resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395)).

American Samoa.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptation in Pacific Island food systems, including American Samoa**, through scenario-based policy insights. Specifically:
- The Center developed **four alternative future scenarios for Pacific food systems**—focusing on fisheries, forests, trade, affordability, and public health—to inform climate adaptation strategies for fishers and farmers (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75610**).
American Samoa.adaptation: • CGIAR developed **four alternative scenarios for Pacific food systems** to enhance resilience and adaptation among fishers and farmers, addressing climate vulnerabilities such as tropical cyclones and droughts (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75610).

Cook Islands.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptation in Pacific Island food systems, including the Cook Islands**, through scenario-based policy insights. Specifically:
- The Center developed **four alternative future scenarios for Pacific food systems**—focusing on fisheries, forests, trade, affordability, and public health—to inform climate adaptation strategies for fishers and farmers (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75610**).
Cook Islands.adaptation: • CGIAR developed **four alternative scenarios for Pacific food systems** to enhance resilience and adaptation among fishers and farmers, addressing climate vulnerabilities such as droughts and tropical cyclones (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75610).

Kiribati.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptation in Pacific Island food systems, including Kiribati**, through scenario-based policy insights. Specifically:
- The Center developed **four alternative future scenarios for Pacific food systems**—focusing on fisheries, trade, affordability, and public health—to inform climate adaptation strategies for fishers and farmers (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75610**).
Kiribati.adaptation: • CGIAR developed **four alternative scenarios for Pacific food systems** to enhance resilience against climate change impacts (e.g., tropical cyclones, droughts) among fishers and farmers, providing policy-makers with tools to strengthen adaptation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75610).

Samoa.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptation in Pacific Island food systems, including Samoa**, through scenario-based policy insights. Specifically:
- The Center developed **four alternative future scenarios** for Pacific food systems—focusing on fisheries, forests, trade, affordability, and public health—to inform climate adaptation strategies for fishers and farmers (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75610**).
Samoa.adaptation: • CGIAR developed **four alternative scenarios** for Pacific food systems (including Samoa) to enhance resilience against climate change impacts such as tropical cyclones and droughts, providing policy-makers with tools to strengthen adaptation among fishers and farmers (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75610).

Tokelau.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptation in Pacific Island food systems, including Tokelau**, through scenario-based policy insights. Specifically:
- The Center developed **four alternative future scenarios for Pacific food systems**—focusing on fisheries, forests, trade, affordability, and public health—to inform climate adaptation strategies for fishers and farmers (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75610**).
Tokelau.adaptation: • CGIAR developed **four alternative scenarios for Pacific food systems** to enhance resilience against climate change impacts (e.g., tropical cyclones, droughts) among fishers and farmers, providing policy-makers with tools to strengthen adaptation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75610).

Tonga.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptation in Pacific Island food systems, including Tonga**, through scenario-based policy insights. Specifically:
- The Center developed **four alternative future scenarios for Pacific food systems**—focusing on fisheries, forests, trade, affordability, and public health—to inform climate adaptation strategies for fishers and farmers (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75610).
Tonga.adaptation: • CGIAR developed **four alternative scenarios for Pacific food systems** to enhance resilience and adaptation among fishers and farmers, addressing climate vulnerability to droughts and tropical cyclones (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75610).

Slovakia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural adaptation** in **Slovakia** through **modeling-based policy analysis and technology innovation**. Specifically:
- CGIAR researchers used the **IMPACT model** to assess climate change impacts on yields and food security, proposing **new crop varieties and irrigation technology upgrades** as key adaptation strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).
Slovakia.adaptation: • CGIAR developed **two adaptation strategies**—**climate-resilient crop varieties** and **improved irrigation technologies**—to enhance agricultural resilience under climate change, with estimates of required R&D and infrastructure investments (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en](https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en)).

Ukraine.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **gender-inclusive climate adaptation in agriculture** in **Ukraine’s food systems** through research on resilient farming practices. Specifically:
- The Center analyzed gaps in climate adaptation solutions for **women farmers**, highlighting the need for context-specific interventions to ensure equitable resilience (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/127103**).
Ukraine.adaptation: • CGIAR researchers examined **gender disparities in climate adaptation strategies**, revealing critical knowledge gaps about whether existing solutions effectively support women farmers amid climate shocks like floods and rising temperatures (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/127103).

Oman.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **agricultural R&D infrastructure analysis** in **Oman** through **data-driven policy support**. Specifically:
- The Center compiled and standardized national-level data on **financial, human, and institutional resources** for agricultural research and development (R&D) to enable evidence-based policymaking (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/IR12XU).

Maldives.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural adaptation** in **Asia (including rice-based systems)** through **trans-disciplinary networks and climate-resilient crop practices**. Specifically:
- CGIAR facilitated the **Direct Seeded Rice Consortium (DSRC)** to enhance resilience to extreme weather events and variable rainfall in rice-based systems (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035)).
Maldives.adaptation: • CGIAR fostered **trans-disciplinary networks** (e.g., the **Direct Seeded Rice Consortium**) to strengthen climate-smart agriculture and resilience to extreme weather events in rice-based systems, addressing reduced crop yields and food security risks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035](https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035)).

Northern Mariana Islands.narrative: CGIAR **did not conduct climate-related research** in the Northern Mariana Islands based on the provided sample. The sole available record pertains to **economic data collection** rather than climate science or agricultural research.

Guam.narrative: CGIAR **did not conduct climate-related research in Guam** as per the provided sample. The sole available record pertains to **economic data collection** rather than climate science or agricultural interventions.

Dominica.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **pest management and crop protection** in **Dominica** through **integrated pest management (IPM) research and training**. Specifically:
- CGIAR conducted an **experimental study on *Rhizopertha dominica* (date palm pest) under IPM frameworks**, contributing to storage pest management strategies (hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/XMWCWQ).

general / cross-cutting.narrative:
CGIAR’s cross-cutting research advanced climate resilience and sustainable development through integrated, multi-disciplinary approaches. Specifically:
- **CGIAR** synthesized global climate adaptation and mitigation strategies, emphasizing ecosystem-health linkages and equitable climate action (Source: [https://doi.org/10.59327/ipcc/ar6-9789291691647.001](https://doi.org/10.59327/ipcc/ar6-9789291691647.001)).
- **CGIAR** quantified food-system GHG emissions (9,800–16,900 MtCO₂e) and proposed mitigation pathways, including agricultural intensification and waste reduction (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-environ-020411-130608](https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-environ-020411-130608)).
general / cross-cutting.adaptation:
• **CGIAR** integrated biophysical and carbon-centric metrics to evaluate deforestation impacts, revealing that tropical deforestation exacerbates global warming while temperate deforestation may induce local cooling via albedo effects, informing climate-adaptive forest management (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/ffgc.2022.756115](https://doi.org/10.3389/ffgc.2022.756115) ).

• **CGIAR** assessed climate change impacts on global agricultural yields (2050 projections) under socioeconomic and emissions scenarios, highlighting regional yield vulnerabilities and the role of endogenous price adjustments in mitigating losses (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/10/8/085010](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/10/8/085010) ).

• **CGIAR** developed stakeholder-driven adaptation portfolios for farmers, prioritizing actionable solutions to address food security threats while centering vulnerable communities (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gfs.2016.06.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gfs.2016.06.002) ).

• **CGIAR** evaluated adaptation measures in South Asian smallholder systems, emphasizing institutional reforms and dynamic policies to avoid maladaptation and strengthen resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10668-019-00414-4](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10668-019-00414-4) ).

• **CGIAR** quantified climate-change costs to agriculture and modeled investments needed to offset food-security risks, particularly for developing-world populations (Source: [https://doi.org/10.2499/0896295354](https://doi.org/10.2499/0896295354) ).

• **CGIAR** analyzed climate variability and extremes, linking increased variability to food insecurity and advocating for adaptive research frameworks to address pest-weed-disease interactions in crops (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12581](https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12581) ).
general / cross-cutting.mitigation:
• **CGIAR** contributed to IPCC’s synthesis of climate mitigation-adaptation synergies, underscoring the role of ecosystems in sustainable development and human well-being (Source: [https://doi.org/10.59327/ipcc/ar6-9789291691647.001](https://doi.org/10.59327/ipcc/ar6-9789291691647.001) ).

• **CGIAR** analyzed forest biophysical climate forcing, demonstrating that deforestation’s net impact depends on latitude (e.g., tropical warming vs. temperate cooling) and advocating for metrics beyond carbon stocks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3389/ffgc.2022.756115](https://doi.org/10.3389/ffgc.2022.756115) ).

• **CGIAR** quantified food-system GHG emissions (19–29% of global anthropogenic emissions) and proposed mitigation strategies like agricultural intensification and waste reduction (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-environ-020411-130608](https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-environ-020411-130608) ).
general / cross-cutting.water:
• **CGIAR** modeled California’s water supply adaptation, demonstrating the system’s capacity to adjust via groundwater storage, inter-user transfers, and technology adoption under climate and demographic changes (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-006-9079-5](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-006-9079-5) ).

• **CGIAR** developed high-resolution, bias-corrected climate projections for agriculture, enabling fine-scale impact assessments of precipitation and temperature changes (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-019-0343-8](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-019-0343-8) ).

rice.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers advanced climate-resilient rice systems through targeted innovations in adaptation, mitigation, and water management. Specifically:
- **Adaptation:** CGIAR developed climate-smart practices and stress-tolerant varieties to enhance rice productivity under extreme conditions.
- **Mitigation:** The Center quantified GHG reduction strategies in rice cultivation, integrating water-saving and fertilizer-efficient technologies.
- **Water:** CGIAR optimized irrigation regimes and cropping intensity to balance productivity and resource use.
rice.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed climate change impacts on rice, projecting increased vulnerability to heatwaves (e.g., 27–46% chance of damaging hot spells at 4°C) and drought, while highlighting regional variations in adaptive capacity (e.g., Mekong Delta’s exposure to flooding and sea-level rise) (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-019-02464-z).

• Researchers developed rice varieties adapted to climate stress, demonstrating that transpirational cooling—not just temperature—affects grain quality (e.g., chalkiness reduction in dry seasons) (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0213641).

• CGIAR’s Climate Risk Vulnerability Assessment prioritized sub-national hotspots (e.g., Vietnam’s Mekong Delta) for National Adaptation Plans (NAPs), integrating ecological models and participatory approaches (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0066218).

• The Center promoted climate-smart agriculture (CSA) technologies (e.g., direct seeded rice, System of Rice Intensification, laser land leveling) to reduce women farmers’ drudgery in high-risk areas like Nepal (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-018-2350-8).

• CGIAR analyzed conservation agriculture (CA) in the Indo-Gangetic Plains, showing it moderates heat stress (reducing canopy temperature by 1–4°C) and boosts water productivity by 66–100% (https://doi.org/10.1016/s2095-3119(15)61093-0).

• Researchers examined determinants of CSA adoption in Vietnam, linking climate-smart practices (e.g., water-saving techniques) to increased net rice income when combined (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2019-0003).

• CGIAR developed a multi-scale forecasting toolbox (CRAFT) for regional rice yield prediction, integrating crop models (DSSAT, APSIM) and climate data to support risk analysis (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envsoft.2019.02.006).
rice.mitigation:
• CGIAR demonstrated that alternate wetting and drying (AWD) irrigation reduces methane (CH₄) emissions by 28% and global warming potential by 27% compared to continuous flooding, without yield loss (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jenvman.2022.114520).

• Researchers found conservation agriculture (CA) in rice-wheat systems cuts GHG emissions by 10–15% vs. conventional methods (https://doi.org/10.1016/s2095-3119(15)61093-0).
rice.water:
• CGIAR quantified global multiple cropping systems, identifying 85 million hectares of irrigated rice under intensified regimes (e.g., 34% of rice area) and potential for further expansion (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2020.102131).

• The Center evaluated water-saving irrigation (AWD) as a mitigation strategy, reducing CH₄ emissions while improving nitrogen use efficiency (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jenvman.2022.114520).

• CGIAR’s CSA research in Vietnam highlighted water-saving techniques as key to adoption, linking them to increased net rice income (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2019-0003).

wheat.narrative:
CGIAR’s wheat research prioritized **climate-resilient adaptation** and **agroclimatic vulnerability assessments** across South Asia, East Africa, and Mongolia. Specifically:
- **ICRISAT/CIMMYT** quantified climate change impacts on wheat yield in India, demonstrating adaptation potential through optimized sowing times and input efficiency (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3354/cr01212](https://doi.org/10.3354/cr01212)).
- **ICARDA** analyzed agroclimatic suitability for wheat in Ethiopia’s Upper Blue Nile Basin, identifying thermal/moisture stress risks and adaptive land-use strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/agriculture15050525](https://doi.org/10.3390/agriculture15050525)).
wheat.adaptation:
• **CIMMYT/ICARDA** demonstrated that **conservation agriculture-based wheat systems (CAW)** serve as ex-ante climate risk mitigation in Haryana, India, with farmers increasing adoption post-extreme rainfall events (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-09-2018-0065](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-09-2018-0065) and [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/4ICF6W](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/4ICF6W) for decentralized breeding trials).

• **ICARDA** highlighted **pathogen range shifts** under climate change, emphasizing disease forecasting models for Fusarium head blight and other wheat diseases (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/b978-0-444-63524-2.00021-x](https://doi.org/10.1016/b978-0-444-63524-2.00021-x)).

• **CIAT/IFPRI** assessed **COVID-19 lockdown impacts** on wheat farmers in Haryana, documenting disruptions to harvesting, borrowing, and food security as adaptive risk coping mechanisms (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PAWM4U](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PAWM4U)).

• **ICARDA** advanced **decentralized breeding** in Ethiopia’s marginal environments, increasing genetic gain through farmer-led tricot trials (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/OEZGVP](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/OEZGVP)).

• **CGIAR** analyzed **climate extremes** in Mongolia’s wheat/potato value chains, proposing government-private sector interventions to enhance resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodpol.2023.102468](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodpol.2023.102468)).
wheat.water:
• **CIMMYT/ICARDA** identified **water stress** as the primary driver of wheat crop failures in Northeastern China and West Africa, advocating for **irrigation and drought-tolerant breeding** as critical adaptation strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/10/8/084003](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/10/8/084003)).

• **ICARDA** projected **spatial precipitation variability** in Ethiopia’s Upper Blue Nile Basin, warning of moisture availability challenges for wheat suitability (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/agriculture15050525](https://doi.org/10.3390/agriculture15050525)).

• **CIMMYT** modeled **irrigation systems** as key adaptation tools to offset yield losses in India’s late-sown wheat regions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3354/cr01212](https://doi.org/10.3354/cr01212)).

livestock (general/beef).narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient livestock systems** through **adaptation strategies, mitigation policies, and vulnerability assessments** across global rangelands and smallholder/pastoralist communities. Specifically:
- **ILRI and CIAT** analyzed **household-level adaptation barriers** (e.g., education, credit access, livestock ownership) in Ethiopia’s Nile Basin (Source_1).
- **Global Rangeland Modeling Teams** quantified **productivity declines (7.5–9.6% livestock loss) and regional variability** under RCP 8.5, highlighting Africa’s vulnerability (Source_5).
- **Economic Modelers** projected **livestock system transitions** could reduce emissions by **736 MtCO₂e/year** via land-use efficiency (Source_4).
livestock (general/beef).adaptation:
• **CGIAR-ILRI** used the **Heckman sample selection model** to link **farmers’ climate perception** (age, wealth, social capital) to adaptation actions in Ethiopia, emphasizing **extension services and credit access** as critical enablers (Source_1).

• **Global Food Security Team** reviewed **climate risks across livestock supply chains**, identifying **hot, resource-limited regions** as high-priority adaptation zones, with **uncertainty in climate futures** requiring flexible strategies (Source_2).

• **ILRI’s Pastoralism Research** highlighted **spatial heterogeneity in smallholder responses** to climate variability, noting **vulnerability gaps** in tropical/subtropical systems and proposing **context-specific adaptation frameworks** (Source_3).

• **Rangeland Ecosystem Modelers** projected **10% global NPP decline by 2050** (RCP 8.5), with **Africa and Australia facing severe losses**, and estimated **$9.7–12.6 billion in economic impacts** on livestock-dependent livelihoods (Source_5).

• **Climate Risk Analysts** mapped **global rangeland biomass declines (−4.7%) and increased variability (+21.3% inter-annual, +8.2% intra-annual)**, targeting **pastoralist communities** with lowest productivity and highest population growth as adaptation priorities (Source_6).

• **ILRI’s Genetic Resources Team** advanced **genomics for climate-adapted livestock**, framing **diversity maintenance** and **genetic modifications** as essential for resilience in global food systems (Source_7).

• **Borana Zone Study (Ethiopia)** documented **moisture stress from delayed rains and reduced rainfall days**, linking **climate variability to livestock disease outbreaks** and **pastoralist adaptation strategies** (Source_8).

• **Rwenzori Region Case Study (Uganda)** evaluated **indigenous vs. scientific forecasts** in shaping **pastoralist adaptation methods**, underscoring **local knowledge integration** (Source_9).
livestock (general/beef).mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s Economic Modeling Group** demonstrated that **livestock system transitions** (e.g., avoided land conversion) could cut emissions by **736 MtCO₂e/year**, with **land-use change policies** offering **5–10x greater efficiency** than livestock-only targets (Source_4).

• **ILRI’s Climate-Livestock Nexus Team** framed **adaptation and mitigation** as interdependent, stressing **carbon sequestration potential** in rangelands and the need for **context-specific policy frameworks** to balance **productivity, poverty alleviation, and emissions reduction** (Source_6).
livestock (general/beef).water:
• **CGIAR’s Rangeland-Climate Team** identified **increased intra- and inter-annual vegetation productivity variability** as a **water-related risk**, with **54% of global rangelands** facing **higher intra-annual moisture stress** by 2050 (Source_6).

• **Borana Zone Research (Ethiopia)** linked **drought and late-onset rains** to **livestock moisture stress**, aggravating **disease prevalence** and **production distortions** (Source_8).

maize.narrative:
CGIAR’s maize research prioritized **climate-resilient adaptation** and **stress-tolerant germplasm** across sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and Latin America through **breeding innovations, agronomic interventions, and socio-ecological resilience strategies**. Specifically:
- **CIMMYT** and **CIAT** advanced **drought- and heat-tolerant maize varieties** to offset yield losses under climate change (Sources: [Adaptation]).
- **Bioversity** and **local partners** integrated **conservation of indigenous maize varieties** and **microwatershed management** to enhance smallholder resilience in conflict-affected regions (Source: [Adaptation]).
maize.adaptation:
• **CIMMYT** quantified the potential of **drought- and heat-tolerant maize varieties** to reduce yield losses by **21–50%** under 1–4°C warming in Eastern/Southern Africa and South Asia, using crop models (e.g., CERES-Maize) to target site-specific adaptation (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2017.10.001).

• **CIAT** assessed **cultivar adaptation potential** for maize, millet, sorghum, and wheat in **10 sub-Saharan African countries**, finding that **better-adapted cultivars** could compensate for **6% yield declines in East/Southern Africa by 2050** but highlighted **remaining adaptation gaps** in West Africa (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137).

• **CIMMYT/CIAT** reviewed **stress-tolerance breeding pipelines**, emphasizing **molecular tools** to accelerate development of **climate-ready germplasm** for sub-Saharan Africa, where yield losses are projected to worsen (https://doi.org/10.1016/b978-0-12-394275-3.00006-7).

• **CIMMYT** evaluated **heat-tolerant maize varieties** in South Asia, demonstrating **33–93% yield loss reductions** under future climate scenarios (2030–2050) for both rainfed and irrigated systems (https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6).

• **CIAT** analyzed **farmers’ perceptions vs. meteorological data** in Nigeria, confirming **later rainfall onset and reduced early-growing-season precipitation**, which aligns with smallholders’ observations and underscores **vulnerability to climate change** (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.wace.2016.12.001).

• **CIMMYT** modeled **maize yield responses to farm management** (planting date, fertilizer use, cultivar choice) in Zimbabwe, finding **nonlinear yield declines** (up to **32% by 2090**) under high radiative forcing, necessitating **transformative cropping system changes** (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13061).

• **World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF)/CIMMYT** demonstrated **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) technologies** (e.g., integrated soil fertility management) in Ethiopia, increasing **GDP by 0.18%** and reducing poverty by **0.15 percentage points** when adopted on **25% of maize/wheat land** (https://doi.org/10.1111/agec.12523).

• **Bioversity/CGIAR** documented **community-level climate adaptation** in Guatemala, showcasing **microwatershed management** and **conservation of local maize varieties** to build **social-ecological resilience** in post-conflict, marginalized regions (https://doi.org/10.5751/es-10250-230305).

• **AgMIP (led by CGIAR centers)** coordinated **global/regional assessments** for **1.5°C/2.0°C scenarios**, revealing **mixed maize yield changes** (declines in breadbasket regions) and informing **risk management strategies** (https://doi.org/10.1098/rsta.2016.0455).
maize.mitigation: • **CIAT/CIMMYT** highlighted **sustainable agronomic practices** (e.g., resource-efficient management) as **low-carbon agriculture strategies** to mitigate climate change impacts while enhancing maize system resilience (https://doi.org/10.1016/b978-0-12-394275-3.00006-7).

roots and tubers.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers prioritized climate-resilient roots and tubers through targeted breeding, agronomic interventions, and socio-economic adaptation strategies. Specifically:
- **CIP (International Potato Center)** developed climate-smart potato varieties with heat, drought, and disease tolerance for extreme climates (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106939).
- **CIAT (International Center for Tropical Agriculture)** advanced cassava and sweetpotato breeding for semi-arid zones, integrating pest/disease-specific traits and biofortification (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/L1TFKE, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/RN40SP).
roots and tubers.adaptation:
• **CIP** analyzed state-level climate projections to align potato variety adoption with abiotic/biotic stress resilience, identifying mismatches in Gujarat and other regions (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2019.01.001).
• **CIP** evaluated global potato late blight risk under climate change, finding regional variability (increases in Rwanda’s Lake Kivu Highlands, decreases in Indonesia’s Southeast Asian Highlands) due to shifting planting dates (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12587).
• **CIP** enhanced rain-fed potato resilience in Kenya via legume intercropping and silicon application, improving water/light use efficiency and reducing soil erosion (https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.566345).
• **CIP** documented Ugandan sweetpotato farmers’ coping strategies (e.g., drought-tolerant varieties, drainage channels) in six agro-ecological zones, linking adaptation to gender and land ownership (https://doi.org/10.5539/jas.v5n8p252).
• **CIAT** developed cassava varieties for semi-arid zones through recurrent selection, prioritizing pest/disease-specific traits (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/L1TFKE).
• **CIAT/HarvestPlus** disseminated biofortified orange-fleshed sweetpotato (OFSP) in Mozambique/Uganda via agricultural extension and nutrition training, evaluating adoption impacts through randomized intervention arms (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/RN40SP).
• **CIAT** assessed cassava varietal adoption in Vietnam using DNA fingerprinting to track productivity impacts (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/4HJJXZ).
• **CIP** conducted climate risk assessments for Tanzanian potatoes, identifying seed quality and marketing gaps as key constraints (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107442).
• **CIP** proposed an analytical framework for climate-disease interactions (e.g., *Phytophthora infestans*), guiding model complexity for potato late blight management (https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-3059.2010.02409.x).
roots and tubers.water: • **CIP** demonstrated legume intercropping and silicon amendments in Kenya to improve potato water productivity (5.99–9.09 kg/ha/m³) and reduce soil erosion by 45–80% (https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.566345).

agroforestry.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers advanced agroforestry as a climate-resilient and carbon-positive agricultural strategy. Specifically:
- **CIAT** quantified global carbon sequestration potential of agroforestry systems, revealing their critical role in national and global carbon budgets (Source: [10.1038/srep29987](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987)).
- **World Agroforestry Centre (ICRAF)** evaluated regional carbon benefits of silvopastoral and improved fallow systems, identifying tropical climates as optimal for soil and above-ground carbon accumulation (Source: [10.1016/j.agee.2017.11.032](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2017.11.032)).
agroforestry.adaptation:
• **ICRAF** demonstrated agroforestry’s role in climate-smart agriculture (CSA) for West Africa, highlighting farmer-managed natural regenerations and soil/water conservation technologies (e.g., zai, contour ridges) as key adaptation tools (Source: [10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199)).

• **CGIAR’s Climate Change Program** emphasized ecosystem-based adaptation (EBA) via agroforestry, showing how trees regulate water, soil, and microclimates to enhance resilience in agricultural and coastal systems (Source: [10.1002/wcc.195](https://doi.org/10.1002/wcc.195)).

• **ICRAF** analyzed agroforestry’s potential to reduce smallholder vulnerability to climate risks, though adoption challenges persist (Source: [10.1016/j.cosust.2013.11.013](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cosust.2013.11.013)).

• **Bioversity International** explored genetic adaptation strategies for tree species under climate change, stressing conservation of evolutionary potential for long-term resilience (Source: [10.1016/j.foreco.2014.04.006](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foreco.2014.04.006)).

• **CGIAR’s Tropical Forests Program** advocated for climate-smart reforestation, integrating mitigation and adaptation goals to maximize synergies (Source: [10.1111/rec.12209](https://doi.org/10.1111/rec.12209)).
agroforestry.mitigation:
• **ICRAF** assessed global tree cover on agricultural land, estimating 45.3 PgC stored in biomass (75%+ from trees) and a 4.6% increase in biomass carbon (2000–2010) (Source: [10.1038/srep29987](https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987)).

• **CIAT** identified silvopastoral systems as the most effective for soil carbon sequestration (4.38 tC/ha/year) and improved fallows for above-ground carbon (11.29 tC/ha/year), with tropical climates yielding higher benefits (Source: [10.1016/j.agee.2017.11.032](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2017.11.032)).

• **ICRAF** linked agroforestry to UNFCCC NAMAs, revealing gaps in measurement/reporting of agroforestry carbon stocks in national communications, despite 40% of countries proposing agroforestry in NDCs (Source: [10.1016/j.agee.2019.106569](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2019.106569)).

• **ICRAF** demonstrated agroforestry’s dual role in carbon sequestration and livelihood improvement in Western Kenya, with firewood self-sufficiency as a primary benefit (Source: [10.1007/s10457-019-00383-7](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10457-019-00383-7)).

legumes.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers—including **CIAT (International Center for Tropical Agriculture)** and **CIP (International Center for Tropical Agriculture, now part of CIAT’s legume portfolio)**—advanced legume resilience and productivity through **genetic innovation, climate-smart farming systems, and gender-inclusive adaptation strategies**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** leveraged wild relatives and genomic tools to introgress climate-resilient traits into common beans, enhancing yield potential under stress (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/agronomy3020433](https://doi.org/10.3390/agronomy3020433)).
- **CIAT and partners** designed weather-indexed insurance models to stabilize farmer incomes by aligning payouts with yield losses, reducing basis risk in legume production (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cpb.2020.100149](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cpb.2020.100149)).
legumes.adaptation:
• **CIAT** developed **genomic and physiological frameworks** to improve legume adaptation to elevated CO₂ and abiotic/biotic stresses, integrating multi-omics and crop modeling for climate-resilient breeding (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cpb.2020.100149](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cpb.2020.100149) and [https://doi.org/10.7910/DVN/JR4X4C](https://doi.org/10.7910/DVN/JR4X4C) for drought-tolerant germplasm mapping).

• **CIAT and PABRA (Pan Africa Bean Research Alliance)** released **513 disease- and stress-tolerant common bean varieties** across 17 Sub-Saharan African countries, addressing fungal, bacterial, viral, and abiotic constraints (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/RPATZA](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/RPATZA)).

• **CIAT** used **spatial modeling (DSSAT)** to identify **high-impact climate zones** for drybeans in Central America, revealing yield declines (10–38%) and increases (10–41%) under future scenarios, guiding crop diversification strategies (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-015-9696-2](https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-015-9696-2) and [https://doi.org/10.7910/dvn/heqqih](https://doi.org/10.7910/dvn/heqqih)).

• **CIAT** improved **weather-index insurance contracts** in India, increasing farmer satisfaction by 50–72% for soybean and pearl millet through optimized triggers and reduced basis risk (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2019.100189](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2019.100189)).

• **CIAT** assessed **gender-differentiated adaptation strategies** in Mali, revealing women’s lower adoption of soil/water conservation techniques due to resource constraints, recommending policy reforms to enhance their access to land/labor (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10668-021-01242-1](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10668-021-01242-1)).

• **CIAT** incorporated **soil organic carbon (SOC) constraints** into crop suitability models for Tanzania, showing SOC currently limits common bean production more than climate but projecting climate as the dominant constraint by 2030 (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/02571862.2017.1281447](https://doi.org/10.1080/02571862.2017.1281447)).

• **CIAT** promoted **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) in Uganda**, estimating CSA could reduce soybean yield losses (worth **$1.5B by 2050**) while improving resilience and incomes (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103239](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103239)).

coffee and cocoa.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers—including CIP (International Potato Center) and CIAT (International Center for Tropical Agriculture)—led groundbreaking studies on climate resilience in coffee and cocoa systems. Specifically:
- CGIAR’s climate vulnerability assessments mapped shifting suitability zones for Arabica coffee and cocoa under climate change, identifying critical adaptation needs (Source A).
- The Center quantified yield declines in Tanzanian coffee due to rising nighttime temperatures, providing evidence for urgent adaptation strategies (Source E).
coffee and cocoa.adaptation:
• CGIAR projected a **50% global reduction in coffee suitability** due to higher temperatures and intra-seasonal variability, with Robusta and Arabica facing distinct threats (Source A).
• Researchers at CGIAR identified **shade trees as essential adaptation strategies** for West African cocoa, countering deforestation trends and high-temperature stress (Source B).
• A **two-dimensional adaptation framework** was developed for coffee in Nicaragua, distinguishing incremental (short-term) and transformative (long-term) strategies across altitudes (Source C).
• CGIAR’s global modeling revealed **Arabica suitability losses in Mesoamerica and Vietnam**, while Ethiopia and Kenya may gain suitability, necessitating regional production shifts (Source D).
• Evidence from Tanzania demonstrated **nighttime temperature increases reducing yields by 137 kg/ha per °C**, with projections of 80% yield loss by 2060 without adaptation (Source E).
• The Center highlighted **elevated CO₂ as a mitigating factor** for coffee’s supra-optimal temperature stress, suggesting lower climate sensitivity than previously assumed (Source F).
• CGIAR’s **vulnerability framework for Mesoamerican coffee growers** integrated exposure, sensitivity, and adaptive capacity, revealing El Salvador’s highest suitability loss and the need for flexible, localized strategies (Source G).
• An **innovation perspective** was proposed to scale adaptation uptake via certification programs and landscape approaches, addressing barriers like knowledge access and resource constraints (Source H).
• Agro-ecological zoning was used to **identify hot/dry zones for Arabica expansion** and predict 500m upward migration of suitability zones, guiding long-term adaptation research (Source I).
• CGIAR’s **Peruvian cacao study** mapped climate-tolerant genotypes and predicted **wild cacao suitability gains** while cultivated areas contract, targeting collection missions for resilient varieties (Source J).
coffee and cocoa.mitigation: • CGIAR’s research demonstrated that **elevated atmospheric CO₂ concentrations** could mitigate coffee’s temperature stress, reducing projected climate impacts (Source F).

fisheries and aquaculture.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient fisheries and aquaculture systems** in **Pacific Island countries, Sub-Saharan Africa, and South/Southeast Asia** through **adaptation strategies, economic modeling, and livelihood diversification**. Specifically:
- **WorldFish Center** synthesized pathways for climate adaptation in fishery-based livelihoods, identifying asset-based strategies and multi-sectoral policies (Source: [10.1016/j.marpol.2009.08.007](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2009.08.007)).
- **CGIAR economists** modeled economic impacts of **fish aggregating devices (FADs), aquaculture, and marine protected areas (MPAs)** to offset climate-induced declines in fish production in **Solomon Islands, Fiji, Vanuatu, and Timor-Leste** (Sources: [10.1016/j.marpol.2016.01.004](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2016.01.004), [10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.023](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.023), [10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.010](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.011)).
fisheries and aquaculture.adaptation:
• **WorldFish Center** developed a **livelihoods framework** to assess climate impacts on fisherfolk, proposing **anticipatory adaptation strategies** (e.g., asset diversification, indigenous knowledge integration) to reduce vulnerability in **global fisheries** (Source: [10.1016/j.marpol.2009.08.007](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2009.08.007) and [10.1002/aff2.182](https://doi.org/10.1002/aff2.182) for SSA focus).

• **CGIAR’s Pacific team** evaluated **FADs, aquaculture expansion, and MPAs** as adaptation tools, projecting **20–50% increases in coastal fish production** by 2050 in **Solomon Islands, Fiji, Vanuatu, and Timor-Leste** (Sources: [10.1016/j.marpol.2016.01.004](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2016.01.004), [10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.023](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.023), [10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.010](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.011)).

• **WorldFish and CIAT** proposed **fuel subsidy reforms** to curb overfishing in **Senegal’s artisanal fisheries**, demonstrating how policy interventions could **prevent fishery collapse by 2035** (Source: [10.1371/journal.pone.0220433](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0220433)).

• **CGIAR’s Pacific Food Systems team** designed **resilience scenarios** for fishers and farmers, addressing **tropical cyclones and droughts** in **Pacific Island countries** (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75610](https://doi.org/10.7910/DVN/https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75610)).

• **Bangladesh-based researchers** documented **post-flood coping strategies** in rural fisheries, linking **shock recovery** to **livelihood diversification** (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/UJA9N5](https://doi.org/10.7910/DVN/UJA9N5)).
fisheries and aquaculture.mitigation: • **WorldFish Center** analyzed **fisheries’ mitigation potential**, highlighting **fuel subsidy reductions** as a **low-cost, high-impact intervention** to improve ecological sustainability in **Senegal** (Source: [10.1371/journal.pone.0220433](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0220433)).

livestock (general/small ruminants).narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers advanced climate-resilient livestock systems through targeted adaptation, mitigation, and socio-economic interventions. Specifically:
- **Adaptation:** CGIAR assessed heat stress risks in livestock (e.g., cattle, sheep, goats) and evaluated climate-smart practices for smallholder farmers (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.15825**, **https://doi.org/10.3390/cli5030063**).
- **Mitigation:** The Center quantified GHG reductions via pasture intensification and feeding modifications, while exploring synergies between adaptation and mitigation in mixed crop-livestock systems (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2017.06.130**, **https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-61194-5_17**).
livestock (general/small ruminants).adaptation:
• CGIAR projected **increased extreme heat stress days** for livestock (cattle, sheep, goats) globally, highlighting risks to outdoor production systems and adaptation challenges in lower-income regions (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.15825).

• The Center evaluated **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) adoption pathways** in Tanzania, documenting farmer uptake of improved crop varieties, agroforestry, and weather forecasts via the *Farms of the Future Approach* (FotF) (https://doi.org/10.3390/cli5030063).

• CGIAR analyzed **drought impacts on rangelands** in Australia, showing herd recovery times of years-to-decades and the need for proactive restocking strategies (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14669).

• Researchers in **Senegal** identified **socio-economic and institutional barriers** (e.g., credit access, extension services) to CSA adoption among smallholders, linking these to reduced grain/fodder yields (https://doi.org/10.3389/fclim.2024.1332196).

• CGIAR assessed **climate information services (CIS)** in Kenya/Senegal, though findings were inconclusive on direct links between CIS and CSA uptake (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2018.08.002).

• In **Zimbabwe**, participatory analyses revealed **diversification (crops/cultivars), staggered planting, and soil fertility management** as key adaptation options to stabilize yields against rainfall variability (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2014.05.004).

• The Center qualitatively evaluated **CSA trade-offs in mixed crop-livestock systems**, synthesizing expert knowledge on constraints and policy gaps (https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-61194-5_17).
livestock (general/small ruminants).mitigation:
• CGIAR developed the **LCA4CSA framework**, integrating life cycle assessment (LCA) to evaluate environmental impacts of CSA practices (e.g., compost use, animal feeding) in Colombia’s coffee-livestock systems (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2019.02.001).

• Researchers quantified **GHG reductions** (19–35%) in Brazilian Amazon cattle farms via **pasture intensification and rotational grazing**, with enteric fermentation and manure management as key drivers (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2017.06.130).

• The Center explored **synergies between adaptation and mitigation** in mixed systems, though comprehensive cost-benefit evaluations remain limited (https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-61194-5_17).

dryland cereals (sorghum/millets).narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient dryland cereals (sorghum/millets)** in **arid/semi-arid regions of Africa and Asia** through **genetic improvement, agronomic adaptation strategies, and modeling tools**. Specifically:
- **ICRISAT** developed **drought- and heat-tolerant pearl millet cultivars** to sustain yields under climate change (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2017.06.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2017.06.002)).
- **CIAT** and **ICRISAT** assessed **climate-smart adaptation strategies** (e.g., early sowing, improved varieties) to reduce yield losses in sorghum and millet systems (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2010.04.012](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2010.04.012), [https://doi.org/10.3389/fagro.2024.1428630](https://doi.org/10.3389/fagro.2024.1428630)).
dryland cereals (sorghum/millets).adaptation:
• **ICRISAT** modeled **drought- and heat-tolerant pearl millet traits** (e.g., maturity duration, radiation use efficiency) to enhance yields in **arid/semi-arid tropical climates**, with greater benefits in hotter environments (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2017.06.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2017.06.002)).

• **CIAT** evaluated **low-cost adaptation strategies** (e.g., cultivar shifts, sowing date adjustments) to reduce sorghum yield losses in **India’s central/south-central zones**, mitigating projected declines of **14–32% by 2080** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2010.04.012](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2010.04.012)).

• **ICRISAT** and **CIAT** used **APSIM modeling** to test **management practices (e.g., fertilizer strategies, planting windows)** for sorghum in **West Africa**, identifying **early-maturing varieties and agronomic sowing windows** as key risk-reducing measures under climate change (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fcr.2019.107685](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fcr.2019.107685), [https://doi.org/10.3389/fagro.2024.1428630](https://doi.org/10.3389/fagro.2024.1428630)).

• **ICRISAT** demonstrated **hydro-priming techniques** (e.g., well/river water priming) to improve **sorghum seed germination and seedling vigor** in **Sahelian Mali**, addressing early-season drought risks (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos12040419](https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos12040419)).

• **ICRISAT** assessed **climate risks in Tanzania/Kenya**, highlighting stagnated yields due to **lack of commercial markets** and **low adaptive capacity** in smallholder systems (Sources: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107719](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107719), [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107722](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107722)).

• **ICRISAT** promoted **weather-resilient technologies** (e.g., lodging-resistant varieties) for **pearl millet in India**, addressing challenges like **excess rainfall and poor irrigation** (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/109724](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/109724)).

• **ICRISAT** used **DSSAT-CERES-Millet models** to evaluate **adaptation strategies (e.g., early-maturing CHAKTI variety, optimal sowing windows)** in **Niger’s Sudano-Sahelian region**, projecting **30–72% yield declines** without interventions (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s10113-024-02313-5](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10113-024-02313-5)).

• **ICRISAT** evaluated **marginal cost-benefit of fertility scenarios** (organic/inorganic) for sorghum in **Mali**, integrating **climate model outputs (CMIP5)** to assess productivity under soil moisture constraints (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SGHJ3J](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SGHJ3J)).
dryland cereals (sorghum/millets).water:
• **ICRISAT** explored **hydro-priming with well/river water** to enhance **sorghum seed germination and drought resilience** in **high-climate-variability contexts** (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos12040419](https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos12040419)).

• **ICRISAT** assessed **soil moisture availability** and **fertilizer responses** (organic/inorganic) to improve **sorghum productivity in water-limited Sudanian Mali**, using **climate model outputs** (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SGHJ3J](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SGHJ3J)).

livestock (dairy).narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient and low-emission dairy systems** across Africa and Asia through **forage innovation, mitigation strategies, and policy integration**. Specifically:
- **ILRI and CCAFS** mapped climate-proof forage suitability for Ethiopian dairy systems to ensure feed security under intensification (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-23461-w](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-23461-w)).
- **CCAFS** linked feed and manure management to Kenya’s NDC targets, scaling GHG mitigation via Napier grass adoption and spatially explicit policy tools (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aaac84](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aaac84)).
livestock (dairy).adaptation:
• **ILRI/CCAFS** projected climate impacts on forage grasses (Buffel, Rhodes, Napier) in Ethiopia, identifying regional suitability shifts by 2050 and warning of feed deficits under herd composition changes (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-23461-w](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-23461-w)).

• **CCAFS** conducted socio-economic baselines in Tanzania/Rwanda to assess **Climate-Smart Dairy (CSD) systems**, focusing on improved forages and feeding strategies for resilience (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/9GBKPW](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/9GBKPW)).

• **CCAFS/GSI** integrated gender-inclusive extension and fodder supply into Kenya’s **Dairy NAMA**, leveraging private-sector investment to boost on-farm productivity (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121866](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121866)).
livestock (dairy).mitigation:
• **CCAFS** quantified GHG mitigation potential in Kenya’s dairy sector, demonstrating that **Napier grass adoption and manure management** could align with the country’s 30% emissions reduction target by 2030 (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aaac84](https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aaac84)).

• **CCAFS/ILRI** developed **GHG mitigation guidelines for Chinese dairy farmers**, covering feed/manure management and farm-level carbon reduction strategies (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/119591](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/119591)).

• **ILRI/CCAFS** catalyzed **Kenya’s USD 223M Dairy NAMA**, securing Green Climate Fund approval via CGIAR-backed research on low-emission fodder production (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106306](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106306)).

• **CAAS/CCAFS** assessed **carbon footprints in Chinese dairy systems**, presenting mitigation options to the Sino-Dutch Dairy Development Centre (Sources: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97868](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97868), [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97867](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97867)).

• **CATIE/CCAFS** tested **Tithonia diversifolia (Botón de Oro)** as a forage to reduce **N₂O emissions** from cattle urine in tropical pastures, evaluating nitrogen flow dynamics (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/93425](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/93425)).

• **CCAFS/ILRI** hosted a **USAID webinar** on low-emission dairy investment options in East Africa, featuring fodder-based mitigation pathways (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/102228](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/102228)).

bananas.narrative:
CGIAR’s research on bananas advanced climate-resilient and data-driven agricultural systems. Specifically:
- **Bioversity International** pioneered water-efficient genotype selection through high-precision phenotyping (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/NHVBN5).
- **CGIAR’s Adaptation Hub** identified drought-tolerant triploid cultivars via osmotic stress testing (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Q0ESWS).
- **CGIAR’s Disease Resistance Program** mapped Fusarium wilt resistance mechanisms using transcriptomics (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/W2M6WG).
bananas.adaptation:
• CGIAR’s **Disease Resistance Program** conducted transcriptomic analysis to identify differentially expressed genes (DEGs) in banana varieties resistant/susceptible to *Fusarium oxysporum* f. sp. cubense Tropical Race 4 (TR4), enabling molecular breeding targets (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/W2M6WG).
• CGIAR **Bioversity International** evaluated triploid banana genotypes under osmotic stress (5% PEG treatment) to screen for drought tolerance (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Q0ESWS).
• CGIAR facilitated **climate-smart decision-making** in Colombia’s banana sector by integrating data mining for farmers’ organizations (e.g., Grupo Santa Maria) to improve adaptive practices (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121261).
bananas.water: • CGIAR **Bioversity International** developed lab-based hydroponic phenotyping to quantify water loss patterns and transpiration in banana germplasm, enabling selection of water-efficient genotypes (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/NHVBN5).

forages and pastures.narrative:
CGIAR’s Tropical Forages Program (CIAT) advanced **forages and pastures research** through **germplasm evaluation, agronomic standardization, and climate-resilient pasture systems**. Specifically:
- CIAT standardized **regional agronomic evaluation protocols** for tropical pastures to ensure comparable, ecosystem-specific adaptation data (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DYR7KS).
- The **International Tropical Pastures Evaluation Network (RIEPT)** assessed germplasm adaptation across ecosystems, identifying high-productivity pastures under grazing systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ZB4DYK).
forages and pastures.adaptation:
• **CIAT** published a **manual for agronomic evaluation** of tropical pastures, standardizing methodologies to identify germplasm adapted to distinct ecosystems (e.g., acid soils, seasonal forests) and assess disease/pest resistance (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DYR7KS).

• The **Colombian Brachiaria Network** (CIAT/CORPOICA) selected **superior Brachiaria accessions** with climate-resilient traits, evaluating yield responses to soil/climate variability across 11 Colombian sites (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/NGL4OG).

• **CIAT-Quilichao** characterized **tropical forage germplasm** for adaptation to severe dry seasons, regrowth potential, and disease resistance on infertile Ultisols (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VGOIIQ).

• **RIEPT trials** (CIAT) evaluated **germplasm adaptation** in semievergreen forests, comparing productivity/persistence under grazing vs. traditional systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ZB4DYK).

• **CIAT** analyzed **genotype×environment interactions** in *Desmodium ovalifolium*, quantifying climate/soil effects on tannin (anti-quality) factors across 6 Colombian sites (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/COQEK8).

• **CIAT** assessed **land suitability** for alfalfa in Ethiopia, modeling supplemental irrigation needs under climate change (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123185).

• **CLIFF-GRADS (CIAT)** facilitated **pasture-agronomy research collaborations**, addressing climate change impacts on forage systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110096).
forages and pastures.water: • **CIAT** developed a **simplified land-suitability framework** for alfalfa in Ethiopia, integrating supplemental irrigation needs and climate change projections (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123185).

livestock (small).narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **sustainable small livestock systems** in **Ethiopia, Mali, and Syria** through **feed innovation, climate-smart practices, and methane emission research**. Specifically:
- **ILRI (International Livestock Research Institute)** developed **irrigated fodder systems** to address feed shortages in Ethiopia’s highlands (Source A).
- **Africa RISING (led by ILRI/World Agroforestry Centre)** intensified **mixed crop-livestock feeding systems** in Mali to improve food security (Source B).
- **ICARDA (International Center for Agricultural Research in the Dry Areas)** quantified **enteric methane emissions** from smallholder sheep in Ethiopia to refine climate mitigation strategies (Source C).
livestock (small).adaptation:
• CGIAR’s **Africa RISING project** (ILRI/World Agroforestry Centre) promoted **sustainable intensification of mixed crop-livestock systems** in Mali to enhance food security and livelihoods (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BUMYV0).
• **ILRI researchers** demonstrated **homemade concentrate preparation techniques** for sheep fattening in Ethiopia’s Basona Werena districts to accelerate marketable weight gain (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/168846).
livestock (small).mitigation: • CGIAR’s **ILRI** compared **Tier 1 and Tier 2 methodologies** to estimate enteric methane emissions from smallholder sheep in Ethiopia, deriving seasonal and annual emission factors for climate modeling (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/4K20I6).
livestock (small).water: • **ILRI** piloted **irrigated oat-vetch fodder production** in Ethiopia’s highlands using shallow wells and manual pumps to supplement sheep diets during dry periods (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DQGTMP).

fruits and vegetables.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient fruit and vegetable systems** through **adaptation strategies** and **water-efficient production models**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR Climate** developed **climate suitability projections** for macadamia in Malawi, identifying critical adaptation needs due to shrinking suitable areas under climate change (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0257007](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0257007)).
- **CGIAR’s Horticulture Centers** (e.g., **WorldVeg, CIP**) supported **crop diversification** and **small-scale irrigation** (e.g., microfinance-backed pumps) to sustain vegetable production in Burkina Faso’s dry seasons (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75061](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75061)).
fruits and vegetables.adaptation:
• **CGIAR Climate** quantified **climate suitability decline** for macadamia in Malawi, projecting **18–21% loss** of suitable areas by 2050 and recommending **region-specific adaptation strategies** to bolster producer resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0257007](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0257007)).
• **CGIAR’s Africa Region** documented **crop diversification** (e.g., vegetables for dry seasons) and **water access** (e.g., small pumps) as key adaptation tactics in Burkina Faso, enabling year-round productivity (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75061](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75061)).
fruits and vegetables.water:
• **CGIAR’s Horticulture Centers** analyzed **vegetable productivity** in Ethiopia, linking **modern input adoption** (e.g., irrigation) to enhanced yields (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Q55PL6](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Q55PL6)).
• **CGIAR’s Africa Region** demonstrated **small-scale irrigation solutions** (e.g., microfinance-backed pumps) to secure water for vegetable gardens in Burkina Faso’s dry seasons (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75061](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75061)).

general cereals.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **sustainable intensification of irrigated cereal systems** in **sub-tropical and semi-arid climates** through **precision agronomic innovations**. Specifically:
- CGIAR re-designed **irrigated intensive cereal systems** by bundling precision agronomic innovations to enhance water-use efficiency and sustainability in **North-West India** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123017).
general cereals.adaptation: • No evidence found in this sample.
general cereals.mitigation: • No evidence found in this sample.
general cereals.water: • CGIAR implemented **precision agronomic innovations** in **irrigated cereal systems** to optimize water management in **sub-tropical and semi-arid climates**, improving sustainability in **North-West India** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123017).
